A  project  typically  lasts  for  3-4  years  and  the  EU  funding  is  awarded  for  this  period.  Subject  to 
delivery of required documents at pre-determined deadlines, payments are released in instalments.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

The programmes have no direct impact on trade. As a general rule, further work and considerable 
additional financial efforts of the participants are always necessary for the results of a project to lead 
to a commercially exploitable process or product.

Furthermore, generally access to the results must be shared among participants and a more widely 
dissemination of results is required and supported, which serves the public interest. One of the main 
benefits lies in establishing links among research community partners within Europe but also with 
non-EU countries.

The economic impact of such programmes can be assessed only several years after the completion 
of  the  projects  because  normally  further  substantive  investment  is  required  after  the  end  of  the 
projects to develop commercial applications.

10.

Research on Coal and Steel

The Treaty establishing the European Coal and Steel Community (ECSC) expired on 23 July 2002. 
The remaining assets at the expiry of the ECSC were transferred to the European Community and a 
common Research Fund for Coal and Steel (RFCS) was created, dedicated to research in the coal 
and steel sectors. The activities carried out under this fund are exclusively financed by the yearly 
returns generated by these assets.

The Research Programme of the Research Fund for Coal and Steel was established by the Council 
Decisions  2003/76/EC,  2003/77/EC,  2003/78/EC  of  1 February 2003,  and  2008/376/EC  of 
29 April 2008  published  in  the  Official  Journal  of  5 February 2003  (see  OJ  L  29/22,  OJ  L  29/25, 
OJ L 29/28).  This  last  decision  has  been  amended  by  the  Council  Decision  2008/376/EC  of 
29 April 2008, published in the OJ L130/7, dated 20.5.2008.

On 16 July 2020, the Commission adopted a package of proposals to revise the three legal bases 
regulating the Research Programme of the Research Fund for Coal and Steel.

In July 2021, the EU adopted the new RFCS package. This modernisation act will enable the fund to 
use a portion of the assets of the European Steel and Coal Community (ECSC) in liquidation for the 
period 2021-27 in order to provide an annual RFCS allocation up to € 111 million.

The new RFCS legal base was used for the calls launched in 2022. The Commission has the mandate, 
through its budgetary services, to manage the assets of the ECSC in liquidation.

This  will  contribute  towards  the  funding  of  large  clean  steelmaking  research  and  innovation 
breakthrough projects, leading to near zero-carbon steel making by 2030; and research projects for 
the coal sector, to assist the just transition of formerly operating coal mines or coal mines in the 
process of closure, and related infrastructure in line with the Just Transition Mechanism.

 
G/SCM/N/430/EU

- 45 -

Specifically to the sector related to the steel industry, the Commission will aim to support research 
projects  via  a  dedicated  Clean  Steel  co-programmed  partnership  in  synergy  with  other 
European Union programmes, especially with Horizon Europe.

The programme is closely co-ordinated with other activities, carried out either in the Member States 
or existing EU programmes, such as the EU Framework Programme for research, and innovation. In 
order  to  increase  the  impact  of  this  programme,  the  European  Commission  will  encourage  and 
promote  co-ordination,  complementarities  and  synergy  between  the  different  programmes  and 
support  the  exchange  of  information  between  projects  financed  under  this  programme  and  those 
financed under the Research Framework Programme.

The  programme  related  documents  and  the  information  package  are  available  on  CORDIS  at: 
https://research-and-innovation.ec.europa.eu/funding/funding-opportunities/funding-programmes-
and-open-calls/research-fund-coal-and-steel-rfcs_en#documents

For coal and steel, the Union contributes financially in the following ways: through research, pilot 
and demonstration (RTD) projects, accompanying measures and support and preparatory actions.

(1)

Research, Pilot and Demonstration:

Research projects are intended to cover investigative or experimental work with the aim of acquiring 
further knowledge. The financial contribution of the Union is up to 60% of the allowable costs.

Pilot projects address the construction, operation and development of an installation or a significant 
part of an installation on an appropriate scale. These installations should have a sufficient scale for 
use.  They  should  allow  the  investigation  of  potential  for  putting  theoretical  or  laboratory  results 
either  into  practice  or  to  increase  the  reliability  of  the  technical  and  economic  data  needed  to 
progress to the demonstration stage, and in certain cases to the industrial and/or commercial stage. 
The financial contribution of the Union is up to 50% of the allowable costs.

Demonstration  projects:  aim  at  constructing  and/or  operating  an  industrial-scale  installation  or  a 
significant part of an industrial-scale installation. Such projects aim to bring together all the technical 
and  economic  data  in  order  to  proceed  with  the  industrial  and/or  commercial  exploitation  of  the 
technology at minimum risk. The financial contribution of the Union is up to 50% of the allowable 
costs.

(2)

(3)

Accompanying  measures  are  designed  to  complement  and/or  co-ordinate 
research activities related to this programme.
Support measures: this  includes  the  dissemination  of  research  results  and 
the promotion of the use of knowledge gained.

It is worth noting that the results of all RFCS funded research projects are made public and can be 
acquired  against  a  small  fee  by  any  organisation  or  individual,  as  provided  for  under  Article  4  of 
Annex III of the model contract. This allows for a wide and non-discriminatory dissemination of the 
research results.

7  THE EUROPEAN UNION'S COHESION POLICY

The  EU  funds  allocated  to  cohesion  policy  are  intended  to  strengthen  the  economic  and  social 
cohesion of the Union, in particular by reducing disparities between  the  levels  of  development  of 
the  various  regions  and  the  backwardness  of  the least-favoured regions.

For  the  programming  period  2021-2027,  the  legal  framework  is  laid  down  in  legislative  package 
composed of the following elements:

-

A  general  regulation  which  defines  common  rules,  applicable  to  the  European 
Regional Development Fund (ERDF), the Cohesion Fund (CF), the Just Transition Fund

(JTF),  the  European  Social  Fund  Plus  (ESF+)17,  based  on  the  principle  of  'shared 
management' between the Union, the Member States and the regions.
A regulation for each of the sources of financing (ERDF  and  CF, JTF, ESF+)  and  on 
European Territorial Co-operation (Interreg).

7.1  European Regional Development Fund

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants  or  other  forms  of  support  (e.g.  financial  instruments)  from  the  Union  budget  for  ERDF 
resources  are  mainly  used  to  co-finance,  among  others,  investment  in  infrastructure,  productive 
investments leading to the creation or maintenance of jobs and activities for applied research and 
innovation.

6.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Regional policy targets all regions and cities in the European Union in order to support job creation, 
business competitiveness, economic growth, sustainable development, and improve citizens' quality 
of life.

For the period 2014-2020 the ERDF focuses its investments on several key priority areas. This is 
known as "thematic concentration":

-
-
-
-

Innovation and research;
The digital agenda;
Support for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs);
The low-carbon economy.

For the period 2021-2027  programming period  are  mainly  used  to support five specific objectives, 
which aim to make Europe:

 more competitive and smarter, through innovation and support to small and medium-

sized businesses (SMEs), as well as digitisation and digital connectivity
greener, low-carbon and resilient


 more connected by enhancing mobility
 more social, supporting effective and inclusive employment, education, skills, social 
inclusion and equal access to healthcare, as well as enhancing the role of culture and 
sustainable tourism
closer to citizens, supporting locally-led development and sustainable urban 
development across the EU.



7.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The rules on eligibility are determined at the national level, except when there are general rules laid 
down in the applicable EU legislation.

In the periods 2014-2020 and 2021-2027 alike, resources for the Investment for growth and jobs goal 
are allocated among the following three categories of regions:

a.

b.

less developed  regions, whose  GDP per capita is less than 75% of the  average  GDP of 
the EU-27;
transition regions, whose GDP per capita is between 75% and 90% of the average GDP 
of the EU-27;

c. more  developed  regions,  whose  GDP  per  capita  is  above  90%  of  the  average  GDP 

of the EU-27.

8.

Principles of intervention

The principles of intervention are complementarity, coherence and coordination. Member States shall 
take  into  account  the  relevant  country-specific  recommendations  in  the  programming  and 
implementation of the Funds.

 
G/SCM/N/430/EU

- 48 -

9.

Subsidy per unit, or in cases where this is not possible, the total amount or the annual amount 
budgeted for that subsidy

10.

Cohesion policy and state aid

Cohesion Policy provides an integrated, strategic, medium term planning and financial framework to 
address  the  reduction  of  regional  disparities  by  promoting  development.  It  does  this  through 
geographic  and  financial  targeting  (concentration),  governance  processes  and  evaluation 
mechanisms. Within the context of providing financial assistance to economic operators, cohesion 
policy financing must respect the State aid rules where relevant.

In  line  with  EU  cohesion  policy,  the  State  aid  guidelines  focus  regional  aid  on  the  most  deprived 
regions of the enlarged Union, while allowing for the need to improve competitiveness and to provide 
for a smooth transition. The use of the structural funds by national authorities is subject to EU state 
aid rules.

11.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

Because  the  Funds  are  designed  to  alleviate  disparities  between  regions  within  the  Union,  and 
because the aid is paid out on a horizontal basis, within eligible regions, it is considered that the 
trade effects are minimal.

SUBSIDIES

NEW AND FULL NOTIFICATION PURSUANT TO ARTICLE XVI:1 OF
THE GATT 1994 AND ARTICLE 25 OF THE AGREEMENT ON
SUBSIDIES AND COUNTERVAILING MEASURES

UNITED KINGDOM

The following communication, dated and received on 27 June 2025, is being circulated at the request 
of the delegation of the United Kingdom.

_______________

The  following  notification  covers  the  reporting  period  2023  and  2024.  The  notification  covers 
subsidies provided by central and regional government authorities within the territory covered by 
the UK's WTO membership.

The UK submits this notification for the purposes of transparency and without prejudice to the legal 
status,  nature  or  effects  of  the  notified  measures  under  the  Agreement  on  Subsidies  and 
Countervailing  Measures  (ASCM)  and  GATT 1994,  including  as  to  whether  or  not  the  measures 
notified are specific within the meaning of the ASCM.

1  INDUSTRY AND R&D&I (RESEARCH & DEVELOPMENT & INNOVATION)

1.1  UK GOVERNMENT

1.1.1  AEROSPACE TECHNOLOGY INSTITUTE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Aerospace Technology Institute.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Aerospace  Technology  Institute  (ATI)  Programme  offers  funding  for  research  and  technology 
development  in  the  UK  to  maintain  and  grow  the  UK's  competitive  position  in  civil  aerospace.  To 
ensure that the UK has the technology and capabilities needed to anchor high value work on future 
aircraft  programmes  by  increasing  the  amount  of  Research  &  Development  (R&D)  invested  by 
industry  and  government  and  giving  the  certainty  over  future  public  funding  needed  to  influence 
industry's R&D and manufacturing investments.

Desired outcomes:















For Government and industry to commit to multi-year R&D projects over a 5-to-10-year 
horizon in priority areas
Create more competitive aerospace sector value-added work in UK
Attract new customers to existing UK companies and sustain or create new employment 
opportunities
R&D Spillovers
For  UK  Aerospace  sector  to  increase  its  capability  to  secure  greater  EU  and  other  R&D 
funding
Create better synergies and sustained collaborative technology development relationships 
throughout the sector
Target  public  resource  on  greatest  sector  growth  opportunities  and  levers  in  significant 
private investment
Enhanced Government reputation for promoting growth and rebalancing the economy

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 7 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 142,140,668.46
2024 - GBP 110,929,566.79

8.

Duration of the subsidy

09/12/2022 - 09/12/2028 .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data available.

1.1.2  BRITISH  BUSINESS  BANK  –  RECOVERY  LOAN  SCHEME/RETURN  TO  GROWTH 
GUARANTEE SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

British Business Bank – Recovery Loan Scheme/Return to Growth Guarantee Scheme.

Recovery  Loan  Scheme  until  30  June  2024.  Renamed  to  Growth  Guarantee  Scheme  from 
1 July 2024.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The scheme helps smaller businesses access loans and other kinds of finance up to GBP 2 million 
per business group, in order to grow and invest. The finance can be used for any business purpose, 
including growth and investment or working capital.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Section 8 of the Industrial Development Act 1982 and Section 50 of the UK Internal Markets Act 2021 
(Charities and FE colleges only).

The  Recovery  Loan  Scheme  first  launched  on  6  April  2021,  its  second  iteration  launched  on 
1 January 2022,  and  the  third  iteration  1  August  2022.  It  was  renamed  the  Growth  Guarantee 
Scheme on 1 July 2024.

The British Business Bank administers the scheme, which is delivered through accredited lenders. 
The Government provides a 70% guarantee to lenders. Lenders should only offer finance through 
the scheme if they are unable to offer a facility without the guarantee or would only be able to do 
so on worse terms. The borrower remains 100% liable for the debt.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 8 -

5.

Form of the subsidy

Loans or overdrafts and invoice or asset finance.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Accredited lenders deliver the scheme. In order to be eligible, a business must be trading in the UK 
and have a turnover no higher than GBP 45 million. Businesses must derive at least half of their 
income from trading activity, unless they are charities or Further Education colleges for whom this 
obligation can be waived.

Businesses from any sector can apply, except:





Banks, building societies, insurers and reinsurers (but not insurance brokers)
Public sector bodies
State funded primary and secondary schools.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Loans or overdrafts of between GBP 25,001 and GBP 2 million per business group, or GBP 25,001 
and GBP 1 million for businesses in scope of the Northern Ireland Protocol.

Invoice or asset finance of between GBP 1,000 and GBP 2 million per business group, or between 
GBP 1,000 and GBP 1 million for businesses in scope of the Northern Ireland Protocol.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme launched on 6 April 2021 and has a current end date of 31 March 2026. This will be 
reviewed and there is a possibility it will be extended.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data available.

1.1.3  BRITISH BUSINESS BANK – REGIONAL INVESTMENT FUNDS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

British Business Bank Regional Investment Funds, namely:

 Northern Powerhouse Investment Fund
 Midlands Engine Investment Fund


Cornwall and Isles of Scilly Investment Fund

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Funds were designed to address the market failures that drive regional disparities in the UK. 
They  did  this  by  providing  debt  and  equity  finance  to  businesses  typically  seeking  to  invest  in 
expanded facilities, hire staff or acquire another firm, in areas that are known to be under-served 
by  finance  providers.  Businesses  were  required  to  show  that  they  had  previously  been  unable  to 
access finance from at least one commercial provider.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Legislative authority is provided by Section 8 of the Industrial Development Act 1982.

The  first  Fund,  the  Northern  Powerhouse  Investment  Fund,  launched  in  February  2017. 
Midlands Engine  and  Cornwall  and  Isles  of  Scilly  Funds  launched  thereafter  and  all  three  funds 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 9 -

finished their investment phase for new applicants in December 2023. Some new follow-on equity 
investments from these funds were made in 2024 and the fund managers will continue to administer 
the loans and investments for several years.

The British Business Bank administers the scheme, which is delivered through accredited delivery 
partners.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Debt and equity finance.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Accredited  fund  managers  delivered  the  scheme  to  small  businesses  in  the  investment  areas. 
Businesses were eligible if they met normal commercial criteria for attracting debt finance or equity 
investment but had difficulty in accessing private finance. Fund managers were expected to attract 
commercial co-investment alongside government capital. The effective subsidy was a discount on 
the  interest  rate  or  equity  investment  terms  that  would  be  provided  in  the  private  market  for  a 
business with the same risk profile, all else being equal. Some of the capital for the programme was 
provided by the European Regional Development Fund.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The programme offered:

 microfinance (loans from GBP 25,000-GBP 100,000);



debt finance (loans from GBP 100,000-GBP 750,000); and
early-stage and later-stage equity (from GBP 50,000-GBP 2 million).

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  Northern  Powerhouse  Investment  Fund,  launched  in  February  2017.  Midlands  Engine  and 
Cornwall and Isles of Scilly Funds launched thereafter and all three funds finished their investment 
phase  for  new  applicants  in  December  2023.  The  equity  fund  managers  will  continue  to  make 
follow-on investments to support the growth of portfolio companies.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

The Regional Investment Funds were open to all sectors. For those recipients of loans or investments 
producing goods, information on the trade effects cannot be disaggregated from general economic 
conditions. The impact of the programme has been independently assessed. Evaluation reports on 
the three funds are available at the following URLs:

https://www.british-business-bank.co.uk/about/research-and-publications/northern-powerhouse-
investment-fund-interim-evaluation-report

interim-evaluation-report-2023

evaluation-report-2023

1.1.4  BRIDGE AI

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

BridgeAI.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 10 -

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The BridgeAI programme aims to stimulate the adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies 
in lower AI maturity UK sectors: 1) agri-food, 2) construction, 3) transport 4) creative industries. It 
will achieve its objectives by building an innovation network to stimulate both demand and supply 
side engagement, develop end-user driven AI technologies and address systemic barriers limiting 
adoption.

Programme Objectives:













Increase  the  number  of  new  trusted  AI  products  and  services  being  developed  for  the 
targeted sectors
Raise industry awareness of economic benefits from adoption of AI
Build networks between AI developers and AI adopters
Build fit for purpose business models for new AI products in the targeted sectors
Increase the number of SMEs (Small or Medium-sized Enterprise) utilising more than one 
AI solution
Reduce  the  technical  skills  gap  limiting  adoption  through  upskilling  and  reskilling  of 
adopters
Support development of business skills to enable growth at scale for AI developers
Reduce Equality Diversity and Inclusion gap in AI technical positions

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 74,857
2024 - GBP 10,669,463

8.

Duration of the subsidy

09/12/2022 – 31/05/2026 .

1.1.5  BRITISH ENERGY SUPERCHARGER

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

The British Energy Supercharger.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The scheme is in place to provide Energy Intensive Industries (EIIs) relief from policy costs passed 
on  by  electricity  suppliers.  The  scheme  aims  to  mitigate  the  risk  of  carbon  leakage  and  make 
electricity costs more competitive.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  Supercharger  consists  of:  the  existing  Contracts  for  Difference  (CFD,  included  in  this 
notification),  Renewables  Obligation  (RO)  and  Feed-In-Tariff  (FIT)  scheme  which  has  existed 
since 2016. An exemption from the costs of the Capacity Market (CM, included in this notification) 
and a partial compensation (up to 60%) for Network Charging costs. Together these costs make up 
a  significant  proportion  of  EII  electricity  policy  costs.  Eligibility  for  the  scheme  is  set  out  in  the 
Electricity Supplier Obligations (Amendment & Excluded Electricity) Regulations 2015 (as amended) 
but there are individual pieces of legislation governing each element of the Supercharger.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The  CFD,  RO,  FIT  and  CM  operate  as  a  bill  discount  which  electricity  suppliers  pass  on  to  their 
business  customers.  The  costs  are  recovered  from  non-exempt  electricity  users.  The  Network 
Charging  element  operates  as  a  compensation  scheme  with  costs  recovered  from  non-eligible 
customers via electricity bills.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

To qualify for the scheme, applicants must:

 Manufacture a product in the UK within an eligible sector ("sector-level test").


Pass a "business-level test" designed to focus support on those businesses most at risk. 
This includes demonstrating that their electricity costs amount to 20% or more of their 
value of their earnings (as Gross Value Added, GVA) over a reference period.
Applicants  must  also  provide  at  least  one  quarter  of  financial  data  and  evidence  of  the 
proportion  of  electricity  used  to  manufacture  the  product  for  a  period  of  at  least 
three months.



There  are  currently  67  eligible  sectors  and  around  500  eligible  businesses  although  the  scheme 
operates on an ongoing basis.

Eligible businesses receive a certificate which they pass to their electricity supplier to receive the 
exemption and submit data to LCCC to benefit from the Network Charging compensation scheme.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 total: GBP 348,866,616






CFD: GBP 49,384,777
RO: GBP 239,716,196
FIT: GBP 59,765,643
CM: GBP 0

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 12 -

2024 total: GBP 508,773,528






CFD: GBP 101,239,190
RO: GBP 340,969,341
FIT: GBP 66,564,996
CM: GBP 0

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Supercharger is subject to regular review with the next scheduled review due in 2029 (five years 
after it started). There is also a scheduled data refresh exercise in 2026 to ensure that the relief is 
targeted at the most electricity intensive sectors.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.6  CAPACITY MARKET

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Capacity Market.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  policy  objective  is  to  ensure  sufficient  reliable  capacity  in  the  Great  Britain  (GB)  electricity 
market.  This  needs  to  be  achieved  in  a  cost-effective  way  while  minimising  unintended 
consequences. It is the government's principal mechanism for capacity adequacy, or in other words, 
for securing sufficient electricity supplies to meet future peak demand. The scheme ensures security 
of electricity supply by providing a payment for reliable sources of capacity, alongside their electricity 
revenues, to ensure they deliver energy when needed. This encourages the investment required to 
replace  older  power  stations  and  provide  backup  for  more  intermittent  and  inflexible  low  carbon 
generation sources.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  CM  scheme  ('the  scheme')  was  introduced  in  2014  as  part  of  the  Electricity  Market  Reform 
programme.  The  legal  basis  for  the  scheme  is  the  Energy  Act  2013  ('the  Act').  S  27(1)  of  the 
Act 2013  states:  "The  Secretary  of  State  may  by  regulations  make  provision  for  the  purpose  of 
providing capacity to meet the demands of consumers for the supply of electricity in Great Britain." 
S 5(2) of the Act contains other general considerations when the Secretary of State (SoS) exercises 
its power under S 27 of the Act.

The Electricity Capacity Regulations 2014 ('the Regulations') were established under the vires of the 
Act to cover significant issues such as functions of the SoS & delivery partners, setting parameters, 
administration of payments, penalties, termination fees and applicant credit cover, holding capacity 
auctions, and the amount of capacity sought through them. The Capacity Market Rules 2014 focus 
on  technical  processes  and  procedure.  The  Rules  and  Regulations  are  amended  periodically  to 
improve the scheme's effectiveness.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The  subsidy  is  given  in  monthly  payments  to  Capacity  Market  Agreement  holders  covering  their 
commitment to meet capacity obligations for that Delivery Year. These costs are levied on suppliers 
(who pass them onto consumers) through a supplier levy. We consider the CM meets the definition 
of a subsidy in the ASCM in which a subsidy shall be deemed to exist if there is a financial contribution 
by a public body which involves the direct transfer of funds (e.g. grants).

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 13 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The CM has been designed to support generators, interconnectors, storage and demand flexibility 
providers in the electricity market. It secures electricity capacity through competitive auctions held 
four years ahead of the delivery year, known as the T-4 auction; and one year ahead of the delivery 
year, the T-1 auction, to effectively "top up" capacity closer to the delivery year should we need to 
(based on the latest information available such as the latest electricity demand forecast, and any 
commercial  information).  The  auctions  are  technology  neutral,  with  existing  capacity  competing 
against a range of new generators, of different technology types, to obtain capacity agreements.

Capacity  providers  receive  regular  payments  in  exchange  for  their  capacity  being  available  when 
consumers  need  it  most  i.e.,  when  consumer  demand  is  high  compared  to  available  electricity 
supplies.  Capacity  providers  may  face  financial  penalties  if  they  fail  to  deliver  against  their 
agreements.  New  build  capacity  can  win  agreements  of  up  to  15  years.  Longer  agreements 
guarantee  revenue  certainty  for  capacity  providers  and  de-risk  the  significant  upfront  capital 
investment  involved  in  building  new  capacity.  Existing  capacity  is  allowed  to  compete  for  annual 
agreements, to incentivise investment in maintaining existing capacity, and reducing the overall cost 
to the consumer of having to continually invest in new build capacity. The scheme has an annual 
delivery cycle.

Two auctions are held each year with the auction targets set in July and later updated just ahead of 
the auctions. Capacity can apply to pre-qualify for the auctions from July to September, with auctions 
taking place a minimum of ten weeks from the Prequalification Assessment closing.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

In  the  period  covered  by  this  notification  (2023/2024),  the  total  amount  provided  to  Capacity 
Providers was GBP 2,154 million, GBP 898 million of which was for 2023 and GBP 1,256 million was 
for 2024.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Regarding  the  scheme's  duration,  the  scheme  has  no  defined  end  point.  The  Electricity  Capacity 
Mechanism  (Amendment  Regulations  2025  ('Amendment  Regulations')  enacted  in  January  2025 
removed  the  requirement  for  an  approval  lasting  no  more  than  ten  years  and  references  to  a 
temporary  nature  in  relation  to  capacity  mechanisms.  This  requirement  was  contained  in  the 
assimilated  EU  Electricity  Regulation  (2019/943)  which  were  brought  into  domestic  legislation 
following EU Exit. These changes were made to ensure the domestic statute book is consistent with 
the continued operation of the CM.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.7  DIGITAL SECURITY BY DESIGN

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Digital Security by Design.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 14 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Cybersecurity is a major UK government strategic priority. Digital Security by Design (DSbD) will 
enable a more trustworthy digital environment, in which only expected access to data and operations 
are  permitted  while  limiting  the  impact  of  vulnerabilities.  DSbD  will  promote  a  mindset  change 
around cyber security, giving the freedom to learn, trade, play, automate and collaborate safely.

Desired Outcomes

It is expected that there would be an acceleration in or towards:









Establishing and growing expertise in DSbD across a variety of UK regions.
Increasing  UK  international  reputation  for  DSbD  solutions  and  innovations  -  with 
opportunities for exports and global leadership.
Laying  the  foundations  for  the  creation  of  new  DSbD  related  products,  services  and 
applications
The commercialisation of DSbD innovations
The uptake of DSbD technologies across industries resulting in more robust information 
security across multiple domains, sectors and solutions

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

1.1.8  DISPATCHABLE POWER AGREEMENT BUSINESS MODEL

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

The Dispatchable Power Agreement Business Model.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The specific policy objective for the Dispatchable Power Agreement (DPA) business model subsidy 
scheme is to incentivise power projects to produce low-carbon, mid-merit electricity, meaning plants 
are only incentivised to turn on when zero carbon sources of generation, such as renewables and 
nuclear power, are not meeting the energy demands of the country, and in doing so, contribute to 
the decarbonisation of the electricity system.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The public authority responsible for the DPA business model subsidy scheme is the Department for 
Energy Security and Net Zero.

The Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero will use the powers in Section 10 of the 
Energy Act 2013 to direct a DPA Counterparty to offer initial DPA contracts to generators. To enter 
into a DPA, a project must meet the definition of an Eligible Generator, specified in the Contracts for 
Difference  (Definition  of  Eligible  Generator)  Regulations  2014  and  amended  by  the  Contracts  for 
Difference (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2022, which came into force on 21 June 2022 
to enable retrofit CCS projects to be eligible and adapted the definition of Complete CCS System to 
include non-pipeline transport of CO2.

Funding for the DPA subsidy payments will be drawn from the electricity supplier obligation levy, a 
compulsory levy on licensed electricity suppliers to meet the cost of the CfD scheme in accordance 
with  The  Contracts  for  Difference  (Electricity  Supplier  Obligations)  Regulations  2014.  Obligation 
payments will be collected from electricity suppliers by the Low Carbon Contracts Company in their 
role as the Counterparty to the DPA contract, then relayed to the DPA Generators subject to their 
performance under the terms of the contract.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Time limited revenue support by way of contract.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  Dispatchable  Power  Agreement  (DPA)  is  a  private  law  contract,  based  on  the  renewables 
contract for difference (CfD), of between ten and 15 years between a power plant developer and a 
DPA contract counterparty.

The applications of prospective DPA projects are first assessed against the following criteria to ensure 
they are eligible for selection:

Located onshore in Great Britain


 Have one of the eligible configurations, i.e. must be:

A natural gas fuelled thermal generation plant; and
A new build or retrofit plant; and



 One of post-combustion, pre-combustion, or oxy-fuelled combustion

 Have a minimum abated capacity of 100MWe
 Have access to a CO₂ transport solution and CO₂ storage site.
 Have a minimum projected capture rate of 90%

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 16 -

The DPA has two payment mechanisms, the availability payment (paid to the generator on a regular 
basis) and the variable payment (effectively a reactive top-up on the wholesale market price paid 
on generation output).

7.

Amount of the subsidy

There  is  no  pre-determined  value  for  the  maximum  individual  award.  We  will  limit  the  maximum 
subsidy award via a threshold of subsidy intensity, or total subsidy revenues as a proportion of the 
total project revenues of a beneficiary.

The maximum amount budgeted for the scheme is GBP 30,000,000,000.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  scheme  was  made  on  28  August  2024.  The  scheme  is  anticipated  to  run  until 
31 December 2045.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.9  ENERGY  BILLS  DISCOUNT  SCHEME  (EBDS)  -  ENERGY  AND  TRADE  INTENSIVE 
INDUSTRIES SCHEME (ETII)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Energy  Bills  Discount  Scheme  –  provision  of  a  higher-level  discount  for  Energy  Trade  Intensive 
Industries.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Energy  and  Trade  Intensive  Industries  Scheme  (ETII)  was  an  element  of  the  Energy  Bills 
Discount  Scheme  which  provided  a  substantially  higher  level  of  support  to  businesses  in  sectors 
identified as being the most energy and trade intensive – predominately manufacturing industries. 
These  firms  were  often  less  able  to  pass  through  cost  to  their  customers  due  to  international 
competition.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  Energy  and  Trade  Intensive  Industries  (ETII)  subsidy  was  granted  under  The  Energy  Bills 
Discount Scheme (EBDS) which provided all eligible businesses and other non-domestic energy users 
in Great Britain and Northern Ireland with a baseline discount on high energy bills. The Energy Bills 
Discount  Scheme  was  established  in  regulations  made  under  the  Energy  Prices  Act  2022.  The 
regulations came into force on 26 April 2023.

The scheme applied to energy usage from 1 April 2023 to 1 March 2024. The scheme didn't change 
over time.

The EBDS  baseline scheme  itself was  not  considered  a subsidy  because it did not  apply selective 
benefits.  This  means  it  was  not  subject  to  the  subsidy  control  requirements  under  the 
Subsidy Control  Act/Trade  Cooperation  Agreement;  an  assessment  against  the  subsidy  control 
principles wasn't required to be made nor, to the extent applicable, was a referral to the Competition 
and Markets Authority for their review required. However, the ETII support provided to Great Britain 
and Northern Ireland was a subsidy.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 17 -

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) was responsible for administering the 
scheme.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Recipients of the subsidy included businesses in eligible sectors in Great Britain and Northern Ireland 
that were highly exposed to both energy prices and international competition and were therefore 
unable to pass through or absorb costs.

Energy suppliers submitted the total ETII discounts applied to bills during specific claim windows to 
DESNZ for payment. The payments were made on the behalf of government by either Xoserve for 
gas discounts and Elexon for electricity discounts.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The  total  value  of  support  provided  over  the  scheme  period  was  GBP  178,246,677.90  –  this  was 
inclusive of the baseline discounts.

The breakdown for ETII specific discounts Great Britain and Northern Ireland is as follows:

 GB ETII Discount: GBP 168,891,396.42
 NI ETII Discount: GBP 1,068,382.69

8.

Duration of the subsidy

1 April 2023 – 31 March 2024..

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.10  ENTERPRISE INVESTMENT SCHEME (EIS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

The Enterprise Investment Scheme (EIS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The aim of the EIS is to support the growth of certain smaller, unquoted trading companies which 
due to their early development stage, would otherwise struggle to have access to finance due to an 
insufficient  track  record  and/or  poor  collaterals.  For  this  purpose,  income  and  capital  gains  tax 
incentives are provided under the scheme to individuals who subscribe for new shares in qualifying 
companies.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  legal  authority  for  the  scheme  is  Part  5  of  the  Income  Tax  Act  2007,  and  Section  150A  and 
Schedule 5B  of  the  Taxation  of  Chargeable  Gains  Act  1992.  The  scheme  is  Administered  by 
HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC).

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 18 -

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax  relief  to  individual  investors  on  the  amounts  subscribed  in  new  shares  issued  by  qualifying 
companies.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The EIS places qualifying conditions on the investor, the shares issued and the issuing company. A 
company seeking to raise tax incentivised funds under the EIS must be unquoted with gross assets 
of maximum GBP 15 million (pre-investment) and with fewer than 250 full-time employees (500 if 
the company is "knowledge intensive"). It must have a permanent establishment in the UK and it 
must raise the funds for a qualifying trade; one which does not consist of "excluded activities" (such 
as energy generation, farming, shipbuilding and steel production). A full list of excluded activities 
2007: 
can 
https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2007/3/section/192.

Section 

192(1) 

found 

Tax 

the 

Act 

be 

of 

in 

The company must be intending to grow and develop its trade over the long-term and the money 
raised must be employed within 2 years of investment. The annual investment tranche which the 
company  can  obtain  and  on  which  tax  reliefs  will  be  available  to  its  investors  is  limited  to 
GBP 5,000,000 and to GBP 12,000,000 overall (GBP 10,000,000 and GBP 20,000,000 respectively 
if  the  company  is  knowledge  intensive).  The  company  must  also  receive  its  first  EIS  investment 
within 7 years of its first commercial sale (10 years if knowledge intensive).

The  tax  incentives  available  to  qualifying  individual  investors  (the  investor  must  not  to  be  an 
employee  of  the  company  or  qualifying  subsidiary  or  have  a  substantial  interest  in  the  issuing 
company) are:


Income tax relief at 30% of the amount invested in new full-risk ordinary shares in target 
companies. The amount of the tax relief is set off against the investor's UK income tax 
charge on amounts invested of up to GBP 1,000,000 per year (GBP 2,000,000 where the 
additional GBP 1,000,000 is in knowledge intensive companies). The eligible shares must 
be held for at least 3 years.
Relief from capital gains tax on gains from shares that have qualified for income tax relief 
and which are disposed of at least 3 years after the investment



 Where the investor has made a taxable capital gain on the disposal of any other asset, the 
tax charge arising on this gain can be deferred if the gain is reinvested in shares under 
the EIS.
If the shares are disposed of at a loss, investors can elect that the amount of the loss, less 
any Income Tax relief given, can be set against income of the year in which the shares 
were disposed of, or any income of the previous year, instead of being set off against any 
capital gains.



7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023-24: GBP 640m.
2024-25: GBP 645m.

Please note:




Values for 2023-24 and 2024-25 are forecasted costs of the EIS tax relief
This includes the cost for the Income Tax and Capital Gains Tax disposals relief
Figures are rounded to the nearest GBP 5m

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2017 - 5 April 2035.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.11  ENTERPRISE MANAGEMENT INCENTIVE (EMI)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Enterprise Management Incentive (EMI).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The EMI scheme is a share scheme which is targeted at small and medium enterprises (SMEs). The 
objective of the scheme is to help these enterprises recruit, retain, and reward key employees so 
they can scale up effectively.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  legal  authority  for  the  scheme  is  Sections  527  to  541  and  Schedule  5  of  the  Income  Tax 
(Earnings  and  Pensions)  Act  2003.  The  scheme  is  administered  by  HM  Revenue  and  Customs 
(HMRC).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax relief (on Income Tax and National Insurance contributions, NICs).

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The scheme, which is a tax advantaged share scheme, allows eligible SMEs to grant their employees 
options to acquire shares at a price fixed when the options are granted.

Neither NICs (on the employer and employee) nor Income Tax (on the employee) are chargeable 
on  the  grant  or  exercise  of EMI options  if  the  options  are  exercised  within  10  years  of  the  grant 
(unless the options were granted at a discount). The total relief depends on the gain in value of the 
shares acquired when options are exercised, which is unlimited. The employer's NICs relief means 
that the company itself receives some direct benefit from the scheme.

To participate, the company must have fewer than 250 full-time employees and GBP 30,000,000 or 
less in gross assets. The company can grant to each employee options worth up to GBP 250,000. 
Each company can have up to GBP 3,000,000 of shares under option at any one time. EMI is available 
whether the employer produces goods or supplies services or conducts both activities.

The scheme is not available to companies which undertake certain excluded activities specified in 
Schedule 5 of the Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003, such as financial activities, farming 
or market gardening, shipbuilding and steel production.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Tax year 2022/23: GBP 500m

Estimated cost of NIC relief and income tax relief for tax year 2023/24 is not yet available.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme commenced in 2000 and is ongoing.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 20 -

1.1.12  ENERGY CATALYST

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Energy Catalyst.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Energy Catalyst programme supports UK and overseas businesses and organisations to develop 
highly innovative, market-focused energy technologies that primarily look to enable energy access 
in Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and the Indo-Pacific regions. Recipients of the award must be a 
UK-based business but can distribute funding to international partners.

Projects must address the following challenges:

Energy trilemma based on 3 pillars: cost, emissions and security
Energy access



 Gender equality and social inclusion

Desired Outcomes





The aim is to accelerate the innovations needed to create new or improved clean energy 
access  in  sub-Saharan  Africa,  South  Asia  or  Indo-Pacific  regions,  to  meet sustainable 
development goals (SDGs) 7 'Affordable and clean energy' and 13 'Climate action'.
Energy  Catalyst  accelerates  the  innovation  needed  to  end  energy  poverty.  Through 
financial and advisory support, and by building strategic partnerships and uncovering new 
insights, Energy Catalyst helps bring to market technologies and business models that can 
improve lives in Africa and Asia.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 21 -

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 18,390,693.53.
2024 - GBP 20,786,579.97.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

15/11/2022-15/11/2028. .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.13  ENHANCED CAPITAL ALLOWANCES IN ENTERPRISE ZONES

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

First-year allowances for expenditure on plant and machinery in designated assisted areas within 
enterprise zones.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Enterprise  Zones  were  introduced  to  encourage  economic  growth  and  investment  in  designated 
assisted areas.

The tax incentives available for investment in enterprise zones are either a 100% Business Rates 
discount of up to GBP 275,000 over a five-year period, or an enhanced capital allowance (ECA), also 
known as a first-year allowance, for the purchase of plant or machinery.

The  ECA  is  designed  to  incentivise  companies  to  invest  in  plant  and  machinery  in  a  smaller 
geographical  area  within  an  enterprise  zone  which  is  designated  by  the  Treasury,  known  as  a 
designated assisted area.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Enterprise  zones  were  introduced  from  2012  and  are  geographically  defined  areas  in  which 
businesses  can  receive  incentives  to  set  up  or  expand.  Initially  only  24  enterprise  zones  were 
announced when the scheme went live, with additional enterprise zones being created in 2015 and 
2016.

The  period  in  which  the  ECA  is  available  has  been  amended  a  number  of  times  since  it  was  first 
introduced:


From 1/4/2012, the ECA was available for qualifying investment during a fixed five-year 
period to 31/3/2017;
The fixed five-year period was later extended to eight years to 31/3/2020;
From 15/9/2016, this was widened to an eight-year period commencing from the date of 
the announcement of an enterprise zone; and
From  1/4/2020  the  period  was  extended  to  31/3/2021  for  those  areas  where  the 
eight-year period was due to expire before then.






The legal authority for the scheme is the Capital Allowances Act 2001 (Sections 45K-45N; 212U). 
The Scheme is administered by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax concession.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 22 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Companies  investing  in  new  plant  and  machinery  which  is  primarily  for  use  within  a  designated 
assisted area within an enterprise zone for their business are eligible for this subsidy. To qualify, the 
company must be within the charge to corporation tax and it has incurred expenditure on plant or 
machinery, which is:

Primarily for use within a designated assisted area.


 Unused and not second-hand.



For an activity which is a trade, or certain activities treated as a trade for tax purposes.
For the purposes of a new business, or expansion of an existing business, or a new activity 
relating to a fundamental change in a product, process or service provided by the business.

 Not replacement expenditure.


Incurred  on  or  before  31  March  2021,  or  before  the  expiry  of  the  eight-year  period  for 
investment in the relevant designated assisted area if after then.

 Not  covered  by  any  general  exclusions  for  plant  and  machinery  first-year  allowances, 

e.g. where the plant or machinery is acquired for leasing

The allowance is partially restricted where, at the time it incurs the expenditure, the company intends 
to use the plant and machinery partially outside of a designated assisted area, and a main purpose 
for this is to obtain the first-year allowance. There is also a clawback of the subsidy where the plant 
and machinery later become primarily for use outside of a designated assisted area within 5 years 
from when it was first available for use in the company's business.

There  are  further  restrictions  which  exclude  certain  sectors1,  and  also  provide  that  otherwise 
qualifying expenditure:





does not exceed EUR 125,000,000 per undertaking;
has not been subsidised by a grant;
incurred by large businesses is only eligible for ECA for investment in certain designated 
assisted areas where it is to expand an existing business.

In addition, one of the general conditions for this tax concession is that the plant or machinery must 
be for an activity which is a trade or certain activities, which are treated as a trade for tax purposes. 
Not all activities meet this condition, e.g. companies with an investment business, or for property 
rental.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Estimated total subsidy:

2023: GBP 1,000,000.
2024: GBP 1,000,000.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

April 2012 – March 2024.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.14  FARADAY BATTERY CHALLENGE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Faraday Battery Challenge.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 23 -

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Faraday  Battery  Challenge  Innovation  programme  is  supporting  UK  businesses  to  push  the 
boundaries  of  battery  innovation  and  grow  the  UK  battery  supply  chain.  UKRI  funding  from 
Innovate UK has been invested for businesses to lead feasibility studies, and collaborative research 
and development projects across the battery value chain. This investment has been in collaboration 
with the UK's world-leading academics and research technology organisations. These projects have 
supported over 200 organisations across the UK, with 80% of the projects led by micro, small or 
medium-sized enterprises (SMEs).

To aid skills development across the UK, the Faraday Battery Challenge has commissioned projects 
to tackle known skills challenges and provided training through UKBIC. It is collaboratively aligning 
industry and skills providers across the UK through a skills framework and unlocking training demand 
in regions linked to industry need.

Projects  funded  through  this  scheme  support  SME  research  and  development  for  the  scale-up  of 
battery technologies within the UK. Its aim  is to move UK battery innovations from technological 
potential towards commercial capability and help develop and secure material and manufacturing 
supply chains for battery technologies in the UK.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

Grant.

organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved

1.1.15  HEALTH & LIFE SCIENCES, HEALTHY LIVING

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Health & Life Sciences.

Healthy Living.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Health and Life Sciences supports the development of ever more innovative solutions to address the 
challenges  and  the  demand  for:  more  efficiently  produced  food,  healthier  and  traceable  food, 
improved healthcare with better outcomes for patients and for the health system. Funding will help 
bridge an identified investment funding gap in early-stage life sciences innovation.

Desired Outcomes






To stimulate investment in R&D
To deliver growth to UK Life Sciences sector
To deliver solutions to healthcare challenges quickly and more effectively
Support Life Sciences companies to scale-up and grow in the UK by increasing the supply 
of venture capital growth

 Development and commercialisation of Life Sciences goods and services

As Healthy Living is closely linked, we are reporting under one scheme. The challenge vision was to 
allow people to remain active, productive, independent and socially connected across generations 
for as long as possible.

It  would  achieve  this  by  enabling  businesses,  including  social  enterprises,  to  develop  and  deliver 
products and services which support people as they age, and the innovative business models that 
enable them to be adopted at scale.

Taking  an  ecosystem  approach  to  innovation  and  research,  the  Healthy  Ageing  Challenge  was  a 
transformative initiative, with both social and economic impacts already felt across the UK.

Research and innovations addressed key societal challenges aligned to seven themes:

Creating healthy active places

 Design for age-friendly homes
Living well with cognitive impairment

 Managing common complaints of ageing
 Maintaining health at work



Supporting social connections
Sustaining physical activity

brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018,

1.1.16  HEAT PUMP INVESTMENT ACCELERATOR COMPETITION

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Heat Pump Investment Accelerator Competition.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Heat Pump Investment Accelerator Competition aims to incentivise the manufacturing of heat 
pumps and their components. The Competition will award non-repayable grant funding for eligible 
capital  costs,  relating  to  either  the  construction  of  new  factories,  or  the  expansion,  retooling  or 
reworking  of  existing  factories,  to  produce  heat  pumps  and/or  their  components,  such  as  heat 
exchangers, compressors, evaporators, control systems or thermal storage.

The supported factories must be operational by 31 March 2027 at the latest.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme bid window opened in July 2022 and the first award was made in November 2024. The 
grant is being made under the Natural Environment and Rural Communities Act 2006, Section 98.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 26 -

The  scheme  has  and  is  being  administered  solely  by  the  Department  for  Energy  Security  and 
Net Zero,  previously  the  Department  for  Business,  Energy  and  Industrial  Strategy  when  the  bid 
window was open. The grants were awarded through a competitive process, with one bid window 
for all applicants to apply during, and applications were then scored and ranked based on this final 
score.

Grants were then awarded through the use of grant offer letters to the top ranked applicants which 
agreed the intervention rate and maximum amount of grant that will be paid. The grant letters also 
include contractual obligations concerning job creation, job protection and capacity creation (but not 
production output).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

In terms of eligibility, any company engaging in the manufacture of heat pumps and/or heat pump 
components was eligible to apply for the competition.

The grant is paid in arrears based on incurring eligible expenditure based on an agreed intervention 
rate, up to a maximum amount of grant not exceeding GBP 15m in Great Britain and EUR 15m in 
Northern Ireland.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The  maximum  amount  of  grant  per  project  was  GBP  15m  in  Great  Britain  or  EUR  15m  in 
Northern Ireland,  at  a  maximum  intervention  rate  of  45%  for  large  companies,  55%  for  medium 
companies and 65% for small and micro companies. The total budget for the scheme was GBP 30m.

In  2024,  two  grant  offer  letters  were  signed.  Recipient  1  was  awarded  GBP  5,176,518,  on 
14 November 2024,  and  Recipient  2  was  awarded  GBP  4,614,000  on  11  December  2024,  to  be 
drawn down through to 31 March 2027.

The  total  amount  of  grant  funding  awarded  during  2024  through  grant  offer  letters  was 
GBP 9,790,518, however during 2024, only GBP 607,609 was paid out.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme will run from 15 November 2024 through to 31 March 2027.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.17  HORIZON EUROPE GUARANTEE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Horizon Europe Guarantee.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 27 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Horizon  Europe  is  the  EU's  key  funding  programme  for  research  and  innovation  with  a  budget  of 
EUR 95.5billion  over  7  years.  It  tackles  climate  change,  helps  to  achieve  the  UN's  Sustainable 
Development Goals and boosts the EU's competitiveness and growth.

The programme facilitates collaboration and strengthens the impact of research and innovation in 
developing, supporting and implementing EU policies while tackling global challenges. It supports 
creating and better dispersing of excellent knowledge and technologies. It creates jobs, fully engages 
the EU's talent  pool, boosts  economic growth,  promotes industrial  competitiveness and optimises 
investment impact within a strengthened European Research Area.

The  UK  launched  its  Horizon  guarantee  in  November  2021  to  provide  funding  through  UKRI  for 
eligible calls in the absence of EC funding.

Desired Outcomes

Horizon  Europe  –  The  programme  aims  to  deliver  scientific  impacts  by  improving  research  and 
innovation capacity, scientific excellence and reputation and by integrating research and innovation 
efforts across sectors and industries.

The guarantee will deliver the following objectives:



Ensure that the UK R&D sector has access to funding that enables it to deliver on UK and 
BEIS (now DSIT) R&D priorities, such as 2.4% and unleashing innovation.

 Maintain UK industry and academic involvement and influence in important pan-European 

R&D networks and projects.

4.

1.1.18  INNOVATION TO COMMERCIALISATION OF UNIVERSITY RESEARCH (ICURE)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

ICURe (Innovation to Commercialisation of University Research).

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

ICURe  (Innovation  to  Commercialisation  of  University  Research)  aims  to  move  ideas  from  the 
academic base to the market, via a programme of customer validation. The programme consists of 
two elements. The first is phased training and support, delivered via university partners to upskill 
researchers  with  potential  to  commercialise  their  research  in  the  skills  required  to  achieve  this, 
including market evaluation, business and investment readiness. The second element is a competed 
grant award for the spin out companies that emerge from the programme and are assessed to have 
a viable commercialisation opportunity for their research.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018,

1.1.19  INVESTOR PARTNERSHIPS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Investor Partnerships.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Its  aim  is  to  stimulate  research  and  development  in  micro,  small  and  medium-sized  enterprises, 
while  accelerating  equity  investment  into  those  companies  so  that  they  can  grow  more  rapidly 
through innovation.

Investor partnerships are designed to encourage investors to invest in companies that they would 
otherwise have found too risky, by taking a significant amount of the technology risk via non-dilutive 
grant funding. It also addresses the failure that many early-stage grant recipients are insufficiently 
capitalized. Early engagement with investors not only provides aligned capital, but it also provides 
access to the investors' networks including further investment to enable the company to grow and 
scale.

In practice the grant funding can have a number of impacts on the investor's investment strategy 
including;  enabling  investors  to  consider  investing  early;  extending  the  level  of  R&D  that  their 
investment  can  support;  considering  investing  outside  their  standard  (safe)  geographic  area  or 
investing in technologies / markets that they would previously have considered too risky.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 30 -

of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid

1.1.20  LOW COST NUCLEAR PROGRAMME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Low Cost Nuclear Programme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

A  GBP  210m  direct  grant  to  Rolls  Royce  SMR  Ltd,  matched  by  GBP  250m  of  private  funding,  to 
develop their Small Modular Reactor design to pass Step 2 of the Office of Nuclear Regulation's (ONR) 
Generic Design Assessment (GDA) process.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  Government  has  given  a  grant  of  up  to  GBP  210  million  to  Rolls-Royce  SMR  Ltd  to  develop 
Small Modular  Reactors  in  the  UK.  In  April  2022,  the  nuclear  regulators  began  a  Generic  Design 
Assessment  (GDA)  looking  at  the  safety,  security,  and  environmental  impact  of  the  Rolls-Royce 
SMR Ltd reactor design, which is the first Small Modular Reactor design to be assessed in the UK. In 
April 2023,  the  nuclear  regulators  concluded  that  all  required  activities  for  Step  1  of  the 
Rolls-Royce Ltd GDA have been completed and Rolls Royce SMR Ltd progressed to Step 2 of 3 of the 
GDA. Step 2 was completed successfully in August 2024.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Direct Grant of GBP 210m to develop the Small Modular Reactor design administered by UKRI for 
DESNZ.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Rolls Royce SMR Ltd invoiced via UKRI acting as DESNZ agent, with UKRI monitoring the agreed 
project  plan  and  paying  invoices  at  milestone  points  agreed  to  by  all  parties.  Focus  was  on 
Rolls Royce SMR Ltd achieving GDA step 2 and being able to progress to step 3.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 31 -

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 89,875,336.77.
2024 - GBP 50,422,205.98.

GBP 210m over the whole scheme period, invoiced as milestones met.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2018 to 2025, the second phase started in November 2021 and finished March 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.21  MADE SMARTER INNOVATION

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Made Smarter Innovation.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Made  Smarter  review  published  in  2017  set  the  goal  that  'by  2030,  the  UK  will  be  a  global 
industrial leader in creating, adopting and exporting advanced digital technologies, shaping how the 
world does business'. The review outlined the significant impact that digital technologies could have 
on improving productivity and competitiveness of the full UK manufacturing sector and in addition 
grow the technology sector that enables the improvement.

The key digital technologies that can be harnessed to deliver the improvement were highlighted as:

1.
2.
3.
4.
5.

Artificial intelligence, machine learning and data analytics;
Additive manufacturing;
Robotics and Automation;
Virtual reality and augmented reality;
The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) and connectivity (5G, LPWAN etc.)

for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

1.1.22  MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING SECTOR SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Materials and Manufacturing Sector Scheme.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  purpose  of  the  subsidy  is  to  accelerate  growth  by  stimulating  and  supporting  business-led 
innovation, to encourage collaboration and effective co-operation between companies and research 
organisations, including higher education institutions, and to raise both the level and the quality of 
commercially relevant R&D&I activity.

The  Material  and  Manufacturing  sector  scheme  aims  to  improve  economic  growth  through 
sustainable increases in long-term productivity across the UK and internationally. The aim is to boost 
the  UK  manufacturing  supply  chains  through  innovation  in  areas  such  as  digital  manufacturing, 
additive manufacturing, composites, materials integration, and industrial biotechnology. The scheme 
also aims to help businesses gain access to the latest manufacturing and materials technologies.
