1.1.24  NUCLEAR FUEL FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Nuclear Fuel Fund (NFF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The NFF was established to support the UK's nuclear fuel supply chain to stimulate the development 
of  the  capabilities  needed  to  meet  current  and  future  fuel  demand.  The  NFF  was  designed  from 
recognition  of  the  need  to  maintain,  and  enhance,  the  UK's  strong  domestic  fuel  supply  chain  to 
support ambitions to increase civil nuclear deployment and protect our energy security.

Through a Request for Information process, the NFF identified specific categories of fuel capabilities 
which were considered as particular priority and considered to be the most strategically important 
to  the  UK's  future  nuclear  deployment  ambitions,  and  where  funding  was  expected  to  be  most 
impactful to remove market barriers:

Light Water Reactor (LWR) Fuel Supply


 High Assay Low Enriched Uranium (HALEU) Supply Chain



Advanced Modular Reactor (AMR) Fuel Fabrication Capabilities
Enabling UK Fuel Production Capabilities

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal basis for this scheme is Section 50 of the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020. The 
scheme ran from December 2022 to March 2025.

The Central Grant and Loans team within DESNZ is responsible, with support from the NFF project 
team, for administering the scheme.

5.

Form of the subsidy

A grant scheme with elements of match-funding.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

NFF  was  an  open  competition  offering  match  funding  to  promising  projects  which  support  the 
preservation  and  development  of  UK  fuel  production  capabilities.  The  strategic  aim  of  the 
Nuclear Fuel Fund is to support the UK fuel supply chain to develop the capabilities needed to meet 
fuel demand for current and future reactors in the UK and globally. The NFF seeks to support projects 
which will develop the front-end supply chain (excluding mining and milling) for relevant uranium 
and fuel products.

Grant payments are made quarterly, in arrears, throughout the lifetime of the project, in line with 
Schedules in the grant offer letter and terms and conditions.

The awarded grants are monitored monthly to assess progress and to ensure that they are delivering 
against the agreed scope and objectives for each project. Quarterly monitoring takes place to review 
the grant claim of each project; these are reviewed by grant experts who sample evidence provided 
by grant recipients and recommend payment of grant to DESNZ. Working groups, programme boards 
and  other  governance  structures  occur  routinely  to  monitor  risks,  resolve  issues  and  escalate  to 
NFF SRO when required.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2022/23: GBP 101,433.
2023/24: GBP 4,935,600.95.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 36 -

Total combined: GBP 5,037,033.95.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The NFF was active from December 2022 to March 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

greater capacity, and improved productivity across the UK through wider diffusion and adoption of

1.1.26  RD&I PROGRAMMES AND FELLOWSHIPS SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

UK Research & Innovation Research Councils – RD&I Programmes and Fellowships Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The UKRI Future Leaders Fellowships (FLF) scheme exists to provide longer term flexible funding to 
tackle  difficult  and  novel  challenges,  supporting  ambitious  programmes  of  work  built  on  research 
and/or innovation excellence with clear routes to impact. The scheme also invests in career support 
for the main applicant and their research, development and/or innovation team, to develop the UK's 
next  future  leaders.  This  scheme  funds  grants  in  all  regions  of  the  UK  across  all  sectors,  in  both 
industry and academia. The focus of this ASC form is on grants made to industry only.

Quality  and  high  impact  research  and  innovation  is  ultimately  about  people.  The  UK  has  a 
world-leading research and innovation base because we have developed and retained outstanding 
leaders.  Building  the  capacity  and  capability  of  our  workforce  is  vital  if  we  wish  to  meet  the 
Government's target to increase R&D investment in the economy to 2.4% of GDP by 2027.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  Higher  Education  and  Research  Act  2017  is  the  legal  authority  for  the  provision  of  these 
subsidies.

The scheme was created on 01 August 2019 whilst the UK negotiated its departure from the EU and 
prior to the enactment of the Subsidy Control Act 2022. This subsidy scheme is administered by the 
UK Research and Innovate Talent team.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Direct grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Recipient of the fellowship can be hosted by any size of business, including those with charitable 
objectives, Public Sector Research Establishments and Higher Education Institutions. Subsidies are 
awarded by competitive assessment against criteria (details can be found for each competition here 
Granting authority scheme details – UKRI). Successful applications are funded by direct grant with 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 38 -

payments made against a quarterly schedule (with the exception of those funded under round 7, 
see link).

For round 7 payments are made in arrears on the basis of actual costs incurred and reimbursed at 
the aid intensity fixed at the time of the award.

All projects are reviewed every quarter by a responsible monitoring officer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

GBP 12,906,045.20.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

01 August 2019 to 01 December 2028.

9.

1.1.28  SEED ENTERPRISE INVESTMENT SCHEME (SEIS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

The Seed Enterprise Investment Scheme (SEIS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The SEIS aims to help start-up and very early-stage companies to raise equity finance by offering 
tax  reliefs  to  individual  investors  who  purchase  new  shares  in  those  companies.  In  doing  so,  the 
scheme recognises the particular difficulties faced by these very early-stage companies in attracting 
investment from the market.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

HMRC is responsible for administering the scheme. The legal authority for the scheme is Part 5A of 
the Income Tax Act 2007, and Sections 150E, 150F and 150 and Schedule 5BB of the Taxation of 
Chargeable Gains Act 1992.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax relief.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 40 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  relief  is  provided  to  individual  investors  on  the  amounts  subscribed  in  new  shares  issued  by 
qualifying companies. The SEIS places qualifying conditions on the investor, the investment and the 
issuing company.

The shares must be issued in order to raise money for a qualifying business activity. There is also a 
requirement  that  the  issuing  company  must  either  exist  for  the  purpose  of  carrying  on  a  new 
qualifying trade or it must be the parent company of a group whose business is that of carrying on 
qualifying activities. A new qualifying trade is one which has not been carried on for longer than at 
the date of issue of the shares and where the company (and any qualifying subsidiaries) has not 
carried on any other trade before the company in question began to carry on the new trade. A trade 
is  not  a  qualifying  trade  where  it  consists  of  certain  activities  (or  if  such  activities  amount  to  a 
substantial part of the qualifying trade) which are excluded under Section 192(1) of the Income Tax 
Act  2007  (such  as  energy  generation,  farming  and  forestry  activities,  shipbuilding  and  steel 
production). A full list of excluded activities can be found in Section 192(1) of the Income Tax Act 
2007: https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2007/3/section/192.

To qualify for the relief, the company must be unquoted, have fewer than 25 employees and less 
than GBP 350,000 gross assets at the date of investment. The company can raise up to GBP 250,000 
in total. The investor must not to be an employee of the company or qualifying subsidiary unless 
they are a director or have a substantial interest in the issuing company.

The tax incentives available to qualifying individual investors are:





Income tax relief at 50% of the amount invested in new full-risk ordinary shares in target 
companies. The amount of the tax relief is set off against the investor's UK income tax 
charge on amounts invested of up to GBP 100,000 per year. The eligible shares must be 
held for at least 3 years.
Relief from capital gains tax on gains from shares that have qualified for income tax relief 
and which are disposed of at least 3 years after the investment.

 Where the investor has made a taxable capital gain on the disposal of any other asset, 
50%  of  the  gain  arising  on  amounts  of  up  to  GBP  100,000  are  exempt  from  tax  if 
reinvested in shares under the SEIS that also qualify for income tax relief
If the shares are disposed of at a loss, investors are able to elect that the amount of the 
loss, less any Income Tax relief given, can be set against income of the year in which the 
shares  were  disposed  of,  or  any  income  of  the  previous  year,  instead  of  being  set  off 
against any capital gains.



7.

Amount of the subsidy

Budget for tax year 2022/23: GBP 80m
Budget for tax year 2023/24: GBP 120m
Budget for tax year 2024/25: GBP 125m

Please note:




Values for 2023-24 and 2024-25 are forecasted costs of the SEIS tax relief
This includes the cost for the IT and CGT disposals relief
Figures are rounded to the nearest GBP 5m

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme commenced on 6 April 2012 and is ongoing.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 41 -

1.1.29  SKILLS,  EDUCATION,  MANUFACTURE  AND 
SEMICONDUCTORS

INNOVATION 

(SEMI)  FOR 

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Skills, Education, Manufacture, Innovation for Semiconductors

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The programme aims to stimulate growth in the UK economy by supporting companies to develop 
new products, processes and services based on emerging and enabling semiconductor technologies. 
This will help build a secure and resilient world, strengthening social and economic resilience and 
enhancing  national  security  across  virtual  and  physical  spaces.  Supporting  access  to  the  right 
collaborators, expertise and equipment will accelerate the potential of the UK semiconductor supply 
chain.

The  vision  for  this  semiconductors  program  is  to  ensure  that  the  UK  has  a  secure  and  relevant 
position  and  capability  within  the  global  semiconductor  landscape.  Aligned  to  supporting  the 
UK Government Innovation Strategy vision to make the UK a global hub for innovation by 2035. This 
programme  takes  a  holistic  approach  to  identify  and  address  common  problems  and  underlying 
issues  experienced  throughout  the  community  such  as:  supply  chain  resilience;  access  to 
manufacturing and test facilities; and skills shortages.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UK

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 457,846.12.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 42 -

2024 - GBP 4,336,293.19.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

09/12/2022 – 31/03/2025 .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.30  SIZEWELL C DEVELOPMENT EXPENDITURE (DEVEX) SUBSIDY SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Sizewell C Development Expenditure Subsidy Scheme ("the Devex Scheme").

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Sizewell  C  ("SZC")  is  a  proposed  two-unit  3.2-Gigawatt  nuclear  power  station.  If  built,  it  would 
produce  enough  electricity  to  power  6  million  homes  and  would  be  a  key  contributor  to  the  UK's 
Net Zero targets.

The Devex Scheme is to provide support for developmental expenditure to enable the SZC project 
to reach final investment decision. This supports HMG's objectives to deliver generation of electricity 
that supports system stability, ensures security of supply, and supports delivery of Net Zero.

The  Devex  Scheme  is  designed  to  fund  development,  and  some  initial  construction  works 
expenditure to continue preparatory works and build up their supply chain and workforce ahead of 
HMG's final investment decision.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The subsidy scheme was designed, assessed and created under Section 50 of the United Kingdom 
Internal Market Act 2020 and in accordance with the requirements of the Subsidy Control Act 2022.

The  scheme  was  made  on  the  30  Aug  2024  and  since  then  there  have  been  no  changes  to  the 
scheme design.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (formerly the Department for Business, Energy 
and Industrial Strategy, BEIS) is responsible for the Devex subsidy scheme with the Secretary of 
State for DESNZ being the HMG Shareholder in the SZC project.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Support granted under the Devex Scheme will mainly be comprised of equity injections, in the form 
of equity investment and shareholder loans, by the UK Government. However, the Devex Scheme 
also provides for additional support through other mechanisms where appropriate and proportionate, 
such as (i) letters of credit, (ii) indemnities and (iii) guarantees.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The main beneficiary of the Devex Scheme will be Sizewell C Limited (SZC GenCo), although other 
specified  categories  of  beneficiaries  may  benefit  from  the  Devex  Scheme,  including  for  example 
owners of land subject to a Compulsory Purchase Order for the purposes of the SZC project, or EDF 
Energy Nuclear Generation Ltd, the owner/operator of Sizewell B nuclear power plant, to the extent 
it is eligible to be compensated in respect of any works at the SZC site pre-FID that adversely impact 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 43 -

Sizewell  B's  ability  to  generate  electricity.  To  note,  currently  only  SZC  has  been  the  recipient  of 
subsidy.

For  equity  and  shareholder  loans,  subsidy  is  provided  to  the  project  by  a  Written  Shareholder 
Resolution which creates an obligation for HMG, as shareholder, to make available to the project an 
amount  of  funding  ("Shareholder  Funding  Cap").  The  project  then  makes  cash  calls  periodically 
throughout the financial year, matching these calls to the agreed Company Plan & Budget, an annual 
plan approved by the Shareholders.

For credit support instruments, such as guarantees, the financial assistance will be provided in the 
form of a government contingent liability provided to the relevant beneficiary.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Sizewell  C  is  currently  the  only  beneficiary  of  the  scheme  and  has  currently  been  awarded 
GBP 3,900,000,000 of subsidy.

The scheme budget is GBP 5,500,000,000.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The start and end dates of the scheme are 23 Aug 2024 – 30 Jun 2026.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.31  SIZEWELL C INVESTMENT FUNDING SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Sizewell C Investment Funding Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Sizewell  C  ("SZC")  is  a  proposed  two-unit  3.2  Gigawatt  nuclear  power  station.  If  built,  it  would 
produce  enough  electricity  to  power  6  million  homes  and  would  be  a  key  contributor  to  the  UK's 
Net Zero targets.

The SZC Investment Funding Scheme has the purpose of funding the SZC's continuing development 
so it can reach a final investment decision. This supports HMG's objectives to deliver generation of 
electricity  that  supports  system  stability,  ensures  security  of  supply,  and  supports  delivery  of 
Net Zero.

The  scheme  allows  HMG  to  provide  equity  funding  to  the  project  to:  (a)  strengthen  delivery 
confidence through further maturation of non-replicated design aspects, and fund early contractor 
involvement for enabling works to ensure timely realisation of the current schedule; and (b) further 
enhance preparations to enable construction to start and to support plans to launch a capital raise 
process.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The subsidy scheme was designed, assessed and created under the requirements of the EU-UK Trade 
and Cooperation Agreement. Legacy scheme modifications are made using section 48(1)(a) of the 
Subsidy Control Act 2022.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 44 -

The scheme was created on the 29 Nov 2022 and is classified as a legacy scheme for the purposes 
of section 48(1)(a) of the Subsidy Control Act 2022. The scheme budget has been increased by way 
of  permitted  legacy  subsidy  modifications  as  shown  below  to  allow  for  continued  support  for  the 
project to continue the necessary development stages towards a final investment decision:






on 26 May 2023 from GBP 678,542,429 to GBP 753,542,429;
on 7 July 2023 from GBP 753,542,429 to GBP 848,142,429.
on 29 August 2023 from GBP 848,142,429 to GBP 1,189,542,429
on 22 January from GBP 1,189,542,429 to GBP 2,489,542,429

The scheme has been subsequently replaced with the Sizewell C Development Expenditure Subsidy 
Scheme on the 30 Aug 2024.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (formerly the Department for Business, Energy 
and  Industrial  Strategy,  BEIS)  is  responsible  for  the  SZC  subsidy  scheme,  with  the  Secretary  of 
State for DESNZ being the HMG Shareholder in the SZC project.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The subsidy is provided to the project in the form of equity investment and shareholder loans, both 
of which are governed under the Interim Shareholder Agreement between the investors (HMG, EDF) 
and the project.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Sizewell C is the only beneficiary of the scheme.

The  subsidy  is  provided  to  the  project  by  a  Written  Shareholder  Resolution  which  creates  an 
obligation  for  HMG,  as  shareholder,  to  make  available  to  the  project  an  amount  of  funding 
("Shareholder Funding Cap"). The project then makes cash calls periodically throughout the financial 
year, matching these calls to the agreed Company Plan & Budget, an annual plan approved by the 
Shareholders.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Sizewell C is the only beneficiary of the scheme and has received the maximum amount of subsidy 
available under the scheme - GBP 2,489,542,429.

The scheme budget is GBP 2,489,542,429.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The start and end dates of the scheme are 29 November 2022 – 11 June 2024.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.1.32  SMART GRANTS PROGRAMME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Smart Grants Programme.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 45 -

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Smart  Grants  programme  is  designed  to  bring  original,  high-impact  innovations  into  the 
marketplace. The innovation must have a solid strategy to generate considerable economic benefits 
for  the  UK.  The  grants  are  available  to  all  industry  and  technology  areas  from  science  and 
engineering  to  the  creative  industries.  Open  to  the  best  game-changing  and  commercially  viable 
innovative or disruptive ideas. The purpose of the Smart grant funding initiative is to target support 
for  UK  businesses  by  providing  crucial  support  to  projects  from  businesses  of  all  sizes  especially 
those  with  significant  ambition  and  potential  for  growth,  to  develop  highly  innovative  products, 
processes  or  services  with  considerable  potential  to  result  in  successful  commercialisation.  The 
programme  does  this  by  both  reducing  the  financial  risk  to  companies,  and  addressing  a  funding 
gap  that  many  businesses  experience,  in  particular  Small  or  Medium  Enterprises,  to  overcome 
barriers to innovation and market access, as well as to grow and scale up.

Smart provides Innovation Research & Development funding to support projects in the space after 
basic (fundamental) research, but before commercial investment steps in to take a product, process 
or  service  into  the  market  and  be  fully  commercialised.  This  will  provide  more  opportunities  for 
highly  innovative  businesses  across  the  UK  to  apply  for  funding  at  a  time  more  suitable  for  their 
business  and  innovation  development.  Through  this  funding,  we  will  support  hundreds  of 
high-growth  businesses,  collaborations  and  industries  to  innovate  and  compete  in  future  global 
markets.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 97,236,030.97.
2024 - GBP 60,384,562.55.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

15/11/2022 – 15/11/2028 .

1.1.33  SUPPORTING GLOBAL INNOVATION

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Supporting Global Innovation.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Global  Business  Innovation  Programmes  help  high-growth  innovative  UK  businesses  explore  and 
exploit the collaboration, growth and innovation opportunities offered through working globally.

Help  UK  businesses  to  achieve  global  success  through  growth  and  scaling  by  increasing  their 
capacity,  capability,  and  aspiration  for  global  innovation  through  a  programme  of  support  that 
creates new knowledge, skills, IP, products services,  and processes, leading to increased exports 
and inward investment.

The objectives of the Route are to:








Facilitate increased public investment in research, unlocking new discoveries and applying 
research to solving our most pressing problems in government, industry and wider society.
Encourage increased private sector RD&I investment and spend.
Encourage  RD&I  business  growth  and  ensure  an  accessible  funding  landscape  for 
commercialisation.
Encourage  collaborative  RD&I,  especially  strategic  collaborations  between  universities, 
businesses and charities.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research

Grant.

1.1.34  UKRI COVID RESPONSE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

COVID Response.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

In response to the Global pandemic subsidies were offered to accelerate growth by stimulating and 
supporting business-led innovation, to encourage collaboration and effective co-operation between 
companies and research organisations, including higher education institutions, and to raise both the 
level and the quality of commercially relevant R&D&I activity.

The support identifies and accelerates the development of technologies and capabilities that lead to 
new  products  in  major  new  industries,  as  well  as  supports  the  improvement  of  productivity  and 
growth in existing areas.

The support finds and proves early-stage technologies, often from the research base; underpinning 
technologies that have the potential to disrupt a broad swathe of products across many sectors.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate  UK,  the  UK's  Innovation  Agency,  is  part  of  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which 
brings together seven disciplinary research councils, and Research England. Formed on 1 April 2018, 
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department 
for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT).

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

Form of the subsidy

1.1.35  VENTURE CAPITAL TRUST SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Venture Capital Trust (VCT) Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  aim  of  the  Venture  Capital  Trust  (VCT)  scheme  is  to  support  the  growth  of  certain  smaller, 
unquoted trading companies which due to their early development stage, would otherwise struggle 
to have access to finance due to an insufficient track record and/or poor collaterals.

For  this  purpose,  income  and  capital  gains  tax  incentives  are  provided  under  the  scheme  to 
individuals who subscribe for new shares, or acquire shares on the secondary market, in authorised 
financial intermediary companies – VCTs - which are then required to carry out eligible investments 
into the target companies.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal authority for the scheme is Part 6 of the Income Tax Act 2007;Sections 100(1) and 151A 
of  the  Taxation  of  Chargeable  Gains  Act  1992;  and  Sections  709  -  712  of  the  Income  Tax 
(Trading and Other Income) Act 2005.

The scheme is administered by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax relief (to intermediary VCT and individual investors).

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 49 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

To be authorised as a VCT, a company must be listed on a recognised stock exchange and sufficiently 
demonstrate for the most recent complete accounting period it meets a number of conditions relating 
to investments raised and deployed to ensure the targeted companies of the scheme receive the 
necessary funding:













its income was derived wholly or mainly from shares or securities;
it did not retain more than 15% of its income from shares and securities;
it  has  no  holding  in  any  company  that  represents  more  than  15%  by  value  of  its 
investments;
at least 80% by value of its investments were in qualifying holdings of shares or securities;
at least 70% by value of its qualifying holdings were in eligible shares;
it has not made, and will not make in future, an investment which is neither an investment 
included in the company's qualifying holdings or an investment within the non-qualifying 
investments condition;
it has not made, and will not make in future, an investment in a company which breaches 
the maximum age condition (the company must receive this VCT investment within 7 years 
of its first commercial sale / 10 years if knowledge intensive) or exceeds the permitted 
investment limit (GBP 5,000,000 annually / GBP 10,000,000 if knowledge intensive and a 
maximum of GBP 12,000,000 or GBP 20,000,000 if knowledge intensive);
it has not made, and will not make in future, an investment in a company which breaches 
the prohibition on business acquisitions.

To  be  'qualifying  holdings',  the  investments  made  by  the  VCT  must  be  in  companies  that  meet 
conditions in respect of their structure, activity and use of the funds received. These conditions also 
restrict the types of activities the company can undertake, with "excluded activities" including energy 
generation, farming and forestry activities, shipbuilding and steel production.

A  VCT  is  required  to  provide  annual  returns  to  HMRC  of  both  the  investments  it  receives  from 
individuals  and  those  investments  it  makes  in  companies,  demonstrating  that  the  qualifying 
conditions of the scheme have been satisfied for that year.

The scheme provides the following tax reliefs for VCTs and their investors:







Exemption for VCT's from Corporation Tax on chargeable gains (and losses for chargeable 
gains purposes are not allowable losses).
individual investors in VCTs can claim 'front-end' income tax relief on subscriptions of up 
to GBP 200,000 each year where these shares are held for a minimum period of 5 years;
Individual investors are exempt from Capital Gains Tax on the disposal of ordinary shares 
and  also  income  tax  on  any  dividends  received  on  ordinary  shares  acquired  within  the 
'permitted maximum' of GBP 200,000.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Budget for tax year 2022/23: GBP 430m
Budget for tax year 2023/24: GBP 460m
Budget for tax year 2024/25: GBP 480m

Please note:




Values for 2023-24 and 2024-25 are forecasted costs of the VCT tax relief
This includes the cost for the IT, IT Dividend Exemption and CGT disposals relief
Figures are rounded to the nearest GBP 5m

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2017 - 5 April 2035.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

- 50 -

1.2  ENGLAND

1.2.1  ENGLISH FREEPORTS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

English Freeports Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The policy objective of the Freeports Subsidy Scheme is to promote investment and job creation in 
port regions that have underperformed economically.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme was created under the following legislation:













Sections 1 to 4 & Section 8 National Insurance Act 2022 (Zero rate of secondary National 
Insurance contributions for Investment Zone employees).
Section 61A and Schedule 6C to the Finance Act 2003 (Stamp Duty Land Tax relief).
Sections  270BNA  to  270BNC  Capital  Allowances  Act  2001  (Enhanced  structures  and 
buildings allowances).
Sections 45O to 45R Capital Allowances Act 2001 (First year allowances for expenditure 
on plant and machinery).
Section 47 Local Government Finance Act 1988 (Business rates relief).
Either: a) Section 1 of the Localism Act 2011 for local authorities; or b) Section 113D of 
the  Local  Democracy,  Economic  Development  and  Construction  Act  2009  for  combined 
authorities, where a Section 113D order exists; or Section 113A of the Local Democracy, 
Economic  Development  and  Construction  Act  2009  for  combined  authorities,  where  no 
Section 113D order exists (seed capital).
Section 113 Finance Act 2021 (gives HM Treasury powers through secondary legislation 
to designate tax sites).

5.

Form of the subsidy

The Scheme provides subsidies via grants and tax relief.

The Scheme covers 6 different support measures, listed below.

The  specific  eligibility  terms  and  conditions  for  each  support  measure  can  be  found  at: 
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/english-freeports-subsidy-scheme

1. A  zero-rate  of  secondary  Class  1  National  Insurance  contributions  (NICs)  is  available  on 
earnings up to the upper secondary threshold (GBP 25,000) for qualifying employments in 
designated special tax sites.

2. Stamp  Duty  Land  Tax  relief:  Full  Stamp  Duty  Land  Tax  relief  is  available  for  land  and 

buildings situated in a special tax site, provided they are used in a qualifying manner.

3. Enhanced  Structures  and  Buildings  Allowances:  Enhanced  Structures  and  Buildings 
Allowances  are  accelerated  tax  allowances  for  qualifying  expenditure  on  non-residential 
structures and buildings situated in a special tax site. The allowances are available at 10% 
of the expenditure per year for 10 years.

4. Enhanced  Capital  Allowances:  Enhanced  Capital  Allowances  are  100%  first-year  tax 
allowances for qualifying expenditure on plant or machinery primarily for use in a special tax 
site.

5. Business Rates Relief: Relief up to 100% from business rates on certain business premises 
is available within Freeport tax sites. The relief applies for 5 years from the point at which 
relief is first claimed.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 51 -

6. Seed  Capital  funding:  The  8  Freeports  in  England  have  been  granted  access  to  an  equal 
share  of  GBP  200  million  of  seed  capital.  The  purpose  of  the  seed  capital  is  to  address 
infrastructure  gaps  in  Freeport  locations  and  create  the  right  physical  conditions  to 
encourage private sector investment.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The award of subsidies under the Scheme is limited to activity undertaken in the 8 existing Freeport 
locations in England:

 With regards to measures 1-5 above, these are available only in each of the 24 designated 

'special tax sites'

 With regards to measure 6 (seed funding), this can be used within the 'Outer Boundary' 



of any of the 8 English Freeports', or within the wider travel to work area.
The  8  Freeport  locations  are  East  Midlands,  Felixstowe  and  Harwich  (Freeport  East), 
Humber, Liverpool City Region, Plymouth & South Devon, Solent, Teesside and Thames.

 Maps 

of 

tax 

sites 

and 

outer 

boundaries 

can 

be 

found 

here: 

https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/maps-of-uk-freeports

Purchasers of land and buildings situated in special tax sites, provided they are used in a qualifying 
manner. Stamp Duty Land Tax relief is provided as a full or partial reduction from tax that would 
otherwise be payable on the purchase of qualifying land and buildings.

Enhanced  Structures  and  Buildings  Allowances  and  Enhanced  Capital  Allowances  for  plant  and 
machinery are available for qualifying investment incurred in designated tax sites and operate by 
reducing the profits chargeable to tax at an accelerated rate compared to other capital allowances.

The zero-rate of secondary Class 1 NICs relief up to the upper secondary threshold is available to 
employers for qualifying employments in designated special tax sites. The relief can be claimed by 
eligible  employers  when  they  apply  the  relevant  National  Insurance  contributions  category  letter 
when running payroll.

Business Rates Relief is available to new businesses moving into a freeport tax site after the site has 
been designated. It can apply to both new and existing buildings.

Seed capital funding subsidies can be made to eligible projects undertaken within a Freeport. The 
funding can be by way of grant, loan or other forms as agreed in the original Freeport business case.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Total of reliefs in GBP millions rounded: 17.1

1.2.2  ENGLISH INVESTMENT ZONES

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

English Investment Zones Subsidy Scheme ("the Scheme").

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The policy objective of the Scheme is to provide the market inputs required to catalyse investment 
and create jobs in a small number of high potential areas that have underperformed economically 
in the past, resulting in worse social outcomes. The aim is to boost productivity and growth in key 
sectors, ultimately addressing inequality in regional outcomes.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme was created on 26 April 2024 under the following legislation:











Sections 1 to 4 & Section 8 National Insurance Act 2022 (Zero rate of secondary National 
Insurance contributions for Investment Zone employees).
Section 61A and Schedule 6C to the Finance Act 2003 (Stamp Duty Land Tax relief).
Sections  270BNA  to  270BNC  Capital  Allowances  Act  2001  (Enhanced  structures  and 
buildings allowances).
Sections 45O to 45R Capital Allowances Act 2001 (First year allowances for expenditure 
on plant and machinery).
Section 47 Local Government Finance Act 1988 (Business rates relief).
Section 113D of the Local Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009 
for combined authorities, where a Section 113D order exists; or Section 113A of the Local 
Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009 for combined authorities, 
where no Section 113D order exists (grant funding).

MHCLG oversee the grant funding element, HMRC oversee the tax reliefs.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants and tax reliefs.

The Scheme covers 6 different support measures:

1.

2.

3.

4.

Stamp Duty Land Tax relief: Full Stamp Duty Land Tax relief is available for land and 
buildings situated in a special tax site, provided they are used in a qualifying manner.
Enhanced  Structures  and  Buildings  Allowances:  Enhanced  Structures  and  Buildings 
Allowances are accelerated tax allowances for qualifying expenditure on non-residential 
structures and buildings situated in a special tax site. The allowances are available at 
10% of the expenditure per year for 10 years.
Enhanced  Capital  Allowances:  Enhanced  Capital  Allowances  are  100%  first-year  tax 
allowances for qualifying expenditure on plant or machinery primarily for use in a special 
tax site.
A zero-rate of secondary Class 1 National Insurance contributions (NICs) is available on 
earnings up to the upper secondary threshold (GBP 25,000) for qualifying employments 
in designated special tax sites.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 53 -

5.

6.

Business  rates  relief:  Relief  up  to  100%  from  business  rates  on  certain  business 
premises is available within Freeport tax sites. The relief applies for 5 years from the 
point at which relief is first claimed.
Grant  funding  for  local  infrastructure,  research  and  innovation,  skills,  and  business 
support.

Grants  can  be  awarded  to  subsidise  activity  under  4  investment  categories:  local  infrastructure, 
research and innovation, skills and business support.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The Scheme enables relevant public authorities to provide targeted subsidies in English Investment 
Zone areas in the form of tax reliefs or grant funding to help achieve the policy objectives. The eight 
Investment Zones in England each benefit from GBP 160 million over ten years that can be used 
flexibly between grant and tax reliefs.

The award of subsidies under the Scheme is limited to activity undertaken in the 8 Investment Zone 
locations in England.

With regards to measures 1-5 above, these are available only on designated Investment Zone 'tax 
sites' 
(https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/maps-of-investment-zones-and-investment-
zone-tax-sites). These tax sites are underdeveloped areas within the Investment Zone.

With regards to measure 6 (grant funding), this can be used within the English Investment Zone 
areas.  The  8  Investment  Zone  locations  are  East  Midlands,  Greater  Manchester,  Liverpool  City 
Region, North East, South Yorkshire, West Midlands, West Yorkshire, and Tees Valley

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The  total  budget  for  the  Investment  Zones  programme  from  commencement  to  the  end  of  the 
scheme in 2035 is GBP 1,280,000,000. The total cost of subsidies paid out under the scheme during 
the reporting period was GBP 3m.

For the information cited in items 3 to 7 above, the notification does not necessarily have to have 
an independent heading corresponding to each item and may provide information on multiple items 
in one heading (e.g., provide information on items 3 and 4 under one heading). In this case, the 
notification must clearly specify what items are covered by which heading.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

26 April 2024 to 05 April 2035.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.3  SCOTLAND

1.3.1  EUROPEAN STRUCTURAL AND INVESTMENT FUNDS (ESIF) SMART GROWTH SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

European Structural and Investment Funds (ESIF) Smart Growth Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 54 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy











Increase  business  commercialisation  and  investment  in  RTDI,  particularly  in  sectors 
identified through Smart Specialisation
Enabling  individuals  and  businesses  throughout  Scotland  to  access  digital  services  and 
business opportunities
Support  SME  internationalisation  as  a  route  to  growth  and  increasing  employment, 
particularly in Smart Specialisation sectors and regionally important sectors in fragile areas
Support key growth sectors by promoting graduate and post-graduate skills relevant to 
those growth sectors and developing new curricula to meet their needs
Support for key growth sectors by promoting relevant vocational skills at ISCED level 3 
and above
Increase the number of employers who offer vocational placements and opportunities


 Decrease the number of young unemployed who are NEET in SW Scotland


Reduce the number of young people not in employment, education or training (NEET) who 
are inactive and socially excluded in SW Scotland
Investing in education, training and vocational training for skills and lifelong learning



4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  legal  basis  for  the  scheme  is  the  Scotland  Act  1998  and  is  administered  by  the 
Scottish Government.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The  assistance  takes  the  form  of  direct  grant/  Interest  rate  subsidy,  Loan/  Repayable  advances, 
Provision of risk finance.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  intervention  involves  the  use  of  public  funds.  In  most  instances  funds  will  be  disbursed  to 
organisations/undertakings involved in economic activity. That is, those that are either engaged in 
providing goods or services to a market, or the support being provided is going to an organisation 
that does.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023: GBP 1,009,851.80.
2024: GBP 379,716.00.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme ran from 8 September 2015 until 31 December 2023.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.3.2  GREEN FREEPORTS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Green Freeports.

2.

Period covered by the notification

May 2 2024 [i.e. Scheme launch date] to May 1 2025.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 55 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Scottish  Green  Freeports  Subsidy  Scheme covers  a  blend  of  measures,  enabling  public 
authorities to provide targeted support to promote economic activity in Green Freeport locations in 
Scotland. The Scheme encompasses 6 different support measures:

1.

2.
3.
4.
5.
6.

Zero  rate  of  secondary  National  Insurance  contributions  for  eligible  Green  Freeport 
employees
Land and Buildings Transactions Tax Relief
Enhanced Structures and Buildings Allowances
Enhanced Capital Allowances
Non-Domestic Rates Relief
Seed Funding.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

This  scheme  has  been  developed  jointly  by  the  Minister  for  Housing  Communities  and  Local 
Government  (MHCLG),  Scottish  Government  (SG),  Revenue  Scotland,  HMT  Treasury  (HMT),  and 
HM Revenue  and  Customs  (HMRC)  as  the  public  authorities  with  the  legal  powers  required  to 
administer this scheme. The legal basis for administering this scheme is as follows:



Land  and  Buildings  Transaction  Tax  –  The  Land  and  Buildings  Transaction  Tax  (Green 
Freeports Relief) (Scotland) Order 2023.

 Non-domestic rates relief – Section 3A of the Local Government (Financial Provisions etc.) 











(Scotland) Act 1962.
First  year  capital  allowance  on  plant  and  machinery  –  Sections  45O  to  45R  Capital 
Allowances Act 2001.
Zero rate of secondary National Insurance contributions for employees – Sections 1-4 and 
section 8 National Insurance Act 2022.
Enhanced  structures  and  buildings  allowances  –  Sections  270BNA-270BNC  Capital 
Allowances Act 2001.
Seed  funding  –  Section  126  of  the  Housing  Grants,  Regeneration  and  Construction 
Act 1996.
Section  113  of  the  Finance  Act  2021  gives  HM  Treasury  powers  through  secondary 
legislation to designate tax sites.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant and tax incentives.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The subsidies are aimed at incentivising private sector to invest in land for development, invest in 
commercial  buildings,  invest  in  plant  and  machinery,  lower  operating  costs,  and  support  new 
employment.  Any  business  locating  within  a  Green  Freeport  tax  site,  providing  it  meets  the  local 
gateway tests (administered by the Green Freeport), can access the tax reliefs.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

From 2 May 2024 to 1 May 2025, the total amount of Green Freeport subsidy awarded to date [as 
relates to devolved tax reliefs and seed capital funding] is GBP 1,770,000.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme became active of 2 May 2024 and is intended to be active until 30 September 2034.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

1.3.3  HIGHLANDS AND ISLANDS ENTERPRISE (HIE) ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT SUBSIDY 
SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Highlands and Islands Enterprise (HIE) Economic Development Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  objective  of  the  scheme  is  to  assist  firms  to  increase  their  contribution  to  employment  and 
Gross Value Added in the economy by investing in business growth.

The  scheme  applies  to  a  variety  of  sectors  such  as  energy,  life  sciences,  creative  industries, 
sustainable  tourism,  financial  and  business  services,  universities.  Principally  it  provides  direct 
support to businesses and social enterprises, specialist programmes, and interventions to create the 
conditions for strong sectoral growth.

The  scheme  is  comprehensive  in  cover  but  is  intended  to  be  applied  in  a  highly  focussed  and 
discretionary way at the level of individual proposals.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Highlands and Islands Enterprise (HIE), which administers the scheme, was established to promote 
the economic and social development of the HIE area, to enhance employment and business skills, 
improve industrial efficiency, international competitiveness and the environment to achieve these.

The legal basis for the scheme is the Enterprise and New Town (Scotland) Act 1990, as amended on 
1 April 2001 by the Scottish Statutory Instrument 2001 No 126.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants, interest subsidy, loans.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Support under the scheme is provided following the appraisal of a business plan. Some measures 
are  only  provided  to  small  and  medium  sized  enterprises  (SMEs),  while  some  measures  provide 
support to both large and small firms, usually at higher grant rates for SMEs than for larger firms. 
To be eligible for the scheme, applicants must be undertaking new development in the qualifying 
area (the Highlands and Islands region of Scotland), or elsewhere which will facilitate HIE's functions.

Support is offered to the minimum extent necessary for the project to proceed, must contribute to 
the  regional  development  policies  and  adhere  to  UK  domestic  subsidy  procedures.  It  is  never 
allocated on the basis of product prices, product costs or the quantities produced or exported.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023: GBP 2,339,263.42.
2024: GBP 4,595,587.42.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

01 January 2021 - 31 December 2027.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 57 -

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.3.4  HIGHLANDS AND ISLANDS ENTERPRISE (HIE) SUMITOMO STANDALONE AWARD

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Standalone award to Sumitomo Electric U.K. Power Cables Ltd.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The primary policy objective is:



to  support  the  supply  chain  for  the  UK's  renewable  energy  generation  and  distribution 
requirements, which then contributes to HMG's and SG's net zero targets and also provides 
a strategic security of supply for a key part of the supply chain, namely HVDC cables which 
are not presently manufactured at scale in the UK.

and in addition:





to support HIE's regional objectives of securing high quality jobs for our area and the just 
transition for redeployment from the carbon-based economy; and
to encourage further investment in the Inverness and Cromarty Firth Green Freeport by 
contributing to building a renewable energy supply chain cluster.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Highlands and Islands Enterprise (HIE), which granted the award, was established to promote the 
economic  and  social  development  of  the  HIE  area,  to  enhance  employment  and  business  skills, 
improve industrial efficiency, international competitiveness and the environment to achieve these.

The legal basis for the award is the Enterprise and New Town (Scotland) Act 1990, as amended on 
1 April 2001 by the Scottish Statutory Instrument 2001 No 126.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Sumitomo Electric Industries U.K. Power Cables Ltd.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023: GBP 0
2024: GBP 1,409,021

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Grant duration 20/02/24 to 31/03/2026 .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.3.5  SCOTTISH ENTERPRISE REGIONAL AND SMALL TO MEDIUM ENTERPRISE SUBSIDY 
SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Scottish Enterprise Regional and SME Investment Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objectives of the Scheme are:






to encourage companies to invest in projects which create or safeguard jobs in Scotland;
to  support  the  development  of  premises  and  buildings  for  commercial  purposes  by  the 
private sector in Scotland where there is a market failure; and
to promote the development of assisted areas in Scotland, as set out in the UK Regional 
Aid Map 2014 – 2020.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

This  scheme  is  administered  by  Scottish  Enterprise.  Scottish  Enterprise  is  established  under  the 
Enterprise and New Towns (Scotland) Act 1990 and provides support to enterprises across Scotland 
under the provisions of that Act.

The Scheme previously operated under:







Article  366  of  Title  XI  of  the  UK-EU  Trade  and  Cooperation  Agreement  signed  on 
30 December  2020  -as  implemented  by  section  29  of  the  European  Union  (Future 
Relationship) Act 2020;
Article 10 of the Northern Ireland Protocol to the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement agreed on 
17 October 2019 in respect of EU Structural Funds (as implemented by section 7A of the 
European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018); and
Article 138 of the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement agreed on 17 October 2019 in respect of 
EU Structural Funds (as implemented by section 7A of the European Union (Withdrawal) 
Act 2018).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Principally grant contribution.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The scheme can be awarded to enterprises of all sizes. Applicants must submit an application for 
assistance  to  Scottish  Enterprise  before  work  on  the  project  or  activity  has  started,  and  the 
application must be approved in writing before work can commence on the project.

Eligibility  and  amount  of  support  provided  towards  eligible  costs  varies  depending  on  the  type  of 
activity.

Subsidy is typically provided through a grant contribution.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The total amount budgeted for the scheme is GBP 80m.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Scheme runs from 23 December 2022 to 31 December 2025.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 59 -

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

1.3.6  SCOTTISH ENTERPRISE SMALL TO MEDIUM ENTERPRISE AID SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Scottish Enterprise Small or Medium Enterprise Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  objective  of  the  scheme  is  to  encourage  development  and  growth  of  businesses  in  Scotland 
through increasing competitiveness, efficiency and exploitation of commercial potential.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

This  scheme  is  administered  by  Scottish  Enterprise.  Scottish  Enterprise  is  established  under  the 
Enterprise and New Towns (Scotland) Act 1990 and provides support to enterprises across Scotland 
under the provisions of that Act.

The Scheme previously operated under:







Article  366  of  Title  XI  of  the  UK-EU  Trade  and  Cooperation  Agreement  signed  on 
30 December  2020  -as  implemented  by  section  29  of  the  European  Union  (Future 
Relationship) Act 2020;
Article 10 of the Northern Ireland Protocol to the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement agreed on 
17 October 2019 in respect of EU Structural Funds (as implemented by section 7A of the 
European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018); and
Article 138 of the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement agreed on 17 October 2019 in respect of 
EU Structural Funds (as implemented by section 7A of the European Union (Withdrawal) 
Act 2018).

The scheme is now classed as and operates as a "legacy scheme" for the purposes of the UK Subsidy 
Control Act 2022.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Principally grant contribution.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

In general, financial assistance will only be provided to Small and Medium sized Enterprises (SME's). 
Support to large enterprises under this Scheme is only permitted for environmental studies.

Applicants  must  submit  an  application  for  assistance  to  Scottish  Enterprise  before  work  on  the 
project  or  activity  has  started,  and  the  application  must  be  approved  in  writing  before  work  can 
commence on the project.

Eligibility  and  amount  of  support  provided  towards  eligible  costs  varies  depending  on  the  type  of 
activity.

Subsidy is typically provided through a grant contribution.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The total amount budgeted for the scheme is GBP 20m.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 60 -

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Scheme runs from 23 December 2022 to 31 December 2025.

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

South of Scotland Enterprise (SOSE) Economic Development Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

This  scheme  aims  to  provide  support  to  businesses,  individuals  and  communities  in  the  South  of 
Scotland  area  (being  the  areas  of:  (a)  Dumfries  and  Galloway  Council;  and  (b)  Scottish  Borders 
Council).

Support under this scheme includes:















Regional aid;
Support to Small and Medium sized enterprises (SMEs), such as investment and operating 
aid, and access to finance;
Support for research and development and innovation (R&D&I)
Support for disadvantaged workers and for workers with disabilities
Support for environmental protection
Support for broadband infrastructures
Support for culture and heritage conservation
Support for audio-visual works
Support for sport and multifunctional recreational infrastructures
Support for local infrastructures
Support for regional airports and ports
Training aid

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Scheme is provided under the legislative authority of South of Scotland Enterprise Act 2019. 
Scheme is implemented by South of Scotland Enterprise (SOSE). SOSE, launched in April 2020, is 
the  economic  and  community  development  agency  responsible  for  Dumfries  &  Galloway  and  the 
Scottish Borders.

5.

Grants, loans, equity investments, guarantees.

Scheme is available in principle to all enterprises. Eligibility is subject to certain specific restrictions 
(Agriculture,  Horticulture  and  Forestry  sectors;  fishing  and  aquaculture  sectors;  and  additional 
restrictions  and  conditions  provided  for  under  the  UK's  domestic  subsidy  regime  and  the 
UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA).) and location within the South of Scotland region.

Amount of the subsidy

1.4  NORTHERN IRELAND

1.4.1  GRANT FOR RESEARCH, DEVELOPMENT AND INNOVATION

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Grant for Research, Development and Innovation.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy









To  support  research,  development  and  innovation  (R&D&I)  activities  undertaken  within 
Northern Ireland companies.
To  encourage  collaboration  and  effective  co-operation  between  companies  and  higher 
education  institutions  including  research  organizations  to  raise  both  the  level  and  the 
quality of commercially relevant R&D activity.
To stimulate R&D and innovation and encourage best practice throughout business, foster 
the creation and development of innovative business starts, help firms develop innovative 
products, processes and internationally tradable services with commercial potential, boost 
the scale and relevancy of R&D&I in Northern Ireland through increased business as well 
as higher education expenditure in R&D.
The  objectives  of  the  scheme  are  also  focused  on  the  increase  both  in  the  number  of 
companies  performing  effective  R&D&I  and  the  number  of  companies  participating  in 
R&D&I for the first time.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

EC State aid references – SA.37855, SA.40154, SA.61545.

UK legal authority for the scheme:

The Industrial Development Act (NI) 2002.
The Industrial Development Order (NI) 1982.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

SMEs  and  large  companies  in  Northern  Ireland's  manufacturing  sector.  Grants  are  paid  against 
vouched expenditure.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total - GBP 1,880,648
2024 Total - GBP 2,220,067

- 62 -

8.

Duration of the subsidy

This scheme will operate until 31 December 2026.

1.4.3  RISK CAPITAL

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Risk Capital.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The overall objective of the Risk Capital support is to improve business growth and development. 
Small  or  Medium  Enterprises  (SMEs)  are  the  backbone  of  our  economy  providing  approximately 
three quarters of all private sector jobs and turnover.

Regardless of size or stage of development, SMEs need funding to scale to the next level and fulfil 
ambitious growth plans. The funds are designed to support both early stage and established SMEs 
which are growing or exporting, or which demonstrate the potential for growth. The funds, which 
operate  on  a  fully  commercial  basis,  are  managed  by  independent  NI  based  Financial  Conduct 
Authority (FCA) approved fund managers.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

UK legal authority for the scheme:

The Industrial Development Act (NI) 2002.
The Industrial Development Order (NI) 1982.

EC State aid references – SA.39644, SA.34142.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Equity and loan.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Companies in the manufacturing sector in line with EU restrictions on shipbuilding, coal and steel, 
synthetic  fibres,  transportation,  agricultural  production,  fisheries  and  aquaculture.  Support  is 
provided through equity and debt finance.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total - GBP 1,411,675
2024 Total - GBP 1,358,756

This is the principal scheme of assistance in Northern Ireland to attract new inward investment, to 
facilitate new start-up enterprises and to encourage existing companies to develop their international 
competitiveness leading to growth and greater employment opportunities.

SMEs are eligible for all types if investment projects. In respect of large companies, the Selective 
Financial Assistance (SFA) Scheme supports the setting up of a new establishment, the starting up 
of a new economic activity or the extension of an existing establishment or involving a fundamental 
change in the product or production process.

Companies in the manufacturing sector in line with EU restrictions on shipbuilding, coal and steel,

Accelerating CCUS (Carbon Capture, Usage and Storage) Technologies (ACT3).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

To accelerate and mature CCUS technologies through funding research and innovation projects.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy



Accelerating  CCUS  Technologies  (ACT)  is  an  initiative  to  facilitate  the  emergence  of 
CO2 Capture, Utilisation and Storage (CCUS) via transnational funding of projects aimed 
at accelerating and maturing CCUS technology through targeted innovation and research 
activities.
ACT 3 is an international initiative between 14 countries including the UK.


 Grant funded under the GBP 1 billion Net Zero Innovation Portfolio.


The  scheme  commenced  in  October  2021  providing  GBP  5million  to  11  projects  to 
accelerate  and  mature  CCUS  technologies  through  funding  research  and  innovation 
projects. The scheme has not changed over time.
The  Department  for  Energy  Security  &  Net  Zero  (formerly  BEIS)  is  the  authority 
responsible for administrating the scheme.



5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided








Recipients  are  typically  consortia  consisting  of  technology  companies,  industrial 
companies, and research organisations. Universities or non-commercial organisations may 
be part of the consortia but were not eligible to lead the application for funding.
Recipients must meet a range of eligibility criteria as set out in the relevant competition 
guidance  documents.  The  successful  applicants  were  selected  through  a  moderated 
scoring process.
The recipients must deliver outputs in accordance with the grant requirements.
The subsidy is provided through payment milestones. Grant recipients submit claims to 
reimburse eligible costs once agreed milestones within the funded activities are complete.

2.1.2  ADVANCED FUELS FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Advanced Fuels Fund.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Advanced Fuels Fund provides capital grant funding to first-of-a-kind (FOAK) commercial and 
demonstration-scale  projects  in  the  UK  to  accelerate  the  development  of  UK  sustainable  aviation 
fuel (SAF) plants.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Section  5  of  the  Science  and  Technology  Act  1965.  Grant  funding  is  administered  by  the 
UK Department for Transport.

The  Advanced  Fuels  Fund  subsidy  scheme  commenced  on  12  December  2022  and  was  published 
once the AFF window 1 winners were announced, 22 December 2022 (window 1 winners' allocations 
were notified in the 2023 WTO Notification Template and have been restated below).

A  second  competitive  window  opened  on  7  June  2023,  with  winners  announced  on 
17 November 2023 (notified below).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  scheme  is  available  to  fuel  producers  that  can  demonstrate  a  clear  potential  to  produce  SAF 
capable  of  reducing  emissions  by  more  than  70%  on  a  lifecycle  basis  when  used  in  place  of 
conventional fossil jet fuel. The specific eligibility criteria can be found on the AFF website.

Grant payments are paid by DfT in instalments on a reimbursement basis once the funded activity 
has been evidenced.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The Advanced Fuels Fund invited industry to bid for a share of the GBP 165m of capital grant funding 
from  July  2022  until  March  2025,  of  which  GBP  135,269,052  has  been  allocated  across  thirteen 
successful projects. An extension to the scheme has invited industry to bid into a third window. This 
extension will take the full available funding pot across the scheme to GBP 198m.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Window 1: 12 December 2022 - 31 March 2025.
Window 2: 17 November 2023 – 31 March 2025.

No statistical data is available.

2.1.3  ADVANCED PROPULSION CENTRE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Advanced Propulsion Centre.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Advanced  Propulsion  Centre  (APC)  provides  funding,  support,  insight  and  foresight  for  the 
development of low emission transport solutions, and automotive technologies. It aims to support 
the UK's transition towards net zero product manufacturing and supply chain in the UK automotive 
sector.

The  objective  of  this  funding  is  to  support  the  UK's  drive  toward  zero  emission  targets  through 
development of late-stage R&D.

To seek proposals for collaborative R&D projects that design, develop and manufacture technology 
that will work towards delivering net-zero carbon emission on-vehicle technologies for on-road or 
off-road vehicles.

All funded projects must:







contribute  to  the  UK's  strategic  aims  and  direction  of  travel  towards  net  zero  transport 
vision, such as the Automotive Council's Roadmaps;
deliver on-vehicle technologies and/or associated manufacturing processes which support 
the transition to net zero;
support growth,  transition  and  security  of  the  UK's  automotive supply  chain, increasing 
capability, whilst improving productivity, efficiency and competitiveness.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 69 -

monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 80,468,337.05.
2024 - GBP 91,596,108.66.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

20/12/2022 – 31/03/2026 .

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

2.1.4  AUTOMOTIVE TRANSFORMATION FUND RESEARCH & DEVELOPMENT

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Automotive Transformation Fund - Research and Development.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Automotive  Transformation  Fund  is  delivered  by  the  Department  of  Business  and  Trade  in 
partnership with the Advanced Propulsion Centre (APC), Innovate UK and partners. It supports the 
industrialisation at scale of a high value electrified automotive supply chain in the UK.

The ATF R&D competitions play a key role in delivering the transition to zero within the automotive 
sector. It will achieve this by enabling both business led R&D and through capital support to secure 
the UKs future electrified automotive supply chain.

The  purpose  of  the  subsidy  is  to  accelerate  growth  by  stimulating  and  supporting  business-led 
innovation, to encourage collaboration and effective co-operation between companies and research 
organisations, including higher education institutions, and to raise both the level and the quality of 
commercially relevant R&D&I activity.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

CCUS (Carbon Capture, Usage and Storage) Innovation 2.0.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  objective  of  the  CCUS  Innovation  2.0  programme  is  to  support,  through  grant  funding, 
innovation projects that either significantly reduce the cost of Carbon Capture Usage and Storage 
(CCUS)  or  help  UK  industry  to  understand  the  opportunity  for  deploying  next  generation  carbon 
capture technology on industrial, waste, or power generation sites for 2030.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Granted under the GBP 1 billion Net Zero Innovation Portfolio. The scheme commenced in July 2021 
and has been delivered in two Calls.

Each  Call  comprised  2  Lots:  Lot  1,  mid  stage  CCUS  Innovation,  with  a  maximum  grant  award  of 
GBP 1 million and Lot 2, Late Stage CCUS Innovation, with a maximum grant award of GBP 5 million.

The Department for Energy Security & Net Zero is the authority responsible for administrating the 
scheme.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided











Recipients  are  typically  consortia  consisting  of  technology  companies,  industrial 
companies, and research organisations. Universities or non-commercial organisations may 
be part of the consortia but were not eligible to lead the application for funding.
Recipients must meet a range of eligibility criteria as set out in the relevant competition

Innovation and technology readiness: the project must be at a Technology Readiness

below)
Project status: no funding could be provided for retrospective work


 Match-funding:  private  funding  was  required  to  be  in  place  to  cover  the  balance  of



eligible costs within 3 months of the grant agreement being approved
Project location: over 50% of the project's costs must be spent within the UK

of CO2
Project duration: All grants end on 31 March 2025
Key Knowledge Deliverables: non confidential project outcomes and learnings would 
be published





be materially different
Project funding lot: projects could receive up to GBP 1 million (Lot 1) or GBP 5 million 
(Lot 2) – see further information on this criterion below

applicant

TRL 1-3: Early-stage research and proof-of-concept
TRL 4-6: Prototype development and validation
TRL 7-9: Full-scale demonstration, testing, and deployment.

Technology readiness level (TRL) is a system that assesses the maturity of a technology, 
typically on a scale from 1-9. The following scales are normally used:



The  TRL  of  the  projects  dictated  which  round  of  funding  businesses  could  apply  for. 
Technologies that could enter Lot 1 had to have an entry criterion of TRL 3-5, and were 
eligible of funding for up to GBP 1 million, to reflect the lower investment levels required 
to  achieve  proof-of-concept  to  prototype  demonstration  technology.  For  Lot  2,  the  TRL 
entry  criterion  was  6-8,  and  projects  could  apply  for  up  to  GBP  5  million  of  funding, 
reflecting the higher level of maturity of the technology and greater investment required 
to achieve demonstration style projects
The subsidy is provided through payment milestones. Grant recipients submit claims to 
reimburse eligible costs once agreed milestones within the funded activities are complete

Amount of the subsidy






A maximum of GBP 5,000,000 per project was available to Lot 2 projects.
