2.1.6  CLIMATE CHANGE AGREEMENT (CCA)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Climate Change Agreement (CCA).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the scheme is to provide relief from the Climate Change levy given to businesses 
who have a CCA in place.

The CCA scheme is administered by the Environment Agency on behalf of the Department for Energy 
Security and Net Zero, who have overall policy responsibility for the scheme.

CCA  are  voluntary  agreements  made  between  UK  industry  and  the  Environment  Agency,  an 
executive  non-departmental  public  body  sponsored  by  the  Department  for  Environment,  Food  & 
Rural Affairs, to reduce energy use and carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions. In return, operators receive 
a  discount  on  the  Climate  Change  Levy  (CCL),  which  is  a  tax  on  certain  non-domestic  energy 
commodities (e.g. fuel and electricity).

The  scheme  serves  the  dual  purpose  of  incentivising  energy  and  carbon  savings  through  setting 
energy-efficiency targets, whilst also helping to reduce energy costs in sectors with energy intensive 
processes by providing significant discounts to the main rates of Climate Change Levy (CCL).

As well as incentivising energy efficiency, the scheme has an important function of keeping energy 
affordable  for  Energy  Intensive  Industries  (EIIs)  with  the  focus  intended  to  be  on  EIIs  facing 
international competition from businesses facing lower energy costs.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The percentage discounts on energy commodities given to CCL by this scheme are reviewed regularly 
and any changes to them are made through the annual Finance Act.

Legal authority for the scheme is Schedule 6 (paragraphs 44 to 50) of the Finance Act 2000. The 
facilities to which CCAs can apply are set out in the Schedule to the Climate Change Agreements 
(Eligible Facilities) Regulations 2012.

The tax element of the scheme is administered by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC), while the CCA 
scheme is administered by the Environment Agency on behalf of the whole of the UK. The Climate 
Change  Agreements  (Administration)  Regulations  2012  set  out  the  procedures  for  administering 
CCAs in the UK.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax Relief.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  discounts  are  aimed  at  energy  intensive  business  consumers  who  sign  CCAs.  The  Scheme  is 
available  to  a  wide  range  of  energy  intensive  industry  sectors,  from  chemicals  and  paper 
manufacturers  to  supermarkets  and  agricultural  businesses  such  as  intensive  pig  and  poultry 
farming.

CCAs require signatories to meet challenging targets to become more energy efficient and reduce 
CO2 emissions. In return for meeting those targets, the businesses are eligible to pay reduced rates 
of CCL on the energy they use. Intensive energy users can receive up to a 92% discount on CCL 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 73 -

rates if they commit to meet energy efficiency targets agreed with the UK Government. The level of 
relief given depends on the fuel used for energy production.

If a business does not meet its targets to become more energy efficient and reduce CO2 emissions, 
they can be removed from the CCA scheme and lose their entitlement to claim any CCL relief.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Estimated total subsidy for Tax Year 2023/24: GBP 240,000,000
Estimated total subsidy for Tax Year 2024/25: GBP 265,000,000

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  current  CCA  scheme  began  in  2013  and  will  run  until  31  March  2027.  The  new  6-year  CCA 
scheme is expected to start from 1 January 2026 and continue until 31 March 2033.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.7  COATED PARTICLE FUEL PROGRAMME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Advanced Modular Reactor Coated Particle Fuel Programme – Step 1 (the Programme).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  objectives  of  the  Programme  are  to  overcome  the  RD&I  challenges  associated  with  Coated 
Particle Fuel (CPF) production in order to maintain the option for a sovereign CPF process & supply 
for  High  Temperature  Gas  Reactors  (HTGRs).  Specifically,  the  Programme  will  carry  out  the 
necessary R&D, modelling, design, quality assurance, and prototype production activities to lead to 
the UK's first prototype CPF fuel block that is ready for irradiation testing in 2025.

The Programme is supported through the Advanced Nuclear Fund (ANF), as part of the Department 
for Energy Security and Net Zero's (DESNZ) GBP 1 billion Net Zero Innovation Portfolio (NZIP). The 
NZIP aims to accelerate the commercialisation of innovative clean energy technologies and processes 
through the 2020s and 2030s. The ANF was established under the previous Administration as part 
of the 2020 Ten Point Plan for a Green Industrial Revolution.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

DESNZ awarded a grant to the UK National Nuclear Laboratory (UKNNL) in respect of the Programme 
on 10 July 2023. The grant was made pursuant to section 5 of the Science and Technology Act 1965. 
In DESNZ's view, there is no other organisation with the necessary underpinning capabilities (people, 
facilities and knowledge), capable of acting as the integrator to deliver the required programme of 
work (i.e., building UK capacity and capability whilst ensuring energy security and independence).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Direct grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

One match-funded direct-award grant was awarded to the UKNNL.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 74 -

Payments  are  made  monthly 
invoice  with  relevant 
deliverables/evidence  of  work  completed.  Invoice  and  deliverables  are  reviewed  by  SICE  Project 
Manager,  NIRO  technical  support,  and  independent  Monitoring  Officer,  with  all  checks  requiring 
sign-off before any payments are approved and released.

in  arrears,  on  submission  of  an 

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Up to GBP 16 million across the full Programme (July 2023 – December 2025).

Amount paid in calendar year 2023: GBP 4.63 million.
Amount paid in calendar year 2024: GBP 6.92 million.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Programme/grant runs between July 2023 – December 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

2.1.8  CONNECTED AND AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Connected and Autonomous Vehicles.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy













To  ensure  the  UK  is  at  the  forefront  of  the  revolution  and  transition  to  connected  and 
autonomous mobility.
Creating  future  jobs  in  the  Connected  and  Automated  Mobility  (CAM)  supply  chain, 
expected to be worth GBP 42bn by 2035.
Reduce accidents and increase road safety, ensuring connected and autonomous vehicles 
work for and benefit society.
To support the UK CAM sector to prosper from a fair and just transition across the nation, 
through taking action to develop a world-class intellectual and physical supply chain for 
CAM technologies in the UK and secure the future of the industry.
Support Net Zero, as CAM services will benefit the drive to Net Zero through automation, 
connectivity and electrification.
Levelling up and improving transport services for the user (public) - CAM has potential to 
help better connect people in under-served places, including those in rural communities, 
to opportunities such as jobs, education, and key services. These outcomes are a core aim 
of this programme and CCAV more widely. CAM can offer improvements in road safety, 
which is often worse in deprived areas, and it can offer accessibility benefits for those who 
have restricted personal mobility.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together

seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

2.1.9  CONTRACTS FOR DIFFERENCE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Contracts for Difference for renewables (CfD) scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the Contracts for Difference (CfD) scheme is to encourage low carbon electricity 
generation in Great Britain.

The  scheme  is  designed  to  incentivise  investments  in  new  renewable  electricity  generation  by 
stabilising revenues for generators in receipt of a contract, at a pre-agreed price for the term of that 
contract. This provides developers of projects with high upfront costs and long lifetimes with direct 
protection  from  volatile  wholesale  prices,  as  well  as  protecting  consumers  from  paying  increased 
support costs when electricity prices are high.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 76 -

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme launched with the opening of the first allocation round in October 2014. Subsequent 
allocation rounds were opened to applications in 2017, 2019, 2021, 2023. The most recent round 
(Round 6) was launched in March 2024.

Amendments have been made to the scheme over this period, which include:


















Amendment to prevent cumulation of State aid (in relation to European Union funding);
Amendment  to  include  new  classes  of  technology,  as  well  as  ending  new  support  for 
conversion of coal to biomass stations;
Amendment of technical rules around standards for various technologies;
Amendment  to  which  technologies  compete  against  each  other  in  auctions  (to  improve 
auction dynamics and fairness);
Amendment to auction rules to allow better and fairer use of alternative bids, introduce 
use of capacity caps, simplify the operation of auctions and ensure better value for money 
outcomes;
Amendments to improve commitments in, and delivery of, developers' plans to support 
renewable supply chain development;
Amendment to the penalty for non-delivery and change to the timescale for meeting early 
delivery commitments;
Change to prevent payments when wholesale electricity prices are negative;
Change to make renewable generators that directly supply offshore oil and gas facilities 
ineligible for the Private Network CfD Agreement;
Introduction  of  a  Clean  Industry  Bonus  to  provide  revenue  support  for  projects  taking 
action  to  accelerate  sustainable  deployment  of  low  carbon  electricity  generation  (for 
three allocation rounds from 2025 onwards).

The legal authority of the Scheme is the Energy Act 2013, and subsequent regulations made under 
the Act:








The Electricity Supplier Obligations (Amendment & Excluded Electricity) Regulations 2015;
The Contracts for Difference (Allocation) Regulations 2014;
The Contracts for Difference (Definition of Eligible Generator) Regulations 2014;
The Electricity Market Reform (General) Regulations 2014;
The Contracts for Difference (Counterparty Designation) Order 2014;
The Contracts for Difference (Standard Terms) Regulations 2014;
The Contracts for Difference (Electricity Supplier Obligations) Regulations 2014.

These regulations have been amended periodically to improve the scheme's effectiveness.

The  Department  for  Energy  Security  and  Net  Zero  is  responsible  for  the  underlying  CfD  policy 
framework and for determining key parameters, such as budgets. The Electricity System Operator 
(National Grid) has been the Delivery Body responsible for managing the application and allocation 
process (now the National Energy System Operator, for future allocation rounds), with the Office of 
Gas  and  Electricity  Markets  (Ofgem,  a  non-departmental  public  body)  as  the  authority  for  any 
application  appeals.  The  Low  Carbon  Contracts  Company  (LCCC,  a  Government-owned  company) 
acts as the counterparty, managing and enforcing contracts including arranging subsidy payments.

The  CfD  scheme  was  preceded  by  the  time-limited  Final  Investment  Decision  Enabling  for 
Renewables  (FIDeR)  programme.  The  FIDeR  programme  was  established  in  2013  to  enable 
developers  of  low  carbon  electricity  projects  to  take  final  investment  decisions  ahead  of  the  CfD 
regime being put in place (allocation by competitive CfD auctions was introduced from 2014). The 
programme took the form of "investment contracts", which functioned similarly to the CfD contracts 
in  how  electricity  generators  receive  payment  (as  explained  below),  and  had  the  same  central 
objective as the CfD of encouraging low carbon electricity generation. In total, 8 FIDeR investment 
contracts  were  issued  in  2014.  Investment  contracts  are  treated  as  CfDs  for  the  purposes  of  the 
Energy Act 2013 provisions relating to CfDs (barring those pertaining to the allocation of CfDs and 
the CfD standard terms and conditions), with the LCCC acting as the counterparty.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 77 -

5.

Form of the subsidy

The CfD scheme provides for private law contracts between electricity generators and the LCCC, who 
provides payments.

Payments are made on a daily basis under those contracts in the form of a variable premium for the 
metered  electricity  generated  by  generators.  This  premium  is  the  difference  between  the  market 
reference price, which represents the wholesale electricity price in Great Britain, and a 'strike' price, 
which is set in the CfD allocation process through competitive auctions.

Payments are funded by a levy on all electricity suppliers in Great Britain determined in accordance 
with  their  market  share  of  supply  (typically  we  expect  these  costs  to  be  passed  through  from 
suppliers to consumers via electricity bills).

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The Scheme is available to low carbon generators of electricity with projects in Great Britain which 
use  eligible  (renewable)  technologies.  Eligibility  requirements  for  the  scheme  are  set  out  in  the 
Contracts for Difference (Definition of Eligible Generator) Regulations 2014 (as amended) and the 
Contracts for Difference (Allocation) Regulations 2014 (as amended). The key criteria includes:








applicable planning consents;
a connection agreement (to the national electricity transmission system or a distribution 
system), or confirmation of intention to supply a private network;
supply  chain  plan  statement,  issued  by  the  Secretary  of  State.  (not  applicable  to  all 
developers);
a non-receipt of other funds provided under Government support schemes.

Successful applicants are determined through a competitive allocation process (with rules published 
beforehand in an 'allocation framework'). Generators who secure a contract receive a subsidy from 
the LCCC as difference payments between the market reference price and their strike price, when 
the reference price is lower. When the reference price is higher than the strike price, generators are 
required to pay back the difference. Contracts awarded last for 15 years.

Payments are funded by a levy on all electricity suppliers in Great Britain determined in accordance 
with  their  market  share  of  supply  (typically  we  expect  these  costs  to  be  passed  through  from 
suppliers to consumers via electricity bills).

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The table below provides the total amount of CfD payments provided during the notification period 
for  renewable  generation  (in  megawatt  hours).  This  includes  payments  made  under  both  CfD 
allocation round contracts and the 8 FIDeR investment contracts (which are now treated as CfDs for 
the purposes of the Energy Act 2013 provisions relating to CfDs).

2  To  note  that  payments  per  unit  and  in  total  per  year  are  not  known  in  advance,  and  payments  can  vary 



considerably depending on:
Electricity generating capacity and actual generation - whilst a maximum generating capacity is known 
for each unit, some projects have long lead-in times before they start generating and the amount of 
actual generation in a given year is uncertain until metered (e.g., for some renewable technologies 
this will be dependent on unpredictable factors such as the weather);

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

 Market  prices  -  CfD  payments  are  dependent  on  variable  wholesale  electricity  prices,  which,  in 

advance, can only be estimated and can be volatile.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  CfD  was  established  in  2014  and  opened  to  applications  for  the  first  allocation  round  on 
16 October 2014. The CfD scheme is subject to periodic review, with the last major review taking 
place between 2018 and 2021.

The delivery years for the CfD scheme, when projects aim to start generation and begin receiving 
payments, were initially provided for up to March 2025, but in 2021 were extended to March 2035. 
CfD contracts, under which subsidy payments may be made, last for 15 years.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

development  of  clean  technology  supply  chains,  worth  GBP  80  billion  in  gross  domestic  product

The investment will accelerate the UK's ability to deliver:

renewable energy;
next generation electric vehicles;
hybrid aircraft – hydrogen and fully electric;
smart grids for energy distribution;
industrial drives;
robotics;
low-carbon construction and agriculture equipment;
low-carbon maritime and rail.

by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

2.1.11  FIRST YEAR ALLOWANCE ON ZERO EMISSION GOODS VEHICLES

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

First-year allowances for expenditure on zero-emission goods vehicles.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  aim  of  the  scheme  is  to  incentivise  the  uptake  of  zero  CO2  emission  goods  vehicles  such  as 
vans,  which  would  also  support  the  wider  policy  on  climate  change  to  reduce  all  greenhouse  gas 
emissions from the UK to net zero by 2050.

The  concession  is  available  to  businesses  purchasing  the  vehicles,  but  this  may  also  benefit  the 
producers/manufacturers of the zero-emission goods by making them more competitive.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Legislation  was  introduced  in  Finance  (No3)  Act  2010  to  provide  a  100%  first-year  allowance  for 
expenditure on new zero-emission goods vehicles.

The Scheme was initially introduced for a five-year period from April 2010, but has been extended 
a number of times. The last extension was made following the Government's announcement that it 
will consult on bringing forward the phase-out date for the sale of new petrol, diesel and hybrid cars 
including vans from 2040 to 2035, or earlier. The scheme ended in April 2025.

The legal authority for the scheme is the Capital Allowances Act 2001 (Sections 45DA; 45DB; 212T). 
The Scheme is administered by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC).

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 80 -

5.

Form of the subsidy

Tax relief.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

UK  business  owners  investing  in  new  zero-emission  goods  vehicles  for  use  in  their  business  are 
eligible  for  this  subsidy.  Companies  and  unincorporated  businesses  are  eligible  for  this  allowance 
although  there  are  a  number  of  exclusions  to  comply  with  EU  state  aid  requirements  which have 
been retained.

The tax concession is claimed in their tax return as a deduction from business profits. It provides a 
timing benefit through providing tax relief for 100% of the expenditure for the chargeable period in 
which it was incurred, in place of a lower rate of capital allowance which would otherwise be available 
to spread the tax relief over multiple chargeable periods.

To qualify for the allowance the expenditure must be:





on a vehicle which is:



'unused and not second-hand';
of  a  design  primarily  suited  for  the  conveyance  of  goods  or  burden  of  a  description 
that cannot in any circumstances emit CO2 by being driven;
registered for use on the road.


Incurred  on  or  after  1  April  2010  but  before  1  April  2025  for  persons  chargeable  to 
corporation tax, and on or after 6 April 2010 but before 6 April 2025 for persons chargeable 
to income tax.

 Not  covered  by  any  of  the  general  exclusions  for  plant  and  machinery  first-year 

allowances, e.g. where the plant or machinery is acquired for leasing.

In addition, there are further restrictions which provide that otherwise qualifying expenditure:




does not exceed EUR 85,000,000 per undertaking;
has not been subsidised by a grant.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Estimated total subsidy 2023: GBP 1,000,000
Estimated total subsidy 2024: GBP 1,000,000

8.

Duration of the subsidy

April 2010 – April 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.12  FREIGHT INNOVATION FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Freight Innovation Fund.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Freight Innovation Fund (FIF) supports the commercialisation of late-stage innovative solutions 
to  support  the  freight  industry.  FIF  helps  innovators  trial  their  technologies  and  scale  up  their 
activities in collaboration with industry partners and investors.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 81 -

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Legal authority for the scheme is the Science and Technology Act 1965, Section 5.

Grants  are  funded  by  the  Department  for  Transport,  via  the  Connected  Places  Catapult  (the 
Catapult).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

 UK based SMEs are eligible to be funded via FIF.



The FIF accelerator requires 30% in-kind funding from successful applicants.
For Cohorts 1 and 2 of the accelerators: 60% paid at the beginning of the programme and 
40% paid on completion of final deliverable of a project report.
For Cohort 3 of the accelerator: 40% paid at the beginning of the programme, 20% after 
trial planning and 40% will be paid on completion of final deliverable of a project report.
The  Freight  Innovation  Cluster  (FIC)  provided  grants  through  two  competitions  - 
Technology stacking/Collaborative Competition and Innovation procurement programme.





7.

Amount of the subsidy



Applicants to the accelerator can apply for up to GBP 150,000 each, with GBP 3.9m being 
allocated across three cohorts and 29 projects.

 GBP 1.2m granted to 9 projects in Accelerator Cohort 1
 GBP 1.3m granted to 10 projects in Accelerator Cohort 2
 GBP 1.4m granted to 10 projects Accelerator Cohort 3


In addition to the accelerator, funding has also been provided through competitions run 
through the FIC with GBP 255,000 granted to 14 technology stacking projects and a further 
GBP 60,000 granted to six projects through a procurement programme.

 GBP 55k to 4 technology stacking projects through Collaborative Competition 1
 GBP 100k to 5 technology stacking projects through Collaborative Competition 2
 GBP 100k to 5 technology stacking projects through Collaborative Competition 3
 GBP 60k to 6 projects through Innovation procurement programme

8.

Duration of the subsidy

FIF launched in January 2023.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy





Through  targeted  support,  funding,  and  ecosystem  development,  the  programme  has 
significantly accelerated innovation and investment in freight and logistics, delivering an 
impressive 14X return on investment to date.
Across the first two cohorts, the accelerator has provided GBP 2.5m of funding to SMEs, 
who have then gone on to leverage over GBP 100m of additional investment, with SMEs 
providing GBP 1.2m of match funding. The third cohort is still ongoing.

 Using a general hurdle rate methodology, we have calculated the benefit cost ratio (BCR) 
for the previous cohorts (years 1 and 2) at 2.3, which is categorised as 'Good'. However, 
it should be noted that a significant portion of the benefits can be attributed to a single 
firm  with  a  high  level  of  investment.  Even  so,  it  is  still  likely  that  this  BCR  value  is  an 
underestimate, as we are currently capturing only the direct benefits of investments made, 
without accounting for the broader economic spillovers generated by SMEs.
The FIC supports innovators, industry and academics to come together to learn from each 
other  and  attend  workshops  to  upskill  themselves  on  areas  such  as  procurement  and 
pitching. The accelerator directly funds SMEs to trial their technology in the real world, to 
help them identify a route to market.



G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 82 -

2.1.13  FUTURE NUCLEAR ENABLING FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Future Nuclear Enabling Fund (FNEF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The primary objective of the FNEF is to help mature nuclear projects that are potentially viable for 
Final Investment Decisions (FID) between 2025 and 2030. Potential projects have been identified 
by examining credibility criteria, alongside wider HMG measures. Credibility criteria include robust 
programme plans to deployment and a demonstrable ability to attract funding to a project.

These projects should be well-placed to enter further competitive processes to identify candidates 
for detailed commercial negotiations, with the aim of reaching a Final Investment Decision (FID) in 
the next Parliament, subject to Value for Money (VfM) and all relevant approvals.

FNEF has been designed to:







Create  options  for  new  nuclear  deployment,  which  will  ultimately  contribute  to  the  UK 
reaching Net Zero whilst ensuring energy supply remains secure, reliable, and affordable.
Encourage  diversity  and  competition  in  the  UK's  nuclear  market  to  drive  down  future 
energy costs and ensure VfM for consumer.
Create value for the UK economy which can be measured through Green Book metrics, 
including  high-value  jobs;  increased  productivity;  and  unlocking  UK  nuclear  export 
potential.

 Unlock opportunities for industry co-investment and co-financing on the UK's next nuclear 

project.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  FNEF  is  a  competitively  allocated  fund  announced,  under  a  previous  administration,  in  the 
Net Zero Strategy (NZS) as a vehicle for the UK government to provide targeted support to tackle 
barriers to entry for future nuclear projects. The legal authority for the FNEF scheme is section 50 
of the United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020.

FNEF grant-supported projects commenced in September 2023. Grants were awarded to 3 recipient 
organisations. The scheme was extended from March 2025 to March 2026 to allow grant recipients 
sufficient time to achieve all milestones set out in individual grant agreements. Although the delivery 
period  was  extended,  the  value  of  each  grant  was  not  increased  and  the  scope  of  the  projects 
supported by the scheme remained unchanged. 

FNEF is administered and monitored by the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero.

5.

Form of the subsidy

A grant scheme with elements of match-funding.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  FNEF  was  open  to  established  nuclear  market  players  and  new  entrants,  including  consortia, 
technology vendors, developers, and/or operators. A technology agnostic route was incorporated, 
ensuring  that  FNEF  was  open  to  a  wide  range  of  players,  promoting  competition  and  supporting 
optionality in Britain's civil nuclear pipeline. Proposals needed to demonstrate potential to provide 
on-grid electricity by 2050, if sufficiently matured under FNEF funding and selected for a FID, which 
will be subject to VfM and all relevant approvals.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 83 -

Grant  payments  are  made  quarterly  in  arrears.  Grant  activity  and  corresponding  claims  are 
monitored  via  a  combination  of  defrayed  expenditure  reviews  and  sampling,  alongside  technical 
reviews. Payments are made in accordance with payment schedules and intervention rates set out 
in individual company grant agreements. Claims for the last quarter of each financial year must be 
accompanied by an independent accountant's report attesting propriety of grant funds for the year.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 total GBP 487,884.
2024 total GBP 22,026,299.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2023 to 2026.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.14  GREEN DISTILLERIES

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Green Distilleries.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy








The  Green  Distilleries  Competition  is  providing  funding  for  developing  technologies  that 
enable the use of low carbon fuel in a distillery.
To further understanding of technical feasibility of fuel switching/enabling technologies.
To successfully demonstrate fuel switching/enabling technologies.
To develop industry and market awareness of fuel switching/enabling technologies.
To build an evidence base to improve BEIS knowledge for industrial decarbonisation.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy









Small  Business  Research  Initiative  (SBRI)  funded  under  the  GBP  1  billion  Net  Zero 
Innovation Portfolio. The scheme commenced in December 2020 and finishes June 2025. 
The scheme has not changed over time, however, does have two business cases, one for 
Phase 1 and one for Phase 2.
Phase 1 (GBP 1.01m) has funded 17 feasibility studies, 12 projects are based in Scotland 
and 5 are based in England.
Phase 2 (GBP 8.76m) is currently funding 3 demonstration projects, two projects based in 
Scotland and one based in England.
The  Department  for  Energy  Security  &  Net  Zero  (formerly  BEIS)  is  the  authority 
responsible for administrating the scheme.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI).

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided



Recipients  are  typically  consortia  consisting  of  technology  companies,  industrial 
companies, and research organisations.








Recipients must meet a range of eligibility criteria as set out in the relevant competition 
guidance  documents.  The  successful  applicants  were  selected  through  a  moderated 
scoring process.
The recipients must deliver outputs in accordance with the grant requirements.
The  subsidy  is  provided  through  payment  milestones.  Recipients  submit  claims  to 
reimburse eligible costs once agreed milestones within the funded activities are complete
Through the Net Zero Innovation Portfolio (NZIP), GBP 10m has been provided to several 
projects  to  carry  out  the  research  and  development  of  a  variety  of  novel  solutions  to 
decarbonise  the  Distilleries  sector.  The  Green  Distilleries  programme  was  split  into 
two Phases,  where  Phase  1  funded  17  feasibility  studies  (completed  March  2021)  and 
Phase 2 is funding 3 demonstration projects until June 2025.




2.1.15  HYDROGEN TRANSPORT HUB

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Hydrogen Transport Hub.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Funding multi-modal projects which can demonstrate the use of hydrogen in a real-world transport 
setting. The demonstrations will provide operational insight into capability, capacity and functionality 
of modes of transport which are solely powered by green hydrogen. It also aims to test business 
models  and  user  operating  needs  to  inform  future  large-scale  trials  or  commercially  sustainable 
operations.

Desired Outcomes





Breaking the market failure and realising the opportunities requires scale. A multi modal 
approach  will  guarantee  co-location  of  demand  and  supply  and  develop  a  critical  mass 
which will create efficiencies in cost, learning, and incentivises industry investment.
The  Hub  is  fundamental  to  understanding  and  realising  the  potential  of  hydrogen  in 
transport  and  is  an  enabler  for  parallel  modal  proposals  (UK-SHORE,  ZERFT,  ZEBRA, 
Aviation, Teesside Hydrogen Trains).

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 85 -

UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 147,086.98.
2024 - GBP 2,529,896.39.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

19/12/2022-19/12/2028 .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.16  INDUSTRIAL ENERGY EFFICIENCY ACCELERATOR

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Industrial Energy Efficiency Accelerator.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy





To develop and demonstrate innovative technologies that will reduce energy consumption, 
maximise resource efficiency, and cut carbon emissions from industry.
Provide  grant  funding  to  eligible  organisations  to  develop  and  demonstrate  their 
technology in industry.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

 Granted under the GBP 1 billion Net Zero Innovation Portfolio.


The scheme commenced in January 2021 and has been delivered in two rounds of funding 
and tranches of projects: Phase 3 and Phase 4.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 86 -





The  Department  for  Energy  Security  &  Net  Zero  is  the  authority  responsible  for 
administrating the scheme.
The Carbon Trust are the Delivery Partner on the scheme.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided











The grant recipients are typically consortia including technology developers (often small 
or medium enterprises (SMEs)) and industrial partners, who's industrial process and site 
is used for the demonstration.
Recipients must meet a range of eligibility criteria as set out in the relevant competition 
guidance documents, such as:
1. The industrial process carried out by the industrial partner for the demonstration being 

typically applicable within SIC codes 05, 07, 08, 10 – 33, 36 – 39 and 63110.

2. The lead organisation being a registered company, research organisation, or academic 

institution.

3. Over 50% of direct project spend being within the UK and the demonstration must be 

4.

carried out within UK industry.
If  a  consortium  is  formed  for  the  project,  the  lead  organisation  must  organise  and 
agree  a  collaboration  agreement  with  the  consortium  members  as  the  Department 
only contracts with the lead organisation directly.

5. Technology  companies, 

industrial  companies,  UK  universities,  and  research 
organisations,  or  consortia  were  eligible  to  apply  for  a  grant  for  a  demonstration 
project at a UK industrial site.

To  be  eligible  for  funding,  whether  forming  a  consortia  or  not,  the  lead  organisation  is 
responsible  for  developing  their  energy  and/or  resource  efficiency  technology  and 
demonstrating it in an industrial (or replicable) environment to improve the energy and/or 
resource efficiency and reduce carbon emissions associated with an industrial process.
1. Energy efficiency improvement is a reduction in the energy required to produce a given 

level of output from an industrial process.

2. Resource efficiency improvement is a reduction in the resource/material required to 

produce a given level of output in an industrial process.

3. Carbon  saving  in  this  context  is  a  reduction  in  carbon  dioxide  emissions  from  an 

industrial process to produce a given level of output.

Technologies  which  can  improve  energy  and/or  resource  efficiency  in  industry  are  at 
technology readiness level 5 (technology basic validation in a relevant environment) to 8 
(system/technology  complete  and  qualified  through  test  and  demonstration)  at  project 
start are eligible for funding.
The subsidy is provided through payment milestones. Grant recipients submit claims to 
reimburse eligible costs once agreed milestones within the funded activities are complete.

7.

Amount of the subsidy







The typical subsidy is between GBP 100,000 and GBP 1m in grant award per project.
The total amount of grant awarded is GBP 7,657,193.98 across 14 projects, which works 
out at an average grant award of GBP 546,942.43 per project.
The total amount budgeted for the scheme is GBP 8m.
Totals  spend  2022/23  GBP  1,487,395.21,  2023/24  GBP  3,616,856.81  and  2024/25 
GBP 3,175,376.76 (f/c).

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme is active from January 2021 to October 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 87 -

2.1.17  INDUSTRIAL ENERGY TRANSFORMATION FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Industrial Energy Transformation Fund (IETF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Industrial  emissions  currently  count  for  around  c.17%  of  UK  emissions.  Industrial  emissions  will 
need to fall by around 95% to achieve the UK's net zero target. The IETF will help achieve this by 
offering  crucial  support  to  businesses  of  all  sizes,  across  a  broad  range  of  sectors  to  be  able  to 
finance  projects  to  improve  energy  efficiency  and  decarbonise  that  may  not  get  funded  without 
the IETF.

The Industrial Energy Transformation Fund (IETF) supports industrial sites with high energy use to 
transition to a low carbon future. The fund targets existing industrial processes, helping industry to 
reduce  energy  consumption  by  investing  in  more  efficient  technologies  and  reduce  emissions  by 
bringing down the costs and risks associated with investing in decarbonisation technologies.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal authority for the scheme is Section 98 of the Natural Environment and Rural Communities 
Act 2006. The Scheme is administered by the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero.

The IETF was launched on the 20 July 2020 and has had 3 phases with GBP 500 million of funding 
available up until 2028 (figure includes Scottish IETF). Phase 1 had 2 competition windows, Phase 2 
launched in 2021 had 4 application windows and Phase 3 launched in 2024 has had 1 competition 
window to date. The scheme is currently on hold following the Autumn 2024 budget.

The Department for Energy Security & Net Zero (DESNZ) manages the IETF for England, Wales, and 
Northern Ireland (previously the Department of Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy, BEIS).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Competitive grant funding.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The fund is open to a broad range of industrial sectors of all sizes and will support applicants, both 
within and outside of industrial clusters. The Scheme is available to industrial sites in England, Wales 
or Northern Ireland operating in the following sectors:

Recovery and recycling of materials;

 Mining and Quarrying;
 Manufacturing;

 Data centre;



Industrial Laundries;
Controlled Environment Horticulture (CEH).

Funding is allocated through a competitive process aimed at supporting the highest quality and most 
transformational  bids.  Grant  funding  is  provided  quarterly  in  arrears  once  the  project  has 
successfully passed through:





Eligibility checks – based on all relevant eligibility criteria including SIC code and project 
costs;
Assessment  –  Includes  an  economic  assessment  (the  project's  value  for  money); 
a transformational  assessment  (the  project's  compatibility  with  UK  net  zero 
commitments); and a deliverability assessment;

 Due diligence checks.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 88 -

Grant  amount  per  beneficiary  varies  according  to  competition  strain,  company  size  and  location. 
There are minimum and maximum thresholds that applicants are able to claim.

The Scottish government took their budget allocation to create the Scottish IETF (Also notified in 
this document). They increased their share of the budget to offer up to GBP 34 million to sites in 
Scotland through the Scottish IETF. This means that the IETF does not provide support to sites in 
Scotland.

Grant  payment  after  eligible  costs  have  been  invoiced  and  evidenced  is  the  mechanism  through 
which payment is made.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The  value  of  grants  for  194  IETF  funded  projects  from  Phase  1  to  Phase  3.1  (all  current  phases) 
range from GBP 23k to GBP 30m.The mean average of these grants is GBP 1.4m and the median 
average is GBP 428k.

The total amount provided over the notification period is GBP 42,774,160.90

Budget allocations for grants in last 3 fiscal years were as follows: GBP 47.7m in 22/23, GBP 80.5m 
in 23/24, GBP 100.3m in 24/25. Budget allocations for 2025-2028 are GBP 185m for England, Wales 
and Northern Ireland.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  IETF  was  launched  in  July  2020  and  funding  is  available  up  until  the  end  of  UK  Financial 
Year 2027/2028.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

Industrial Fuel Switching – Phase 2.

2023 and 2024.

The purpose of the funding is to accelerate industrial decarbonisation by supporting the development

industry move from high to low carbon fuels.

Background and authority for the subsidy

and Phase 2. Phase 1 commenced in March 2022, Phase 2 commenced in March 2023 and

responsible for administrating the scheme.

2.1.19  INDUSTRY OF FUTURE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Industry of Future.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy





Supporting the development and demonstration of new energy technologies, systems, and 
processes.
Stimulating private sector investment in the most promising mid-to-late-stage low carbon 
energy innovations facilitating commercialisation.

 Maintaining  UK  international  leadership  in  areas  that  will  benefit  the  UK  clean  energy 



sector and align with Industrial Strategy objectives.
Ensure UK policies on national and international ambition, and decarbonising the energy 
system  and  built  environment,  are  based  on  the  most  up-to-date  and  robust  technical 
evidence.

 Drive international action on climate change by promoting research and innovation efforts 

to drive down cost globally and build new markets.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy








Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) funded under the Net Zero Innovation Portfolio
Funded  from  the  BEIS  GBP  1bn  Net  Zero  Innovation  Portfolio  (NZIP),  the  Industry  of 
Future programme aims to increase the range of options available to industry to enable 
them to decarbonise at a faster rate.
The  programme  focused  on  dispersed  sites  outside  of  industrial  clusters  and  on  seven 
different sectors: chemicals, paper, minerals, food, water, transport, and pharmaceuticals.
Eligible  sites  were  divided  into  three  Lots  based  on  annual  scope  1  and  scope  2  CO2 
emissions.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 90 -





In October 2021, 15 industrial sites applied to be part of the GBP 1.58m scoping study, 
led by Atkins, to develop industrial decarbonisation roadmaps specific to each site. A series 
of  workshops  were  conducted  with  each  site,  producing  a  number  of  decarbonisation 
roadmaps  with  the  aim  of  publishing  a  summary  report  containing  the  key  findings 
early 2023. 
The  Department  for  Energy  Security  &  Net  Zero  (formerly  BEIS)  is  the  authority 
responsible for administrating the scheme.

Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) contract.



supplier, who took on the Phase 1 research study.
Atkins  met  a  range  of  eligibility  criteria  that  were  set  out  in  the  relevant  competition 
guidance documents. They were selected through a moderated scoring process.
The  subsidy  is  provided  through  payment  milestones.  Recipients  submit  claims  to 
reimburse eligible costs once agreed milestones within the funded activities are complete.

2.1.20  NET ZERO HEAT

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Net Zero Heat.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Innovate  UK  Net  Zero  Heat  programme  works  to  build  the  certainty  needs  to  scale  rapidly 
alongside key partners, so the sector can scale at a rate needed to be on track with the 6th Carbon 
Budget. The programme largely deals with the non-technical barriers to scaling out these solutions 
that require innovation spanning digital, space, high-value manufacturing, and sensing sectors as 
well as the core beneficiaries in energy and construction. To deliver net zero heat upgrades of all 
the UK's 30 million buildings requires solution providers across multiple sectors to work together, 
using information and processes that are not fully in place yet and can't be developed any one single 
company.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 91 -

Desired Outcomes



A  'cost-effective'  approach  to  UK  retrofit  could  deliver  a  net  present  value  of 
GBP 7.5 billion.

 Develop a cohort of 30 – 50 net zero heat companies to use as a cohort for development 

of this programme and subsequent plans.

Support greater certainty of performance of net zero heat solutions.

 Drive a faster rise in demand for net zero heat solutions.

 Grow UK supply chains for net zero heat solutions.


Increase flow of green finance to net zero heat solutions.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 719,675.68.
2024 - GBP 4,032,004.86.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

14/12/2022-14/12/2028 .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.21  NET ZERO HYDROGEN FUND STRANDS 1 AND 2

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Net Zero Hydrogen Fund (NZHF) Strands 1 and 2.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 92 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy









The primary policy objective of the NZHF is to support the commercial deployment of new 
low carbon hydrogen production projects through the provision of upfront capital grants, 
to assist with achieving the Government's ambition (at the time of launch) of deploying 
up to 10GW of low carbon hydrogen production capacity by 2030 (with at least half from 
electrolytic hydrogen) and the UK's legally binding 2050 Net Zero target.
The NZHF funds the development and deployment of new low carbon hydrogen production 
to  de-risk  investment  and  reduce  lifetime  costs.  Both  strands  are  designed  to  support 
multiple low carbon hydrogen production technologies that meet the eligibility criteria.
Strand  1  provides  development  expenditure  (DEVEX)  for  front  end  engineering  design 
(FEED)  and  post-FEED  activities,  aiming  to  build  the  pipeline  of  hydrogen  production 
projects to measurably move these closer to deployment.
Strand 2 provides capital expenditure (CAPEX) support for hydrogen production projects 
that do not require revenue support through the Hydrogen Business Model (HBM).

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal basis of this scheme is Section 8 of the Industrial Development Act 1982.

The scheme commenced in 2023. The public authority responsible for administering the scheme is 
The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The subsidy is available to low carbon hydrogen production developers who successfully applied to 
the Net Zero Hydrogen Fund strand 1 and 2 competition Rounds 1 and 2. The subsidy is provided in 
grant funding payments, paid in arrears against eligible expenditure defrayed.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The total amount budgeted for the scheme is up to GBP 240,000,000.

8.

Duration of the subsidy 

The duration of Strand 1 and 2 is 820 days from the 31 January 2023 to 31 March 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.22  NET ZERO MOBILITY

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Net Zero Mobility.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 93 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Net  Zero  domain  of  the  Future  Economy  Review  aims  for  the  UK  to  prosper  from  being  the 
fastest  transitioning  economy  to  net  zero.  Mobility  aims  to  advance  innovative  propulsion 
technologies  that  will  enable  the  movement  of  people  and  goods  from  one  location  to  another 
through seamless, safe, net zero, connected cost-effective, accessible and reliable means.

The  domain  will  comprise  of  four  programme  areas:  Power,  Heat,  Make  &  Use  and  Mobility. 
Objectives of the Net Zero domain include:

Developing  UK  competitive  advantage  in  net  zero  -  saved  costs,  greater  energy  security,  higher 
growth, attracted global investment, retained and grown talent.

Accelerating leverage of private investment for net zero deployment and diffusion.

Creating greater market demand and certainty through overcoming barriers to scale-up – joining up 
standards, regulation and finance and taking a system approach.

Creating  greater  cross-sector  collaboration  to  tackle  the  larger  challenges  and  grow  new  supply 
chains.

Contributing to the levelling up agenda through place-based investments.

Increasing  exports  of  net  zero  goods  and  services  to  all  markets  Reducing  carbon  impact  of  UK 
business in line or ahead of carbon budgets and reducing the impacts of climate breakdown through 
adaptation.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 2,364,852.15.

2.1.23  PLUG-IN VEHICLE GRANTS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Plug-in Vehicle Grants.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective is to support the early market for zero emission vehicles. Support is provided through 
vehicle support subsidies covering:

 Motorcycles;



 Wheelchair accessible vehicles (WAV).

Taxis (hackney carriages);
Trucks;
Vans;

The  grants  partially  bridge  the  price  differential  between  eligible  zero/ultra-low  emission  vehicles 
and traditionally fuelled vehicles.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Legal authority for the scheme is Section 5 of the Science & Technology Act 1965.

Grants  are  provided  by  the  Department  for  Transport,  via  the  Office  for  Zero  Emission  Vehicles 
(OZEV).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Purchasers of vehicles are the beneficiaries. The funding is paid to vehicle manufacturers, with the 
saving passed on to the purchaser.

Grants are open to all vehicles meeting the criteria, regardless of country of manufacture or company 
ownership. Grants are provided as a fixed amount per vehicle. The value of the grant is dependent 
on vehicle type and size.

For plug-in van grants, eligible vans and trucks are allocated into categories depending on weight. 
These are limited to 1500 orders per end customer per financial year. The number of total grants 
available  per  year  and  number  of  grants  that  can  be  applied  for  per  financial  year  also  varies  by 
vehicle size.

For plug-in WAV grants, the vehicle must be converted from a passenger vehicle (sometimes known 
as a 'category M1 vehicle'), have zero tailpipe CO2 emissions and be able to travel at least 112km 
(70 miles) without any emissions.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 95 -

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Plug-in Van and Truck Grant:







Small Vans (under 2.5t): up to GBP 2500 per unit
Large Vans (Between 2.5t and 4.25t): up to GBP 5000 per unit
Small Trucks (between 4.25t and 12t): up to GBP 16,000 per unit. There are a total of 
250 small truck grants available each year, and applicants may receive up to 10 grants 
each financial year. If either limit has been reached, the grant will be paid at GBP 5000 
per unit.
Large Trucks (over 12t): up to GBP 25,000 per unit. There are a total of 100 large truck 
grants available each year, and applicants may receive up to 5 grants each financial year. 
If either limit has been reached, the grant will be paid at GBP 16,000 per unit.

Plug-in  Motorcycle  Grant:  %  of  purchase  price  per  eligible  motorcycle  changed  over  the 
notification period:



35% of purchase price up to GBP 500 for motorcycles that meet the criteria. (for vehicles 
priced below GBP 10k)

 Until  6  April  2024  -  35%  of  purchase  price  up  to  GBP  150  for  mopeds  that  meet  the 

criteria. (for vehicles priced below GBP 10k)

Plug-in Taxi Grant: eligible taxis are allocated into one of two categories based on their carbon 
emissions and zero-emission range:

From 1 January 2023 to 5 April 2024

 Maximum grant amount of GBP 7,500 for vehicles with zero emission range (WLTP 'electric 
city  range'  from  September  2019)  of  70  miles  or  more,  and  emissions  of  less  than 
50g CO2/km; and

 Maximum grant amount of GBP 3,000 for vehicles with zero emission range (WLTP 'electric 
city range' from September 2019) of 10-69 miles, and emission of less than 50gCO2/km.

From 5 April 2024 to 6 April 2025

 Maximum grant amount of GBP 6,000 for vehicles with zero emission range (WLTP 'electric 
city  range'  from  September  2019)  of  70  miles  or  more,  and  emissions  of  less  than 
50g CO2/km; and

 Maximum grant amount of GBP 3,000 for vehicles with zero emission range (WLTP 'electric 
city range' from September 2019) of 10-69 miles, and emission of less than 50gCO2/km.

Plug-in WAV: Maximum amount of 35% of purchase price, up to GBP 2,500 per unit. There is a 
price cap of GBP 35,000, including VAT and delivery fees, but not including the cost of conversion.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Plug-in Van and Truck Grant: November 2012 – Present

Plug-in Motorcycle Grant: October 2016 – Present

Plug-in Taxi Grant: December 2018 – Present

Plug-in WAV Grant: December 2021 – Present

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.24  RED DIESEL REPLACEMENT

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Red Diesel Replacement.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy





To provide funding to develop lower carbon, lower cost alternatives to using red diesel in 
the construction, mining, and quarrying sectors.
Provide  grant  funding  to  eligible  organisations  to  develop  lower  carbon  alternatives  to 
using red diesel in the construction, mining and quarrying sectors.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

 Granted under the GBP 1 billion Net Zero Innovation Portfolio.


The scheme commenced in July 2021 and has been delivered in two phases, with Phase 1 
funding smaller scale & cost feasibility and prototype development projects and Phase 2 
funding larger scale & cost end-to-end red diesel replacement demonstration projects.
The  Department  for  Energy  Security  &  Net  Zero  (formerly  BEIS)  is  the  authority 
responsible for administrating the scheme.



5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided





Recipients  are  typically  consortia  consisting  of  technology  companies,  industrial 
companies, and research organisations. Universities or non-commercial organisations may 
be part of the consortia but were not eligible to lead the application for funding.
Recipients must meet a range of eligibility criteria as set out in the relevant competition 
guidance documents, such as:
1. Projects must be led by private organisation or Research & Technology Organisation 

(RTO)

2. Projects  can  only  be  funded  where  evidence  can  be  provided  that  innovation  would 
not  be  taken  forwards  (or  would be  taken  forwards  at  a  much  slower  rate)  without 
public sector funding. 

3. Projects  can  work  with  international  partners,  but  over  50%  of  the  funded  project 
work (by financial value) must be conducted in the UK. The physical demonstration of 
the proposed solution must be conducted in the UK.

4. Projects agree to share knowledge gained widely and publicly
5. Projects  confirm  that  all  parties  involved  in  the  bid  application  do  not  have  any 

activities with any sanctioned entity.



To be eligible for funding, projects needed to demonstrate and develop innovation in their 
solutions  for  low-carbon/  lower  cost  alternatives  to  diesel  use  applicable  to  off  road 
machinery in the construction, mining and quarrying sectors.

7.

Amount of the subsidy








A maximum of GBP 460,000 per project was available to Phase 1 projects.
Phase 2 projects could apply for between GBP 1m and GBP 12m in grant funding.
The total amount awarded was GBP 6,503,651.03 under Phase 1 and GBP 25,725,610.91 
under Phase 2, totalling GBP 32,229,261.94 across the programme.
The average grant per project in Phase 1 is GBP 406,478.19.
The average grant per project in Phase 2 is GBP 4,287,601.82.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 97 -




Total budgeted for the scheme was GBP 40 million.
Total  spend  22/23  GBP  5,906,55.35,  23/24  GBP  6,718,030.25  and  24/25 
GBP 14,190,722.17 (f/c),

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Started in July 2021 and ends in August 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.25  RURAL TRANSPORT ACCELERATOR

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Rural Transport Accelerator.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Rural Transport Accelerator expedites the uptake of innovations to improve transport in rural 
areas  through  a  series  of  demonstrators  that  will  develop  potential  solutions  to  rural  transport 
challenges.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Legal authority for the scheme is the Science and Technology Act 1965, Section 5.

Grants are funded by the Department for Transport, via the Connected Places Catapult.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

SMEs were eligible for the scheme. Funding was provided via quarterly payments.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

GBP 2m across both financial years (23/24 and 24/25) with GBP 1.2m pass-through to the 8 SMEs 
that were selected. Organisations must complete a Minimal Financial Assistance Declaration to be 
awarded the grant. The Catapult manages this on the Departments behalf.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme commenced in 2023 and ended in 2025.

2.1.26  SMART SUSTAINABLE PLASTIC PACKAGING

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Smart Sustainable Plastic Packaging.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  challenge  brings  together  academia  and  industry  and  is  underpinned  by  the  delivery  of  the 
2025 UK Plastics Pact targets to establish the UK as a leading innovator in smart and sustainable 
plastic packaging for consumer products.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 and 2024 - GBP 2,739,821.83.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

16/11/2022 – 16/11/2028 .

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.27  UK SHORE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

UK SHORE.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

To ensure the UK meets its climate commitments in the required timescale, through supporting the 
decarbonisation of the maritime transport, which currently emits more C02 than buses and trains 
combined.  

To  support  the  UK  maritime  sector  to  prosper  from  a  fair  and  just  transition  across  the  nation, 
through  taking  action  to  develop  a  world-class  intellectual  and  physical  supply  chain  for  clean 
maritime technologies in the UK and secure the future of the industry. Correct the R&I related market 
failures associated with achieving the policy objective.

Desired Outcomes









To accelerate R&D in clean maritime technologies, across the TRL spectrum from 1 to 9 
and across the range of agreed technology pathways available to the sector, reflecting its 
diverse needs
To  decarbonise  the  UK's  domestic  maritime  sector,  both  vessels  and  ports,  and  ensure 
that the UK supply chain benefits from the transition
To  establish  the  UK's  leadership  in  clean  maritime  technologies,  their  development, 
deployment and commercialisation.
A world-class intellectual and physical supply chain for clean maritime, levelling up across 
the UK as this new industrial revolution takes hold and legacy industries transform. 

 New  high-quality  jobs,  skills,  increased  foreign  direct  investment  and  UK  business  R&D 



investment, spurring a surge in successful British talent and innovation. 
Increased sales and improved market share in UK, Europe and globally in clean maritime 
technologies, leading to a self-sustaining and prosperous UK industry. 

 Wider  productivity  (e.g.,  cost  savings  for  user;  reduced  energy  costs;  spill  overs)  and 
societal benefits (e.g., cleaner urban air in port cities and improved access to training).

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid

2.1.28  UK SUSTAINABLE AVIATION FUEL CLEARING HOUSE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

UK Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) Clearing House.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The UK SAF Clearing House provides capital grant funding to support testing costs for innovative 
new fuels. Applications for support are assessed based on factors including sustainability, technical 
suitability, and commercial potential.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Section  5  of  the  Science  and  Technology  Act  1965.  Grant  funding  is  administered  by  the 
UK Department  for  Transport,  and  applications  are  assessed  on  a  'first-come-first  served'  rolling 
basis with application windows.

The  application  window  for  funding  support  through  the  UK  SAF  Clearing  House  opened  on 
17 November 2023 and closed on 14 September 2024. A further application window is likely to open 
in spring/summer 2025.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  scheme  is  available  to  fuel  producers  that  can  demonstrate  a  clear  potential  to  produce  SAF 
capable  of  reducing  emissions  by  more  than  70%  on  a  lifecycle  basis  when  used  in  place  of 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 101 -

conventional fossil jet fuel. The specific eligibility criteria can be found on the UK SAF Clearing House 
website.

Grant payments are paid by DfT in instalments on a reimbursement basis once the funded activity 
has been evidenced.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The UK SAF Clearing House invited industry to bid for a share of GBP 5.35m in capital grant funding 
from  November  2023  until  March  2025.  To  date,  GBP  42,350.32  has  been  allocated  across 
two successful projects.

The following projects have received a grant award to date:

Avioxx Ltd: Up to GBP 25,600
Zero Petroleum Ltd: Up to GBP 16,750.32

8.

Duration of the subsidy

17 November 2023 - 31 March 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.1.29  VEHICLE CHARGING AND INFRASTRUCTURE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Vehicle Charging and infrastructure.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Infrastructure solutions are required to enable the UK to achieve its commitment to end the sale of 
new non-zero emission cars and vans from 2030, and Heavy Goods Vehicles from 2040.

The  aim  of  the  scheme  is  to  unlock  and  expand  the  flexibility  potential  of  Electric  Vehicle 
bi-directional charging technologies and business models.

Delivered  using  funding  combined  of  research  and  development  projects,  infrastructure  support, 
investor partnerships, minimal financial assistance and accelerators.

Aiming to support solutions such as:





Fleet  Charging  solutions:  Route  &  charging  optimisation  tools,  increasing  outlets  per 
charger,  relocatable  rapid  hubs,  new  wireless,  and  autonomous  &  mobile  charging 
technologies
Planning & business models: Charge point planning & optimisation solutions, new business 
models, and the chance to meet with investment funds

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 102 -





Improving  user  experience:  Improving  accessibility,  roaming,  reservations,  payment 
systems, load balancing, sharing home chargers, and electric forecourts
Energy solutions: Enabling locations without sufficient grid capacity, grid boosting, local 
energy storage, and Vehicle to Grid & V2X.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.
