2.1.30  ZERO EMISSION ROAD FREIGHT

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Zero Emission Road Freight.

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 103 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of this competition is to support the UK's drive toward zero emission targets through 
demonstration of leading-edge hydrogen fuel cell and battery electric trucks.

This is necessary to support adoption of zero emission trucks in the UK, Europe and globally. This is 
underpinned by government policy and the phases out dates for non-zero emission trucks:

Phase-out dates confirmed: new, non-zero emission HGVs less than or equal to 26 tonnes will be 
introduced from 2035 and, from 2040, all new HGVs sold in the UK must be zero emission.

Desired Outcomes

 Generate  government  and  industry  understanding  of  the  opportunities,  costs  and 
challenges associated with large scale and high intensity deployments. This will be used 
to inform future policy around decarbonising this sector.

 Without the intervention industry would continue to buy non-zero emission trucks and it 
would  be  impossible  to  achieve  the  UK  Government's  ambitious  targets  and  timelines. 
There is work ongoing in this field but without support and investment, it will be impossible 
to achieve a zero-emission road freight sector before these targets.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

2023 – GBP 2,966,562.96.
2024 - GBP 23,066,842.08.

2.2  ENGLAND

2.2.1  NATURAL ENVIRONMENT INVESTMENT READINESS FUND (NEIRF)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Natural Environment Investment Readiness Fund (NEIRF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Natural Environment Investment Readiness Fund (NEIRF) is a standalone technical assistance 
grant scheme, available in England.

The aim of the fund is to support the development of projects that can support delivery of one or 
more  natural  environmental  outcomes  from  the  Environmental  Improvement  Plan  and  have  the 
ability  to  generate  revenues  from  ecosystem  services  in  order  to  attract  and  repay  investment. 
Grants will be provided to procure technical assistance support and advice from professional advisors 
to address barriers to revenue generation from ecosystem services and present compelling cases for 
potential  investors.  By  exception,  grants  can  be  used  to  develop  ecosystem  market  enablers 
(e.g., ecosystem investment platforms, verification codes, aggregator vehicles).

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme commenced in February 2021, and did not experience any changes to the scheme during 
the notification period.

The legal authority for the scheme is the Natural Environment and Rural Communities Act 2006. The 
Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) is responsible for the scheme.

The  scheme,  including  grant  funding,  is  administered  by  the  Environment  Agency  (a 
nondepartmental public body).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The scheme is available to a wide range of applicants, including not-for-profit organisations, local 
authorities and businesses.

The grants are competitive, and applications judged with those offering the greatest benefit to the 
NEIRF aims being offered an agreement. Each agreement will last for either 1 or 2 years depending 
on the specific activities applied for. Individual grants of up to GBP 100,000 will be made available 
with recipients identified through a competitive application process.

Grants  are  paid  to  provide  reimbursement  for  expenditure  incurred  on  specific  elements  that  are 
agreed during the application process, having been tested on their value for money.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 4,595,389.55.
2024 Total: GBP 1,412,345.58.

Subsidy per unit: Variable between GBP 11,850 – GBP 100,000.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 105 -

8.

Duration of the subsidy

February 2021 –31 March 2026. Final payments expected for a further 3 months, with final payments 
made by 30 June 2026.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.2.2  ZERO EMISSION BUS REGIONAL AREAS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Zero Emission Bus Regional Areas (ZEBRA).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The ZEBRA programme contributes towards the UK government's broader net zero goals.

The objectives of the ZEBRA programme are to increase the uptake of zero emission buses in England 
(outside of London), to aid decarbonisation of the bus fleet, to improve air quality in areas exceeding 
NO2 limits, and to encourage investment in the UK bus industry.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Legal authority for the programme is granted under Section 31 of the Local Government Act 2003. 
The  programme  is  managed  by  the  Department  for  Transport.  It  launched  in  2021  and  provides 
grants  to  local  transport  authorities  in  England  (outside  of  London).  It  has  had  two  phases: 
ZEBRA 1 (2021) and ZEBRA 2 (2023).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Grant  funding  is  provided  to  local  transport  authorities.  ZEBRA  2  split  grant  payments  into 
two tranches, paid over financial years 2023/24 and 2024/25 in lump sums by bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

UK Financial Year 2023/24: GBP 83,875,918
UK Financial Year 2024/25: GBP 33,006,033

8.

Duration of the subsidy

March 2024 to March 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 106 -

2.3  SCOTLAND

2.3.1  SCOTTISH ENTERPRISE ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION SUBSIDY

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Scottish Enterprise Environmental Protection Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Scheme aims to encourage investment in environmental protection measures by companies in 
Scotland to secure both long term economic benefits to Scotland and help achieve Scotland's low 
carbon targets.

The scheme provides subsidies for a number of activities, such as:

Investment subsidy for energy efficiency measures;
Investment subsidy for energy efficiency projects in buildings;
Investment subsidy for high efficiency cogeneration;
Investment subsidy for the promotion of energy from renewable sources;





 Operating subsidy for the promotion of electricity from renewable sources;
 Operating  subsidy  for  the  promotion  of  energy  from  renewable  sources  in  small  scale 

installations; and
Investment subsidy for energy infrastructure.



4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

This  scheme  is  administered  by  Scottish  Enterprise. Scottish  Enterprise  is  established  under  the 
Enterprise and New Towns (Scotland) Act 1990 and provides support to enterprises across Scotland 
under the provisions of that Act.

The Scheme previously operated under:







Article  366  of  Title  XI  of  the  UK-EU  Trade  and  Cooperation  Agreement  signed  on 
30 December  2020  -as  implemented  by  section  29  of  the  European  Union  (Future 
Relationship) Act 2020;
Article 10 of the Northern Ireland Protocol to the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement agreed on 
17 October 2019 in respect of EU Structural Funds (as implemented by section 7A of the 
European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018); and
Article 138 of the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement agreed on 17 October 2019 in respect of 
EU Structural Funds (as implemented by section 7A of the European Union (Withdrawal) 
Act 2018).

The  scheme  is  now  classed  as  a  "legacy  scheme"  for  the  purposes  of  the  UK  Subsidy  Control 
Act 2022.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Principally grant contribution.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  scheme  is  available  to  enterprises  of  all  sizes. Applicants  must  submit  an  application  for 
assistance  to  SE  before  work  on  the  project  or  activity  has  started,  and  the  application  must  be 
approved in writing before work can commence on the project.

Eligibility  and  amount  of  support  provided  towards  eligible  costs  varies  depending  on  the  type  of 
activity.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 107 -

Subsidy is typically provided through a grant contribution.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The total amount budgeted for the scheme is GBP 20m.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Scheme runs from 23 December 2022 to 31 December 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.3.2  SCOTTISH INDUSTRIAL ENERGY TRANSFORMATION FUND (SIETF)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Scottish Industrial Energy Transformation Fund (SIETF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Scottish  Industrial  Energy  Transformation  Fund  (SIETF)  aims  to  support  industrial  sites  with 
high energy use to transition to a low carbon future, improve international competitiveness whilst 
protecting jobs.

The fund is designed to target industrial processes, helping industry to:






cut energy bills by investing in more efficient technologies; and
reduce  emissions  by  bringing  down  the  costs  and  risks  associated  with  investing  in 
decarbonisation technologies
support inclusive growth, aligned to the global Sustainable Development Goals.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  scheme's  legal  authority  is  Section  153(4)  of  the  Environmental  Protection  Act  1990.  The 
Scottish Industrial Energy Transformation Fund (SIETF) is registered under the UK subsidy control 
number SC10290.

In 2020, Scottish Ministers announced that GBP 34,000,000 would be made available, via the SIETF, 
for capital projects over the next five years.

Three competitions were held between 2020 to 2022 for match-funded grants to deploy technologies 
that improve industrial EE and carry out feasibility & FEED studies into EE or deeper decarbonisation 
such as fuel switching.

The fund is administered by the Scottish Government.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Competitive grant funding.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  Scheme  is  available  to  manufacturing  sites  located  in  Scotland,  operating  in  the  following 
sectors, defined by its Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) code:

 Mining and Quarrying (SIC codes: 05101-05200; 07100-08900; 09900)

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 108 -

 Manufacturing (SIC code: 10000–33200)

 Data centre (SIC code: 63110)

Recovery and recycling materials (SIC code: 38320)

Applicants should demonstrate why the chosen technology solution is appropriate and what savings 
it will achieve and check that it meets the standards and eligibility criteria specified in the guidance. 
Applicants  in  all  competition  strands  are  required  to  define  their  proposal  as  either  achieving  an 
energy  efficiency  or  decarbonisation  outcome.  Applicants  seeking  grants  over  GBP  500,000  are 
required to place their projects in the context of a commitment to reduce greenhouse gas emissions 
at a level consistent with Scotland's 2045 net zero target.

Following  assessment  and  due  diligence  processes,  a  Grant  Offer  Letter  is  issued  to  successful 
applicants,  which  includes  the  value  of  grant  funding.  The  amount  of  support  provided  can  vary 
based  on  the  location  of  the  industrial  site,  with  additional  funding  being  made  available  to 
businesses which intend to carry out their project in an area of lesser economic advantage.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

To date, approximately GBP 17m has been offered to 28 projects across a range of sectors. This will 
generate over GBP 50m investment because each GBP 1 of public funds is matched by GBP 1.8 of 
private funds.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2020 – 2026. The programme will be reviewed prior to further invitations to apply.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

2.3.3  SCOTTISH ZERO EMISSION BUS CHALLENGE FUND (SCOTZEB)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Scottish Zero Emission Bus Challenge Fund (ScotZEB).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy







The aim of the ScotZEB fund was to support swift, and significant, change in the new bus 
market in favour of zero emission vehicles/technology.
By promoting a swift transition to zero emission vehicles and infrastructure, the fund will 
help to achieve targets to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, contribute to the delivery of 
Low  Emission  Zones,  encourage  inward  investment  and  growth  in  the  supply  chain  of 
zero carbon  vehicle  and  energy  solutions  in  Scotland,  with  associated  employment, 
economic and social benefits.
The ScotZEB challenge fund focussed on supporting innovative business models designed 
around  the  characteristics  and  opportunities  presented  by  zero-emission  buses  and 
associated charging or refuelling infrastructure.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  scheme  is  administered  by  Transport  Scotland  under  Section  70  of  the  Transport  Scotland 
Act 2001.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Direct Grants.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 109 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided











Subsidy was available to bus operators, bus manufacturers, local authorities in Scotland, 
and finance companies (fleet companies) to acquire buses, which would be operated on 
passenger transport routes registered with the Traffic Commissioner for Scotland.
Applicants  applied  for  grant  funding  towards  the  vehicles  and  the  required  supporting 
infrastructure (hydrogen fuel cell and battery electric powertrains).
Applicants were challenged to compete for the lowest subsidy per-bus, which was a key 
metric that applications were scored against.
Applicants  were  unrestricted  in  their  choice  of  supplier  of  vehicles  and  much  of  the 
charging infrastructure (the area where they could not is the DNO – the owner of the local 
electricity grid – for "non-contestable works" as defined by OfGEM.
The funding was a grant payment to the applicant, upon delivery of the goods/completion 
of works.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

GBP 5.5m - 2023.

Infrastructure - ≤85% Hydrogen infrastructure/≤75% Electrical infrastructure



Infrastructure costs have many location-specific elements and are not comparable across 
sites. Many aspects of these works are controlled by the local grid operator (DNO) who 
was  not  restricted  in  their  choice  of  supplier  in  any  way  by  this  scheme.  Hydrogen 
infrastructure is significantly more expensive, therefore the intensity of support is higher 
in order to prevent exclusion of hydrogen technology as a choice for applicants.

Vehicles –

 ≤GBP 80,000 for a Zero Emission Bus (ZEB) with at least 9 passenger seats and at least 

one wheelchair accessible space.

 ≤GBP 140,000 for a ZEB with a total capacity for 32 or more passengers.
 ≤GBP 180,000 for a battery-electric ZEB with at least 45 seats and a maximum theoretical 

range of at least 310 km.

 ≤GBP 180,000 for a battery-electric ZEB with a total capacity for 60 or more passengers.
 ≤GBP  230,000  for  a  hydrogen  fuel-cell  ZEB  with  at  least  45  seats  and  a  maximum 

theoretical range of at least 310 km.

 ≤GBP  230,000  for  a  hydrogen  fuel-cell  ZEB  with  a  total  capacity  for  60  or  more 

passengers.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Phase  1  of  the  ScotZEB  Challenge  Fund  opened  to  applications  on  4  August  2021  and  closed  on 
9 December 2021. Due to timescales for installing supporting infrastructure, and the required lead-in 
time for OEMs, disbursement of funds for awards made under phase one ran until 31 March 2024.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy



Phase 1 of the Challenge Fund was worth GBP 62 million and supported the acquisition of 
272 zero emission buses and supporting infrastructure. All valid applications received for 
phase 1 utilised battery-electric technology.

 Of the buses awarded 25 were double-decker buses, which were all manufactured in the 
UK,  6  were  accessible  low-floor  minibuses  manufactured  in  the  EU,  4  were  mid-sized 
midibuses made in the UK, and the remaining 237 were single decker buses manufactured 
in the European Union and in the People's Republic of China.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 110 -

3  AGRICULTURE, FOOD AND FORESTRY

3.1  UK GOVERNMENT

3.1.1  FARMING INNOVATION SCHEMES

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Farming Innovation Schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the Farming Innovation Schemes is to stimulate innovation and develop approaches 
to  boost  agricultural  productivity  efficiencies  and  environmental  sustainability.  This  consists  of 
two separate schemes:




Farming Innovation Pathways [last projects closed April 2024].
Farming  Innovation  Programme  industry  led  R&D  Partnerships  Fund  and  the  Farming 
Futures R&D Fund.

The scheme is designed to:





Accelerate research and development of new agricultural solutions by actively engaging 
collaboration with the wider UK research community in the innovation process;
Investigate  early-stage  solutions  that  have  the  potential  to  substantially  improve  the 
overall productivity, sustainability and resilience of farming, and move existing agricultural 
sectors to net zero;

 Develop  solutions  with  the  potential  to  improve  overall  productivity,  sustainability, 





resilience, and move existing agricultural sectors to net zero;
Ensure solutions have positive outputs for farmers, growers or foresters in commercially 
relevant situations; and
Accelerate  adoption  by  ensuring  knowledge  exchange  with  the  wider  sector  and  other 
stakeholders.

The R&D funding streams are designed to deliver farmer led solutions to productivity challenges, as 
well as address longer term strategic challenges such as producing nutritious food more efficiently 
and sustainably, by helping the sector reduce greenhouse gas emissions to achieve Net Zero goals. 
It  aims  to  drive  the  development  and  adoption  of  new  technologies,  processes  and  practices  in 
agriculture, needed to secure sustainable food production into the future.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Farming Innovation Pathways scheme opened for applications 28 April 2021, with the Farming 
Innovation Programme opening 20 October 2021. There have been no changes to these schemes 
since  their  launch  but  Farming  Innovation  Pathways  scheme  is  now  closed  with  the  last  projects 
concluding in April 2024.

The  legal  authority  for  the  schemes  is  Section  5  of  the  Science  and  Technology  Act  1965.  The 
schemes  are  administered  by  UK  Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  with  the  Department  for 
Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) responsible for developing the policy.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Funding  is  provided  to  both  agricultural  producers,  and  non-agricultural  producers  involved  in 
collaborative  project  teams  for  research  and  development  of  new  technologies,  processes  and 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 111 -

practices to increase productivity and sustainability in the sector providing benefits to agriculture 
and agricultural producers. Funding is also available to other types of applicants, such as academic 
institutions,  research  organisations,  consultants,  research  &  technology  organisations  and  other 
agribusinesses.

Amount of support provided towards project costs varies by size of the applicant:





For feasibility or industrial research projects: Up to 70% of eligible costs for micro 
or small organisations, up to 60% for medium sized organisations, and up to 50% for large 
organisations;
For  experimental  development  projects:  45%  of  eligible  costs  for  micro  or  small 
organisations, 35% for medium sized organisations, and 25% for large organisations.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023

2024

Amount

GBP 10,872,623.69 

GBP 16,611,136.03  GBP 14,125.12 

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  Farming  Innovation  Pathways  scheme  launched  in  April  2021.  Projects  were  awarded  in 
October 2021, with funding available to projects for up to 24 months with some project extensions 
of up to 30 months.

The Farming Innovation Programme launched in October 2021 and, under its current schedule, will 
run until March 2028. The most recent competition was launched in May 2024.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.1.2  LEGACY  SCHEMES  UNDER  THE  COMMON  AGRICULTURAL  POLICY  EUROPEAN 
AGRICULTURAL GUARANTEE FUND (EAGF) 2014-2020

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Legacy schemes under the Common Agricultural Policy European Agricultural Guarantee Fund (EAGF 
- Direct aids and interventions in markets ("First Pillar")).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The EU Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) is a system of agricultural subsidies providing support for 
farming,  environmental  measures  and  rural  development.  The  European  Agricultural  Guarantee 
Fund  (EAGF)  forms  part  of  the  "First  Pillar"  of  the  EU  Common  Agricultural  Policy  and  relates  to 
direct aids and interventions in markets.

The  objective  of  the  EAGF  is  to  support  the  agricultural  sector  in  order  to  provide  farming 
communities  with  a  fair  standard  of  living,  supply  consumers  with  food  at  reasonable  prices  and 
promote the effective use of resources.

 
 
 
 
G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 112 -

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Since  the  UK  exited  the  European  Union  on  31  January  2020,  direct  aids  in  the  UK  for  the  2020 
scheme year, and subsequent scheme years, have been operated under domestic legislation rather 
than through the EAGF.

Ongoing  programmes  which  were  approved  before  31  January  2020  continue  to  be  implemented 
under  EU  legislation,  rather  than  retained  EU  law,  until  the  completion  of  those  programmes, 
pursuant  to  Article  138  of  the  UKEU  Withdrawal  Agreement.  For  Common  Organisation  of  the 
Markets  (CMO)  schemes,  these  will  continue  in  the  UK's  Devolved  Administrations,  using 
EU legislation  for  current  programmes  and  then  switching  to  domestic  (retained)  legislation  once 
current  programmes  end.  This  covers  operational  programmes  in  the  fruit  and  vegetable  sector, 
apiculture and promotions.

Legal authority for the payments:

 Direct Payments to Farmers (Legislative Continuity) Act 2020 ("DPFLC Act 2020");


The  retained  EU  legislation  (EU  Regulation  1307/2013)  as  incorporated  by  the 
DPFLC Act 2020.
From  1  Jan  2021,  ongoing  CMO  programmes  have  continued  to  operate  under 
EU regulation  1308/2013  (pursuant  to  Article  138  of  the  UKEU  Withdrawal  Agreement) 
and any new programmes have been covered by retained EU Regulation 1308/2013.



Implementation of the schemes in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland is the responsibility of the 
respective Devolved Administrations.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Payments  are  made  to  businesses  operating  within  the  agricultural  sector,  with  the  majority  of 
payments provided to agricultural producers.

Payments under the EAGF are provided across three broad areas:

Direct Payments – This includes the Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) which offers a basic layer of 
income  support  to  farmers  that  is  decoupled  from  production;  greening  payments  aimed  at 
benefiting the environment; and payments to young farmers (no more than 40 years of age) to help 
support the setting up of agricultural holdings. To receive full payment, applicants must also meet 
minimum  standards  on  the  environment,  animal  and  plant  health  and  animal  welfare.  Direct 
Payments are paid annually.

Market interventions (including CMO) – Aim is to stabilise markets and assure the agricultural 
population  of  an  equitable  standard  of  living.  Payments  include  contributions  towards  improving 
product  quality,  promoting  environmental  measures,  crisis  prevention  and  management,  product 
acquisition and storage (mainly milk and dairy products) and promotion of agricultural products.

Export refunds - No export licenses have been granted since 2013.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  current  direct  payment  schemes  have  operated  since  2015  and  direct  payments  continue. 
However, the Basic Payment Scheme and Young Farmer Payment ended in England at the end of 
the 2023 scheme year, replaced by a new type of direct payment (delinked payments).

For CMO, these schemes will continue in the UK's Devolved Administrations, using EU legislation for 
current programmes and then switching to domestic (retained) legislation once current programmes 
end.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.1.3  LEGACY  SCHEMES  UNDER  THE  COMMON  AGRICULTURAL  POLICY  EUROPEAN 
AGRICULTURAL FUND FOR RURAL DEVELOPMENT (EAFRD)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Legacy  schemes  under  the  Common  Agricultural  Policy  European  Agricultural  Fund  for  Rural 
Development  (EAFRD),  which  were  implemented  under  the  2014-2020  Rural  Development 
Programme  for  England  (RDPE);  Welsh  Government  Rural  Communities  Rural  Development  Plan 
2014-2020; Northern Ireland Rural Development Programme 2014-2020; and for Scotland under 
the 2014-2020 Scottish Rural Development Programme (SRDP).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The EU Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) is a system of agricultural subsidies providing support for 
farming, environmental measures and rural development.

The  European  Agricultural  Fund  for  Rural  Development  (EAFRD)  forms  the  "Second  Pillar"  of  the 
CAP, related to implementation of rural development programmes.

The aim of the EAFRD is to support rural development programmes which aim to achieve at least 
four of the six key priorities:










Foster knowledge transfer and innovation in agriculture, forestry and rural areas;
Enhance  viability  /  competitiveness  of  all  types  of  agriculture,  promote  innovation  and 
sustainable forest management;
Promote food chain organisation, animal welfare and risk management in agriculture;
Restore, preserve and enhance ecosystems related to agriculture and forestry;
Promote resource efficiency and shift towards low carbon and climate resilient economy in 
the agriculture, food and forestry sectors;
Promote social inclusion, poverty reduction and economic development in rural areas.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal authority for this scheme:






the  European  Parliament  and 

EU  Regulation  1305/2013  of 
the  Council  of 
17 December 2013 on support for rural development by the European Agricultural Fund 
for Rural Development (EAFRD);
Title III of Party 3 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU);
The  Common  Agricultural  Policy  (Competent  Authority  and  Coordinating  Body) 
Regulations 2014  and  the  Common  Agricultural  Policy  (Control  and  Enforcement, 
CrossCompliance,  Scrutiny  of  Transactions  and  Appeals)  Regulations  2014.  EU  law 
continues 
the 
20142020 Multilateral  Financial  Framework  (MFF)  by  virtue  of  Article  138  of  the 
Withdrawal Agreement;

schemes  approved  under 

to  Rural  Development 

to  apply 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 114 -



The Rural Development Programme (Transfer) (England) Regulations 2018.

Implementation of the programme and the selection of individual projects in Scotland, Wales and 
Northern Ireland  is  the  responsibility  of  the  respective  Devolved  Administrations,  with  the 
Department  for  Environment,  Food  and  Rural  Affairs  (DEFRA)  being  the  Managing  Authority  in 
England.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Financial assistance provided to businesses within the agriculture, food and forestry sectors. This 
includes farmers, land managers, forest holders, SMEs and rural businesses.

Eligibility criteria for funding through rural development programmes is dependent on the type of 
assistance, the sector / subsector in question and the types of activities involved.

Electronic  bank  transfer  payments  are  made  to  agreement  holders  that  deliver  prescribed 
agrienvironment/forestry  or  socioeconomic  activities.  Claims  for  capital  items  are  paid  in  arrears 
based on evidence of expenditure incurred; revenue activities are paid in annual instalments.

3.1.4  TRANSFORMING FOOD PRODUCTION

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Transforming Food Production

UKRI (UK Research & Innovation) previously delivered its subsidies under one overarching umbrella 
scheme. This came to an end for new competitions on 31 December 2022. From January 2023 new 
subsidy schemes were created to align with the UK Subsidy Control Act 2022. This return includes 
both subsidies delivered under the umbrella scheme and under the new schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Innovate UK's activities in the agricultural and food production areas are based around the critical 
need to develop technologies, systems and processes that will continue to provide highly productive 
agricultural and food systems, and rapidly transform them into low emission, sustainable systems. 
Supporting new ways to produce food that reduce emissions and pollution and contribute to feeding 
a growing world population.

The aim is to:


 minimise waste
improve soil


produce resilient and sustainable food more efficiently
reduce emissions and pollution

The benefits of this funding are varied, the list below is not exhaustive:



 Higher levels of engagement between farmers, growers and foresters with the UK research 
base. This should lead to more effective allocation of R&D funds to focus on areas that are 
important to farmers as well as development of products and services that meet farmer, 
grower etc needs and are therefore more likely to be taken up and used.
Establishment of sustainable, resilient and healthy agricultural and food production supply 
chains,  which  can  decarbonise  other  sectors  through  circular  economy  approaches  and 
co-location of food production adjacent to other industries
Increased skills and capabilities in key thematic areas across supply chain  
Increased private business investment in Agri-Tech R&D 
Enhanced knowledge exchange (research base, business, end-user, supply chain, policy 
makers)





 New precision agricultural technologies, solutions, services developed 
 New precision agricultural solutions adopted by farmers and end-users

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Innovate UK, the UK's innovation agency, is an executive non-departmental public body sponsored 
by  the  Department  for  Science,  Innovation  and  Technology.  Innovate  UK  are  now  part  of 
UK Research  and  Innovation  (UKRI),  which  was  formed  on  1  April  2018,  bringing  together 
seven research councils, and the research and knowledge exchange functions of the Higher Funding 
Council for England.

The legal basis of the scheme is Section 3 of the Research of the Higher Education and Research 
Act 2017.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 116 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Eligible Applicants for Innovate UK funding includes businesses, public sector, research technology 
organisations, academic institutions. Only funding provided to businesses is counted for the purpose 
of subsidy reporting and reported here. For details of eligibility please see our published guidance 
for applicants via Guidance for applicants – Innovate UK – UKRI.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  on  the  basis  of  actual  costs  incurred  and  reimbursed  at  the  aid 
intensity  fixed  at  the  time  of  the  award.  Projects  are  reviewed  every  quarter  by  the  responsible 
monitoring officer, with all finances and checks requiring sign-off before any payments are approved 
and released. Payments are provided via electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 18,410,421.85
2024 - GBP 29,317,824.10

8.

Duration of the subsidy

16/11/2022-16/11/2028

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.2  ENGLAND

3.2.1  DELINKED PAYMENTS

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Delinked payments.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Delinked payments will be made in England in place of payments under the Basic Payment Scheme 
(BPS)  (including  the  Young  Farmer  Payment)  in  England.  Delinked  payments  are  being  made  in 
England, and phased out over time, to help the agricultural industry adjust to the withdrawal of the 
Basic Payment Scheme (BPS). The 2023 scheme year was the last year of BPS in England.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Delinked payments were introduced in 2024.

The legal authority for the payments:






The Agriculture Act 2020 – section 12(1);
The  Agriculture  (Delinked  Payments  and  Consequential  Provisions)  (England) 
Regulations 2023.
The Agriculture (Delinked Payments) (Reductions) (England) Regulations 2024.

Delinked  payments  are  administered  by  the  Rural  Payments  Agency  (RPA)  on  behalf  of  the 
Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 117 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Delinked payments are made each year to eligible former recipients of BPS (plus some inheritance 
cases) in England. DEFRA expects that the majority of recipients will be agricultural producers.

The payment is calculated based on the recipient's reference amount, which is usually the average 
of their BPS payments in a historic reference period. Reductions are expected to be applied to the 
payments each year as the payments are phased out.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 0
2024 Total: GBP 808,060,000

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Delinked payments are due to be made each year during the period 2024 to 2027.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.2.2  FARMING IN PROTECTED LANDSCAPES PROGRAMME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Farming in Protected Landscapes Programme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Farming in Protected Landscapes programme (FiPL) provides grant funding for farmers and land 
managers  to  work  in  partnership  with  National  Parks  and  National  Landscape  organisations  in 
England  ("Protected  Landscapes")  to  deliver  projects  that  achieve  positive  outcomes  for  climate, 
nature, people and place.

The  programme  is  part  of  Defra's  response  to  the  Landscapes  Review  and  contributes  to  the 
government's commitment to protect 30% of land by 2030 and help the UK hit net zero by 2050 
with nature-based solutions.

All  approved  projects  deliver  activities  and  outputs  contributing  towards  at  least  one  of  the 
four themes and outcomes of the programme:

Climate outcomes:

 more carbon being stored, sequestered or both



reduced flood risk
a better understanding among farmers, land managers and the public as to what different 
habitats and land uses can deliver for carbon storage and reduced carbon emissions
a landscape more resilient to climate change



Nature outcomes:






a greater area of wildlife-rich habitat
greater connectivity between habitats
better management of existing habitats for biodiversity
increased biodiversity

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 118 -

People outcomes:

 more opportunities for diverse audiences to explore, enjoy and understand the landscape


greater public engagement in land management, for example through volunteering

Place outcomes:





enhancing or reinforcing the quality and character of the landscape
historic structures and features being conserved, enhanced or interpreted more effectively
an  increase  in  the  resilience  of  nature-friendly  sustainable  farm  businesses,  which 
contributes  to  a  more  thriving  local  economy  (this  must  be  delivered  alongside  other 
activities that deliver for the other programme outcomes)

Examples of project activities eligible to be funded include:










promoting connectivity between habitats to support wildlife and species recovery
replacing stiles with gates on public footpaths, for easier access
conserving historic features on a farm, such as lime kilns or lead mining heritage
projects  that  support  a  locally  branded  food  initiative,  promoting  the  links  between  the 
product and the landscape in which it is produced
actions to reduce carbon emissions on a farm
educational access to understand farming and land management
gathering data and evidence to help inform long-term conservation and farming practice

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The FiPL programme was launched in July 2021.

Protected  Landscapes  organisations  (PLOs)  in  England  –  including  9  National  Parks,  34  National 
Landscapes, and The Broads Authority – are the delivery partners for the programme, responsible 
for the application, approval and monitoring of projects in their respective areas.

The programme uses different legislative powers to deliver the programme for the different Protected 
Landscapes.

For  National  Landscapes,  Section  98  of  the  Natural  Environment  and  Rural  Communities  (NERC) 
Act 2006 has been applied. Section 98 of NERC enables the Secretary of State to provide financial 
assistance in respect of expenditure incurred or to be incurred in any matter related to or connected 
with a Defra function, subject to any conditions specified.

For National Parks, Section 72 of the Environment Act 1995 has been applied. Section 72 enables 
the  Secretary  of  State  to  make  grants  to  a  National  Park  authority  for  such  purposes,  of  such 
amounts and on such terms and conditions as he thinks fit.

For the Broads Authority, Section 15 of the Norfolk and Suffolk Broads Act 1988 as amended by the 
NERC Act 2006 has been applied.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

FiPL is open to all farmers and land managers near and within a National Landscape or National Park 
in England, or the Broads. This includes farmers and land managers from the public, private and 
charity sector. Approved projects and their successful applicants will receive FiPL grant funding to 
deliver project activities.

To be eligible, the project proposal must deliver towards the programme themes (nature, climate, 
people, place) and deliver towards the PLO management plans. PLOs will assess each project against 
application  criteria  outlined  in  the  programme's  National  Framework  guidance  documentation, 
including Project Outcomes, VfM, Ability to Deliver, and Sustainability/Legacy.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 119 -

The  mechanism  in  which  the  subsidy  is  provided  is  Direct  Lump  Sum  to  the  PLOs  to  award  and 
administer  the  funding  to  farmers  and  land  managers  with  approved  projects  as  onward  grants. 
PLOs will then provide monitoring data for these projects on Defra objectives and VFM criteria to 
support our policy objectives whilst providing a clear audit for how the money is spent.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

FiPL project grant awards will vary depending on the size of the project application, ranging from 
under GBP 5,000 to up to GBP 250,000. All PLOs are responsible for managing their individual FiPL 
annual  allocations,  calculated  through  a  funding  formula  using  farmed  area  and  no.  of  holdings, 
population and socio-economic measures.

The PLOs use their allocations: (1) to employ FiPL Farm Technical Advisers to provide advice and 
guidance to potential applicants, engage with their local communities and oversee overall project 
delivery; (2) to provide administrative support to process applications and manage project onward 
grant  agreements  and  payments;  and  (3)  to  award  FiPL  project  grants  to  successful  applicants 
through onward grant agreements.

Over the UK Financial Year 2022-2023, GBP 17.1m was spent.
Over the UK Financial Year 2023-2024, GBP 22.7m was spent.
Over the UK Financial Year 2024-2025, GBP 43.5m was spent.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

FiPL  has  been  extended  by  an  additional  year  with  additional  funding  for  project  delivery  and  is 
currently expected to end on 31 March 2026.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.2.3  FARMING INVESTMENT FUND (FIF)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

The Farming Investment Fund (FIF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  Farming  Investment  Fund  (FIF)  provides  support  for  farmers,  growers,  foresters  and  their 
contractors  to  invest  in  their  businesses  to  improve  productivity  efficiencies  and  enhance  the 
environment. The level of support is based on an intervention rate of 40% to 60% for the majority 
of funds.

There are two strands to the fund:

1.  The  Farming  Equipment  and  Technology  Fund  (FETF)  –  (GBP  1K  -  GBP  50K)  is  based  on  a 
prescribed  list  of  items  that  support  the  purchase  of  specific  equipment,  technology,  and  small 
infrastructure investments aimed at making an immediate difference to farm performance, including 
investments that help farmers use less inputs, reduce emissions, and cut waste.

2. Large grants – [PKA the Farming Transformation Fund (FTF)] – (GBP 15K - GBP 500k) supports 
higher-value more complex investments, with the potential to bring transformational improvements 
to  business  performance. The  Fund  is  based  on  a  set  of  themes  which  broadly  represent  a 
continuation of the themes from the previous Rural Development Programme.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 120 -

 Water Management

Grants are offered towards the costs of construction of on farm water storage reservoirs (above or 
below ground) filled by either peak flow surface water abstraction, borehole or rainwater harvested 
from buildings. The scheme also offers support for on farm water infrastructure.



Improving Farm Productivity

Grants are offered to help improve farm productivity including for robotic and automated equipment 
to aid crop and livestock production, harvesting, grading and storage. Also supports solar equipment 
for installation on rooftops and floating on irrigation reservoirs.



Adding Value

These grants are to improve the processing of primary agricultural products to shorten supply chains 
and  deliver  benefits  to  primary  producers.  Grants  for  processing  equipment  and  machinery, 
innovative  technologies  and  the  construction  or  improvement  of  buildings  associated  with  adding 
value in the processing of agricultural products.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  Agriculture  Act  2020  provides  the  legislative  framework  for  replacement  agricultural  support 
schemes  in  the  UK.  It  replaces  the  Common  Agricultural  Policy  (CAP)  and  focuses  on  rewarding 
farmers and land managers for producing "public goods" such as environmental or animal welfare 
improvements.

Launch  of  FIF  was  in  November  2021.  There  have  been  some  changes  over  time  including 
intervention rates and maximum grant amounts.

Administered by the Rural Payments Agency (RPA) – RPA is an executive agency of Department of 
Environment and Rural Affairs (Defra).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants at an intervention rate of 40% to 60% towards the cost of equipment and infrastructure.

Farming  Equipment  and  Technology  Fund  grants  range  from  GBP  1000  to  GBP  25,000  per 
applicant. In 2024 the maximum grant amount was raised to GBP 50,000 as a one-off change.

Large grant amounts range from (minimum) GBP 15k to GBP 500k (maximum) dependent on the 
respective theme. Grants are paid to the recipients at an intervention rate of between 40% to 60% 
of the cost of equipment after it has been purchased.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Agriculture, Forestry and Horticultural production and contractors to those sectors are eligible for 
the grants.

Support is provided for starting, or improving the productivity or environmental sustainability of, an 
agricultural, horticultural or forestry activity, or for supporting ancillary services carried on, or to be 
carried on, by or for a producer.

7.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  Farming  Equipment  &  Technology  Fund  will  launch  for  new  applications  in  Spring  2025  with 
grants aiming to be paid by the end of the financial year in March 2026.

The  latest  round  of  the  large  grants  Improving  Farm  Productivity  closes  for  full  applications  in 
July 2025. (All other large grant offers are now closed to new applications). Grant payments under 
all three larger grant themes will continue for several years as there is a long implementation phase 
for larger infrastructure projects such as irrigation reservoirs.

Any new grant launches beyond the 2025/26 financial year are subject to budget allocation in the 
Spending review for the 2026-2029 period.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.2.4  FARMING RECOVERY FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Farming Recovery Fund 2024.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The purpose of the support is to help farmers in England recover from the impacts on farmland of 
the extreme wet weather and flooding in winter 2023/24. The payment is a contribution towards the 
cost of restoring this land to productive agricultural use.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal authority for the scheme is Section 98 of the Natural Environment and Rural Communities 
Act  2006.  The  scheme  is  administered  by  the  Rural  Payments  Agency  (RPA),  on  behalf  of  the 
Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 122 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Payments are made to eligible farmers in England. To be eligible for payment the farmer must:





have  eligible  land  next  to  a  main  river  in  England  which  had  a  high  river  level  gauge 
reading during Storm Babet and/or Storm Henk (the two biggest storms in England during 
winter 2023/24); and/or
have eligible land in an area of exceptional rainfall in England over the period October 2023 
to March 2024. To account for variances in the extent of the rainfall and its impact at a 
local level, only 30% of this land is counted as eligible for payment.

Payments were made in bands, based on the total number of eligible hectares the farmer had. The 
minimum payment was GBP 2,895 and the maximum payment was GBP 25,000.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: 0
2024 Total: GBP 57,200,000

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Payments were made from July 2024 to May 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.2.5  FUTURE FARMING RESILIENCE FUND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Future Farming Resilience Fund.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of this scheme is to support farmers and land managers in England during the early 
years of the agricultural transition, specifically the phasing out of Direct Payments. It does this by 
awarding grants to organisations which provide help to farmers and land managers to:

a. Understand the changes that are happening;
b.
c. Access tailored support to adapt.

Identify how, what and when they may need to adapt their business models;

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Direct  Payments,  originally  paid  through  the  Basic  Payment  Scheme  (BPS),  were  planned  to  be 
phased  out  in  England  by  2027.  This  will  impact  every  farming  business  in  England  currently 
receiving BPS. The Future Farming Resilience Fund was created by the Department for Environment, 
Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) to help farmers through the transition away from Direct Payments.

The scheme launched in October 2020, and consists of 3 phases: Initial Phase, Interim phase; and 
Scale up phase. The initial phase ran from February 2020 to March 2021, reaching 1,200 participants 
through  9  providers.  The  Interim  phase  ran  from  August  2021  to  September  2022  and  reached 
7,000  participants  through  19  providers.  The  Scale  up  phase,  with  a  budget  of  GBP  32,000,000, 
started in October 2022 and is expected to run until March 2025 with the goal of providing resilience 
support to up to 32,000 participants.

The fundamentals of the scheme have not changed, but minor amendments have been made to the 
policy design since the Initial Phase through to the Scale Up Phase. These are mainly around ensuring 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 123 -

trusted  local  providers  deliver  the  support  and  that  1  2  1  delivery  is  widely  available  to  support 
access.

The legal authority for the scheme:




Section 1(2) of the Agriculture Act 2020;
Section 98 of the Natural Environment & Rural Communities (NERC) Act 2006.

The scheme is administered by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The grants are paid to third party providers (business support experts) to deliver support free of 
charge to farmers and land managers. This support is available to farmers and land managers in 
England who received Direct Payments under the Basic Payment Scheme (BPS).

The grants are paid to the providers in arrears on a quarterly basis once they have provided sufficient 
evidence of spend/activity within the period.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 8,570,000
2024 Total: GBP 13,050,000

8.

Duration of the subsidy

February 2020 – March 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.2.6  LUMP SUM EXIT SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Lump Sum Exit Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Lump Sum Exit Scheme support was given to help farmers in England who wished to retire or 
leave agricultural production, to be able to do so in a planned and manageable manner.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The application period for the scheme was from May 2022 to September 2022.

The legal authority for the scheme:




The Agriculture Act 2020, Section 13(1);
The Agriculture (Lump Sum Payment) (England) Regulations 2022.

The Lump Sum Exit Scheme operates in England. The scheme is administered by the Rural Payments 
Agency (RPA), on behalf of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA).

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 124 -

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  Lump  Sum  Exit  Scheme  was  targeted  at  farmers  in  England  who  wished  to  retire  or  leave 
agricultural production.

The lump sum is paid once the applicant has provided evidence that they have met the eligibility 
requirements as follows: 







Recipients of the lump sum must have claimed, and been eligible for, payments under the 
Basic Payment Scheme (BPS) in 2018 or earlier; 
Recipients must have transferred their agricultural land in England (with limited exceptions 
for up to 5 hectares of that land and for any land planted with trees under certain woodland 
creation schemes); 
The  farm  business  that  receives  the  lump  sum  must  have  surrendered  the  English  BPS 
payment entitlements they hold and are not eligible for any further BPS payments or any 
delinked payments (which replaced BPS payments in England in 2024) in England.

If recipients wish to claim further agricultural land management payments in England before 2028, 
they must first repay their lump sum.

The payment value is based on the payments made to the applicant under the BPS in a reference 
period. The maximum lump sum is around GBP 100,000.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 17,660,000
2024 Total: GBP 3,590,000

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme launched in May 2022 and closed for applications in September 2022. Applicants had 
until May 2024 to fulfil all the eligibility conditions. Payments are made once the eligibility conditions 
have been met.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

The scheme is not considered to be trade distorting. Around 1,200 applicants successfully provided 
evidence  they  had  fulfilled  all  the  eligibility  conditions.  This  represents  around  1.5%  of  the 
85,000 farmers in England who used to claim under the BPS each year.

3.2.7  NATURE FOR CLIMATE FUND PROGRAMME (NCF)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Nature for Climate Fund Programme (NCF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The primary objective of the Nature for Climate Fund (NCF) programme is to increase tree planting 
and peatland restoration in England to tackle the global crises of climate change and biodiversity 
loss in the terrestrial environment.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 125 -

The programme, delivered through a number of different workstreams, will also deliver a wide range 
of additional environmental, social and economic benefits.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The legal authority for the programme:

Environmental Protection Act 1990, section 153;
Forestry Act 1967;
Forestry Act 1979;
Local Government Act 2003, section 31:





 Natural Environment and Rural Communities Act 2006;


Environment Act 2021.

The NCF programme is overseen by the Department for Environment Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) 
and delivered by multiple third parties, including:

Forestry Commission;


 Natural England;
 National Forest Company;

 Woodland Trust;


England's Community Forests;

Cornwall and Northumberland County Councils.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Grants are provided to landowners for tree planting and peatland restoration. Applications are made 
by  the  landowner  to  the  relevant  delivery  partner  for  assessment  against  given  criteria  for  the 
specific scheme. Defra transfers funding to the relevant body that is responsible for receiving and 
evaluating applications, and for making payments to successful applicants.

Individual  grants  are  provided  to  the  applicant  through  an  agreed  contract.  Woodland  Creation 
contracts have an obligation to maintain the trees for an agreed period of time to ensure they reach 
maturity, but requirements can vary between schemes.

The  payments  made  under  these  measures  do  not  have  the  effect  of  providing  price  support  to 
producers.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The total CDEL (Capital Departmental Expenditure Limit) budget over the life of the programme is 
GBP 957,000,000. The life of the programme is longer than the notification period and the figure 
also includes enabling activities and measures not in scope of this notification.

The total budgets (in GBP) for the measures in scope for the notification period have been included 
below. These values represent the full value of the grant being given.

a. DEFRA led programme workstreams

3.2.8  SUSTAINABLE FARMING INCENTIVE

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Sustainable Farming Initiative.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Sustainable Farming Incentive (SFI) is designed to encourage farmers to adopt practices that 
protect  and  enhance  the  natural  environment.  By  participating  in  the  SFI,  farmers  contribute  to 
improving soil health, increasing biodiversity, and reducing carbon emissions, all of which are crucial 
for long-term environmental sustainability.

The  SFI  offered  a  range  of  actions  that  farmers  can  undertake  to  promote  sustainable  land 
management. These actions include no tillage farming, precision farming, and planting cover crops, 
which help to improve soil structure, reduce erosion, and enhance water quality. Additionally, the 
scheme supports agroforestry and other practices that increase biodiversity and create habitats for 
wildlife.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 127 -

Farmers receive payments for participating in the SFI based on the actions they undertake and the 
area of land involved.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Section 1 of the Agriculture Act 2020;
The Agriculture (Financial Assistance) Regulations 2021;
The Agriculture (Financial Assistance) (Amendment) Regulations 2022.

The Sustainable Farming Incentive opened in June 2022.

The Rural Payments Agency is responsible for administrating the scheme.

The Sustainable Farming Incentive scheme 2023 (SFI23) was launched in October 2023. SFI23 was 
closed to new agreements in June 2024

The SFI Expanded Offer opened on 31 May 2024 to a restricted group, with open access to all eligible 
from  July  2024.  This  scheme  was  larger  in  scale,  with  more  actions  included.  The  format  of  the 
scheme remained the same, and payment rates were based on income foregone and costs incurred 
throughout.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Recipients of SFI agreements are farmers or land managers entered

The Sustainable Farming Incentive provides annual revenue grants, over 3-year agreements. Some 
5-year agreements are in place where actions that last 5 years are chosen.

Under an SFI agreement, the annual payment value is paid in quarterly instalments.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Over the UK Financial Year 2023-2024, GBP 30m was spent.

Over the UK Financial Year 2024-2025, GBP 305m was spent.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme closed in March 2025. It will reopen in early 2026.

Payments will continue to be made under existing SFI agreements until 2030.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 128 -

3.3  SCOTLAND

3.3.1  FOOD PROCESSING MARKETING AND CO-OPERATION (FPMC)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Food Processing, Marketing and Co-operation (FPMC) Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Food Processing Marketing and Co-operation (FPMC) Grants Scheme can provide support grant 
to  suppliers  and  producers  to  contribute  to  the  government's  overall  vision  for  food  and  drink  in 
Scotland, which is to be a Good Food Nation, where it is second nature to produce, buy, serve and 
eat fresh, healthy food. The food and drink sector can also help to make Scotland healthier, wealthier 
and  smarter,  with  stronger  communities  and  production  that  is  socially  and  environmentally 
sustainable.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  scheme  is  administered  by  Scottish  Government  under  legal  basis  of  European  Communities 
Act 1972  –  Section  2  and  Rural  Development  (Scotland)  Regulations  2015.  The  scheme  was 
previously registered  via the European Commission,  with  grant funding being  provided under  the 
(SRDP)  2014-2020, 
legislative  authority  of  Scottish  Rural  Development  Programme 
EU Regulation 1305/2013 and EU Regulation 651/2014.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The assistance takes the form of direct grants. Financial assistance is provided through conditionally 
repayable contributions to commercial (i.e. private sector) food and drink businesses and food and 
drink organisations.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  Food  Processing  Marketing  and  Co-operation  (FPMC)  Grant  Scheme  provides  assistance  for 
food/drink  businesses  in  Scotland  with  start-up  grants  and  also  development  grants  for  existing 
food/drink businesses in Scotland.

Capital Projects: develop or create food processing facilities, including buildings and equipment.

Non-Capital Projects:

 market  their  products  in  home  and  export  markets  at national/international  trade 

shows/fairs:
to  run  co-operative  ventures  to  make  sure  more  value  is  retained by  both  farmers  and 
growers:
to improve supply-chain efficiency.





7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 7.039m
2024 - GBP 3.247m

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme runs from 01 January 2021 until 31 March 2030.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 129 -

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.3.2  FORESTRY GRANT SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Forestry Grant Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Forestry Grant Scheme (FGS) supports the forestry sector in Scotland by providing a range of 
grants  for  woodland  creation  and  the  sustainable  management  of  existing  woodlands.  The 
Scottish Government has set out a vision for forestry through the Scottish Forestry Strategy – this 
recognises that forestry is a long-term activity and that forests should be sustainably managed to 
provide economic, environmental and social benefits. FGS provides the following range of grants:

 Woodland Creation Grants - this is the main grant with a key objective of expanding new 
woodlands to contribute to climate change mitigation and helping to slow the increase of 
carbon  dioxide  in  the  atmosphere.  The  annual  target  was  10,000  hectares  with  step 
increases to 18,000 ha by 2024/25 depending upon funding availability from the SG. In 
years 2023 and 2024, there was significant budget pressures and targets were revised in 
the annual SG's Programme For Government.
Tree  Health  Grants  – aim  is  to  support  the  protection  of  woodland  from  the  impacts  of 
non-routine tree pests or diseases.



 Woodland Improvement Grants - aim is to support forest management, activity to enhance 



the environment and the public's enjoyment of existing woodlands.
Sustainable Management of Forest Grants - aim is to support the management of existing 
forests and woodlands with a high environmental value

 Harvesting and Processing Grant – aim is to support the development of the small-scale 
premium  softwood  and  hardwood  processing  sector  through  funding  for  equipment  to 
increase harvesting in small and undermanaged woods.
Forest  Infrastructure  Grant  –  aim  is  to  support  forest  access  in  small-scale  or 
undermanaged woodlands.



4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Scottish Forestry is the body delegated to administer the Forestry Grant Scheme (FGS) under the 
regulations  of  the  2014-2020  Scottish  Rural  Development  Programme  (SRDP)  and,  for,  contracts 
approved  post  January  2021  under  the  regulations  of  Agriculture  (Retained  EU  Law  and  Data) 
(Scotland) Act 2020.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The assistance takes the form of contributions to costs through the offer of direct grants.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Private  landowners  such  as  farmers,  private  forest  owners  and  small  and  medium  scale  forest 
businesses. Grants are based on 80-100% of 'standard costs' which sets out the average cost for 
carrying  out  a  forest  activity.  The  standard  costs  have  been  independently  verified.  Projects  are 
selected through a competitive monthly process.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 - GBP 57,700,000
2024 - GBP 31,700,000

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 130 -

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The  programme  started  on  26  May  2015  under  EU  regulations  and  will  continue  until 
31 December 2029  under  Agriculture  (Retained  EU  Law  and  Data)  (Scotland)  Act  2020  or  earlier 
(circa 2028) if a new revised approach to forestry grant support is developed under the new legal 
basis of The Agriculture and Rural Communities (Scotland) Act 2024. Individual grants are provided 
through an agreed contract with the woodland owner or forestry business. FGS Woodland Creation 
contracts  have  an  obligation  to  maintain  works  for  up  to  20  years.  Other  FGS  contracts  have  an 
obligation to maintain works up to 5-10 years.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.3.3  HIE AGRICULTURE, FOOD, AND DRINK SUBSIDY

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Highlands and Islands Enterprise Agriculture, Food and Drink Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the scheme is to assist businesses to increase their contribution to employment and 
Gross Value Added in the economy by investing in business growth.

This  scheme  provides  support  for  growers  and  producers  within  the  agriculture,  horticulture  and 
forestry sectors and the processing of food products. This also includes seaweed, whether naturally 
harvested or farmed. Support applies to the following sub-sectors:






agricultural and forestry land use (primary production of farm products and timber)
timber harvesting
first stage of processing of agricultural products
food and drink production

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Highlands and Islands Enterprise (HIE), which administers the scheme, was established to promote 
the economic and social development of the HIE area, to enhance employment and business skills, 
improve industrial efficiency, international competitiveness, and the environment to achieve these.

Legal basis for the scheme is the Enterprise and New Town (Scotland) Act 1990, as amended on 
1 April 2001 by the Scottish Statutory Instrument 2001 No 126.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants, interest subsidy, loans.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The scheme is available to all sizes of enterprises who are producers in the permitted sub-sectors. 
To be eligible for the scheme, applicants must be undertaking new development in the qualifying 
area (the Highlands and Islands region of Scotland).

The amount of support is calculated to provide the minimum necessary of public funding to achieve 
the aims of an agreed business plan. This is allocated to capital investment, product development, 
skills development strategic planning and market awareness. Support is not allocated on the basis 
of product prices, product costs or the quantities produced or exported.

3.3.4  SOSE GRAHAMSLAW DISTILLERY STANDALONE AWARD

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Standalone  Award  to  The  Grahamslaw  Distillery  Hold  Co  Limited  by  South  of  Scotland  Enterprise 
(SOSE).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

SOSE, launched in April 2020, is the economic and community development agency responsible for 
Dumfries & Galloway and the Scottish Borders. The primary policy objective is in line with SOSE's 
purposes and functions to drive inclusive growth, increase competitiveness and innovation, tackle 
inequality  within  the  region,  and  to  establish  the  South  of  Scotland  as  a  centre  of  opportunity, 
innovation, and growth. In addition, it helps meet Scottish Government's Net Zero commitments.

The  purpose  of  the  subsidy  aims  to  increase  productivity  and  reduce  energy  consumption  and 
emissions at the Grahamslaw Farm, Kelso.

The project will significantly reduce the energy used in production and resultant carbon footprint. 
The project will also substantially increase the sites production capabilities, unlocking future growth 
both within the UK and for overseas markets.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Scheme is provided under the legislative authority of South of Scotland Enterprise Act 2019. 
Scheme is implemented by South of Scotland Enterprise (SOSE). SOSE, launched in April 2020, is 
the  economic  and  community  development  agency  responsible  for  Dumfries  &  Galloway  and  the 
Scottish Borders. This standalone subsidy was awarded on 05 December 2023.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Direct Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The Grahamslaw Distillery Hold Co Limited. Lump sum.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

GBP 1,344,784 in total.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 132 -

8.

Duration of the subsidy

One-off standalone award, granted on 05 December 2023.

9.
