No statistical data is available.

3.4  NORTHERN IRELAND

3.4.1  EXCEPTIONAL COSTS PIG SUPPORT SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Exceptional Costs Pig Support Scheme (Northern Ireland) 2022.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

One-off  decoupled  income  support  payment  to  assist  with  exceptional  production  costs  (feed, 
energy, labour and transport costs, as well as higher interest rates), providing a lump sum payment 
to  eligible  pig  producers  for  support  to  the  Northern  Ireland  pig  agricultural  market  arising  from 
exceptional market circumstances.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme was created and administered by DAERA (Department for Agriculture, Environment and 
Rural Affairs of Northern Ireland). The legal basis from which the scheme was made was from the 
powers under paragraph 8 of Part 2 of Schedule 6 to the Agriculture Act 2020. The scheme was only 
open for a short period of time from 7 to 21 February 2023 and there were no changes to the scheme 
while it was open.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant – One off decoupled income support payment.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Support was provided by way of a one-off lump sum payment to eligible pig producers for support 
to Northern Ireland pig agricultural market. Eligibility conditions focused on pigs born and reared in 
Northern  Ireland  and  marketed  (Marketed  pigs  are  defined  as  those  slaughtered  including  totally 
condemned,  dead  on  arrival  and  under/overweight  at  three  named  abattoirs)  through  a 
Northern Ireland processing plant from April 2022 to June 2022 are eligible for support, with support 
capped per farm business.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 1,591,849

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme was open from 7 February to 21 February 2023.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 133 -

3.4.2  FARM BUSINESS IMPROVEMENT SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Farm Business Improvement Scheme – Capital Investment.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2024. 2023 expenditure not reported as it was paid under EU funding.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Provision of capital grant support for investment projects, seeking to support farm businesses within 
the agriculture and horticulture sectors.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme was created and administered by Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural 
Affairs (DAERA) Northern Ireland. The legal basis from which the scheme was made was from the 
powers  within  The  Rural  Development  Programme  Regulations  (Northern Ireland)  2015 
(S.R.2015No.326);  and  EU  Retained  Law:  Regulation  1305/2013.  The  scheme  commenced 
31 October 2016 and there have been no changes to the scheme objectives. 

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Capital investment support for producers under a 2 tier competitive application scheme, with awards 
to those applications that best meet DAERA's scheme objectives.





Tier 1 is designed to purchase equipment and machinery from a pre-defined list of eligible 
items for projects costing between GBP 5,000 to GBP 30,000. The direct grant payment is 
made  after  the  application  is  approved  and  proof  of  purchase  is  submitted  for  the  item 
from the pre-defined eligible list.
Tier 2 provides for on-farm investment projects in future proofed, fit for purpose, modern 
infrastructure  and  equipment,  in  excess  of  GBP  30,000  eligible  project  costs  (to  a 
maximum available grant of GBP 250,000) based on a robust farm business plan. Tier 2 
is  limited  to  40%  aid  intensity.  Once  the  applications  and  business  cases  are  approved 
which will show the full funding for the project has been secured, a direct grant payment 
will be made to the applicant on completion of the project to offset against the secured 
external funding.

A lump sum, in the form of a direct grant, is paid against the eligible costs claimed against as set 
out in letters of offer, on completion of the project and proof of costs are submitted.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2024 Total: GBP 7,142,773.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The scheme commenced on 31/10/2016  and is currently profiled to run to the end of 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 134 -

3.5  BAILIWICK OF GUERNSEY

3.5.1  GUERNSEY DAIRY FARM EMERGENCY FUNDING

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Guernsey Dairy Farm Emergency Funding.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The scheme provided short-term support to the dairy industry on the island in the face of financial 
pressures generated by an artificial increase in the costs of fertiliser and feed.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The scheme is provided on the authority of the States of Guernsey.

The Scheme is administered by the States of Guernsey's Farm Services team (under the Committee 
for the Environment and Infrastructure).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The Scheme is available to Dairy farmers in Guernsey who supply milk to the Island's milk processing 
facility  (Guernsey  Dairy).  Guernsey  Dairy  is  a  state-owned  entity,  owned  and  operated  by  the 
States of Guernsey with The States' Trading Supervisory Board - the States Committee mandated 
to oversee all States of Guernsey owned trading operations - having political responsibility.

Support is provided on a monthly basis. Currently, there are 12 eligible dairy farmers on the Island of 
Guernsey.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Total budget for the Fund in 2023 was GBP 243,000. This funding scheme ceased in March 2023.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

January 2023 to March 2023.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.5.2  GUERNSEY DAIRY FARM MANAGEMENT PAYMENT

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Guernsey Dairy Farm Management Payment.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 135 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The scheme aims to support sensitive land management practices and animal welfare improvements. 
As part of the Scheme, for example, the farms must:



Adhere  to  the  provisions  of  the  RSPCA  (Royal  Society  for  the  Prevention  of  Cruelty  to 
Animals, a UK charity) Assured Standard for Dairy Cows;
Comply with Farm Biodiversity Action Plans for each farm;


 Maintain  good  environmental  practice,  as  set  out  in  the  'Guernsey  Countryside 

Management Scheme';

 Maintain stocking densities below 3.05 livestock unit per verge; and


Agree a Farm Waste Management Plan.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  scheme  was  launched  in  2001  and  is  currently  under  review,  following  a  previous  review  in 
2014.  As  a  result  of  the  2014  review,  the  amount  of  funding  per  annum  was  reduced  from 
GBP 2,025,000 to GBP 1,025,000.

The scheme is provided on the authority of the States of Guernsey. The Scheme is administered by 
the  States  of  Guernsey's  Farm  Services  team  (under  the  Committee  for  the  Environment  and 
Infrastructure).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  Scheme  is  available  to  Dairy  farmers  who  supply  milk  to  the  Island's  milk  processing  facility 
(Guernsey  Dairy).  Guernsey  Dairy  is  a  state-owned  entity,  owned  and  operated  by  the  States  of 
Guernsey  with  The  States'  Trading  Supervisory  Board  having  political  responsibility.  Support  is 
provided on a monthly basis. Currently, there are 12 eligible dairy farmers.

In  return  for  a  farm  management  payment,  participants  of  the  Scheme  must  implement  a  farm 
biodiversity  action  plan  and  comply  with  other  requirements  on  good  farming  and  agricultural 
practice, environmental pollution, stocking density, farm waste management and animal welfare.

Participants must sign a contract annually to renew their commitment to the scheme.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Total annual amount for 2023 and 2024 was GBP 1,025,000 per year.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Scheme began in 2001. It is ongoing but currently under review.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.5.3  GUERNSEY DAIRY OVERDRAFT

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Guernsey Dairy Overdraft.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 136 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  purpose  of  the  policy  is  to  enable  Guernsey  Dairy  to  continue  essential  investment  in  its 
infrastructure whilst the States develops a future sustainable funding and operating model for the 
business.

The Guernsey Dairy has been operating with small trading losses for a number of years. Its reserves 
have  been  depleted  and,  as  such,  an  overdraft  was  provided  to  it  to  fund  essential  capital 
infrastructure pending the outcome of a review of future options for the strategy and future funding 
for the Dairy.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The decision to provide this deficit funding was made by Resolution of the States of Deliberation (the 
Island's  parliament)  following  its  consideration  of  the  States  of  Guernsey  Annual  Budgets 
for 2023-25.

5.

Form of the subsidy

A cash contribution.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The contribution is provided directly to Guernsey Dairy by way of a cash allocation.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

In 2023, the States of Guernsey agreed to write-off this overdraft facility of GBP 361,000.

No funding was required for the Dairy's cash requirements in 2024.

The  States  of  Guernsey  have  made  budget  provision  in  2025  for  a  further  GBP  1.3m  to  support 
Guernsey Dairy's cash requirements.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

There is no commitment to provide this subsidy beyond 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.6  BAILIWICK OF JERSEY

3.6.1  RURAL INITIATIVE SCHEME (RIS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Rural Initiative Scheme (RIS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the Rural Initiative Scheme (RIS) is to support the rural sector to become more 
economically sustainable, improve efficiency and productivity and adapt to future challenges owing 
to the historical low profitability and difficulties in raising investment capital.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 137 -

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  scheme  was  a  key  policy  under  the  Rural  Economic  Strategy  (RES)  2017  –  2021,  but  since 
May 2022 this now falls under the Economic Framework for the Rural Environment.

The legal authority for the scheme:






The Public Finances (Jersey) Law 2019;
The Agricultural (Loans and Guarantees) (Jersey) Law 1974;
The Agricultural (Loans) (Jersey) Regulations 1974;
The States of Jersey Law 2005.

Granting authority for the Scheme is the Jersey Department for the Economy.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The RIS is a core component of the Rural Support Scheme (RSS), with the scheme open to rural 
businesses  who  are  currently  enrolled  in  and  showing  sufficient  progress  through  the  RSS  (see 
separate return).

The RIS is based on an application process which details the business proposal, plan and accounts, 
with a description of why grant funding is necessary and financially beneficial. Following review and 
decisions  made  by  the  RIS  panel,  officers  formulate  'Offer  Letters'  to  successful  applicants  with 
specific conditions. Confirmation that a project can begin is upon receipt of the signed offer letter, 
which also includes an enclosed claim form that enables the beneficiary to begin forwarding receipted 
invoices. With each claim of payment, the beneficiary must also produce a project update report. 
Payments are provided by electronic bank transfer.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 740,229.50
2024 Total: GBP 580,281.99

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2017 – 2021 (under the RES).

The scheme was extended into 2022 and was rolled over to sit under 'the Economic Framework for 
the Rural Environment' which was passed by Government during the second quarter of 2022, and 
which has superseded the RES.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.6.2  RURAL SUPPORT SCHEME (RSS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Rural Support Scheme (RSS).

This return includes the previously notified Quality Milk Payments (QMP) scheme, which was rolled 
into the Rural Support Scheme (RSS) in January 2023.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 138 -

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The aim of the Rural Support Scheme (RSS) is to incentivise farms in Jersey to deliver an integrated 
and  sustainable  agricultural  production  model  based  on  world  class  consumer  and  customer 
assurance schemes, in order to increase productivity, reduce the environmental impact of farming 
and  establish  Jersey  as  the  first  jurisdiction  in  the  world  where  all  farms  are  LEAF  (Linking 
Environment and Farming) Global Standard accredited.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Rural Support Scheme exists under the Economic Framework for the Rural Environment' and is 
a  tiered  scheme  based  on  farmer's  compliance  to  externally  audited  market  focused  assurance 
schemes  which  encourage  sustainable  agriculture,  and  which  deliver  increased  agricultural 
productivity and reduced environmental impact from farming activity.

Legal authority for the Scheme:






The Public Finances (Jersey) Law 2019;
The Agricultural (Loans and Guarantees) (Jersey) Law 1974;
The Agricultural (Loans) (Jersey) Regulations 1974;
The States of Jersey Law 2005.

Granting authority for the Scheme is the Jersey Department for the Economy.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The RSS is targeted at commercial agriculturalists (defined as those commercial farming businesses 
which have a minimum annual GBP 40,000 gross margin) or smallholders (part time or small farming 
businesses which have a minimum annual GBP 5,000 gross margin).

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 3,773,862.92
2024 Total: GBP 5,422,271

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The RSS is run on an annual basis with applications commencing 1 January each year with a deadline 
of 30 September of that same year.

The scheme was extended in 2023 to include a new range of components relating to environmental 
economic  and  social  incentives.  The  Scheme  was  a  continuation  of  a  support  scheme  which  was 
introduced in 2005.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 139 -

3.7  ISLE OF MAN

3.7.1  AGRICULTURE AND FISHERIES GRANT SCHEME 2016 (AFGS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Agriculture and Fisheries Grant Scheme 2016 (AFGS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the Agriculture and Fisheries Grant Scheme (AFGS) is to aid development within 
the Isle of Man's farming, fisheries and food production sectors by supporting commercial businesses 
in making investments to improve the sustainability of businesses. This scheme aims to contribute 
towards the Department for Environment, Food and  Agriculture's (DEFA) objective of achieving a 
reliable, sustainable and self-reliant food chain.

The scheme provides support to businesses for one, or more, of the following purposes:









improve and redeploy production and reduce production costs;
to increase quality and marketability of produce;
to preserve and improve the natural environment, hygiene conditions and animal welfare 
standards;
to reduce energy use;
to promote the diversification of agricultural or fisheries activities;
to improve the skills of persons engaged in farming or fisheries on the Island.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Legal authority for the scheme is Section 3 of the Financial Provisions and Currency Act 2011.

The  budget  available  for  the  scheme  each  financial  year  is  determined  by  the  Department  of 
Environment, Food and Agriculture (DEFA).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The subsidy is paid to individual business who have applied to the Scheme. The payment is calculated 
as a percentage of approved expenditure incurred by the applicant.

The AFGS is made up of sections by sector payments:
Agricultural and Horticultural Capital Grants;
Knowledge and Innovation Grants;
Advisory Grants;
Food Business Grants;
Fisheries Grants;
Agri-Environment grants.








7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023 Total: GBP 1,922,658.17
2024 Total: GBP 2,870,885.11

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 140 -

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The AFGS was introduced in April 2016 and the Agri-Environment initiatives element was introduced 
in April 2021.

The AFGS is an ongoing measure with no end date but is subject to periodic review.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

3.7.2  AGRICULTURE DEVELOPMENT SCHEME (ADS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Agriculture Development Scheme (ADS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the Agriculture Development Scheme (ADS) is to help support a thriving, diverse 
agricultural  industry  and  maintain  its  productive  capacity,  whilst  also  protecting  the  Isle  of  Man's 
landscape and natural heritage.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Section  4  of  The  Agriculture  and  Rural  industries  Act  1914.  The  Scheme  is  operated  by  the 
Department of Environment, Food & Agriculture (DEFA).

The ADS began in  2009 as the Countryside Care Scheme (CCS) but was amended to the ADS in 
2015 following an efficacy review.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The scheme is open to agricultural businesses meeting the active farmer definition. Payments are 
split into three tranches and not provided on a per unit basis, with payments calculated using a flat 
rate payment system based on the area of eligible land managed by the applicant and paid on a pro 
rata basis.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

UK Financial Year 2023/2024 Total: GBP 4,904,674.42
UK Financial Year 2024/2025 Total: GBP 4,796,330.07

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Scheme commenced in 2009 (originally as the CCS). This scheme is ongoing and is subject to 
periodic review.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 141 -

3.7.3  PIG PREMIUM SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

The Pig Premium Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The Pig Premium Scheme provides support to the Island pig producers, to enable them to remain 
competitive.  It  balances  the  value  of  pig  production  to  the  economy  and  the  environment,  whilst 
ensuring the industry continues to focus on efficiency so that it remains as competitive as possible 
with imported products.

The Scheme retains the viability of pig producers on the Island, whilst also focussing on consumer 
confidence through strict criteria on carcase classification, primarily through the requirements to be 
externally and independently verified to Red Tractor Farm Assurance standards.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The introduction of the Pig Premium Scheme was the result of a review of all pig sector support in 
2016  which  was 
the 
Agriculture Development  Scheme  (ADS)  to  being  fully  flat  rate  based  which  meant  that  the  pig 
industry  faced  the  loss  of  the  historic  entitlement-based  payments.  This  scheme  replaced  the 
Pig Premium  Quota  Scheme  2009  and  Pig  Industry  Restructuring  Assistance  Scheme  2011  which 
were revoked.

transition  of  support  payments  under 

instigated  due 

the 

to 

The legal authority for the scheme is Section 4 of The Agriculture and Rural industries Act 1914. The 
scheme is administered by the Department of Environment, Food and Agriculture (DEFA).

The scheme specifies conditions under which premium payments may be made by the Department 
for pig carcasses meeting certain qualifying criteria.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant (premium).

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Scheme  is  open  to  both  existing  and  new  pig  rearing  units,  provided  that  they  demonstrate  that 
they fulfil the following criteria:




The pigs produced have a route to a genuine Market
The Carcase meets Classification criteria - based on its weight and its "P2 measurement" 
which is the thickness of the back fat on the carcase determined by a meat grader.
The pigs have been reared on a certified Red Tractor Assured Premises
Produce, retain and review on an annual basis the following documents:



 Herd Health Plan
 Nutrient (fertiliser) Management Plan


Evidence of Benchmarking

The Scheme provides for a maximum annual budget of GBP 120,000 along with a maximum rate of 
premium per pig of GBP 40 to individual pig producers, which may be subject to pro rata reduction 
if the budget is exceeded in any year.

4  AQUACULTURE, FISHING AND FISHERIES

4.1  UK GOVERNMENT

4.1.1  EUROPEAN MARITIME AND FISHERIES FUND (EMFF)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

2014-2020 European Maritime and Fisheries Fund (EMFF).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The 2014-2020 European Maritime and Fisheries Fund aimed to deliver EUR 243 million of EU funding 
and GBP 60 million of domestic funding to support the UK fisheries and aquaculture industries.

The objective of the EMFF was to fulfil the Union Priorities set out by the European Commission by;








Improving health and safety on fishing vessels
Improving the added value or quality of fish caught including use of unwanted catches
Investments in fishing ports, landing sites, auction halls and shelters
Processing of fishery and aquaculture products
Investments in aquaculture
Fisheries Local Action Groups (FLAG)

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The objective of the EMFF was to fulfil the Union Priorities set out by the European Commission by;







Improving health and safety on fishing vessels;
Improving the added value or quality of fish caught including use of unwanted catches;
Investments in fishing ports, landing sites, auction halls and shelters;
Processing of fishery and aquaculture products;
Investments in aquaculture.

The  power  to  deliver  the  EMFF  is  contained  in  section  98  of  the  Natural  Environment  and  Rural 
Communities Act 2006 (NERC) and the UK Covid-19 temporary framework. Articles 17 to 20 of the 
EMFF  regulation  (Regulation  (EU)  No  508/2014)  required  member  states  to  produce  an 
'Operational Programme  (OP)'  for  the  EMFF.  This  showed  how  they  intended  to  spend  their 
allocations. The UK's Operational Programme was adopted in December 2015.

The UK's Managing Authority for the EMFF is the Marine Management Organisation (MMO).

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 143 -

The scheme closed for applications in 2020. By virtue of the n+3 rule, payments can continue to be 
made until 31 December 2023.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Beneficiaries of the scheme included fishermen, fishermen's organisations, businesses involved with 
fisheries,  aquaculture,  fish  processing  sectors,  Small  and  medium  sized  enterprises  (SMEs),  fish 
producer organisations, professional organisations, fisheries local action groups and cooperatives.

Beneficiaries  are  selected  based  on  selection  criteria  agreed  between  the  UK  Government,  the 
Devolved Administrations and the MMO. There were strict provisions in place with regard to eligibility 
of  applicants.  For  example,  applicants  seeking  full  funding  for  projects,  retrospective  funding  for 
projects  already  financed  and  completed,  or  funded  from  other  schemes  were  excluded  from  the 
scheme, as were applicants convicted of serious fishing/aquaculture infringements or fraud.

There  were  different  co-financing  rates  and  matched  funding  depending  on  the  nature  of  the 
applicant's business operation, and the type of project for which funding was being sought.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

The total UK EMFF budget was EUR 243,139,437.

Of  this  GBP  7,118,611.59  was  provided  between  01  January  2022  and  31  December  2023  to 
1752 recipients.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The Scheme opened in 2015 and closed for applications in 2020.

Under the n+3 rule, recipients must pay invoices related to their grants by 31 December 2023. All 
grant payments will be made before 31 June 2024.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

4.1.2  UK SEAFOOD FUND – INFRASTRUCTURE ROUNDS 1 AND 2

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

UK Seafood Fund – Infrastructure Rounds 1 and 2.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  objective  of  the  infrastructure  scheme  is  to  strengthen  the  seafood  supply  chain  through 
investment  in  infrastructure  projects.  The  scheme  will  bring  social  and  economic  benefits,  whilst 
reducing the seafood industry's impact on the environment. The focus of the infrastructure scheme's 
first  and  second  funding  rounds  focus  on  improved  capability  in  ports,  harbours,  processing  and 
aquaculture facilities.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 144 -

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Infrastructure Scheme forms part of the wider UK Seafood Fund (UKSF). The UKSF, managed 
by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA), was created to support the 
long term future and sustainability of the UK fisheries and seafood sector.

Rounds 1 and 2 of the Infrastructure scheme commenced in 2022. Round 1 closed on 14 June 2022, 
while round 2 closed on 3 February 2023.

The  Infrastructure  scheme  is  delivered  using  section  50(1)(a)  and  Section  50(1)(b)  of  the 
United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020 (UKIM). Using the UKIM power ensures there is a single 
power to deliver all pillars of the UKSF. 

Support under the scheme is managed by Defra.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The UKSF Infrastructure scheme is a GBP 65,000,000 grant-based programme, with grants used to 
co-finance projects alongside other private financing.

The following types of organisations can apply for the grant:










public bodies (including trust ports, local authority ports and public charities);
private small to medium-sized enterprises (SMEs);
private non-SMEs;
fishermen's associations;
private trusts and foundations;
trade associations;
officially recognised producer organisations set up by fishery or aquaculture producers;
trust ports.

Applicants must be involved in the catching, processing, or aquaculture sector.

The  scheme  is  delivered  via  a  competitive  grant  process.  Applicants  seeking  funding  for  projects 
already  financed  and  completed,  or  projects  funded  from  other  schemes  are  excluded  from  this 
scheme, as well as applicants previously convicted of serious infringements or fraud.

Applicants must pass eligibility checks and a robust due diligence process. Applicants are required 
to  provide  answers  and  evidence  towards  the  scheme's  assessment  criteria,  with  the  criteria 
determining how the project can demonstrate value for money, deliverability, and its ability to meet 
the  scheme's  objectives.  The  assessment  team  comprises  of  industry  experts  with  a  minimum  of 
three evaluators assessing each bid.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  following  a  submission  by  the  supplier  of  the  breakdown  of 
expenditure claimed. Successful projects require Project Progress reports as part of the deliverables.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

In 2024 and 2025, GBP 38.349.011.46 was committed and GBP 18,354, 841.37 was paid out before 
the 31 of March 2025.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The UKSF ran from 2022-2025. 

Rounds 1 and 2 of the infrastructure scheme commenced in 2022. Round 1 closed on 14 June 2022, 
while round 2 closed on 3 February 2023. 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 145 -

The  majority  of  projects  were  paid  out  before  the  31  of  March  2025.  GBP  4.5m  of  funding  was 
transferred to the 25/26 financial year for late projects across Rounds 1, 2 and 3.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

4.1.3  UK SEAFOOD FUND – INFRASTRUCTURE ROUND 3

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

UK Seafood Fund – Infrastructure Round 3.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The  objective  of  the  infrastructure  scheme  is  to  strengthen  the  seafood  supply  chain  through 
investment  in  infrastructure  projects.  The  scheme  will  bring  social  and  economic  benefits,  whilst 
reducing the seafood industry's impact on the environment.

The aims of the pillar are;







To purchase new, more fuel-efficient engines for commercial fishing vessels, or to modify 
existing  engines.  Engines  funded  through  the  scheme  are  only  eligible  if  they  do  not 
increase the catching capacity of the vessel.
To improve commercial vessels to reduce fuel use and emissions.
To improve port infrastructure for the commercial catching sector.
To improve the health, safety and wellbeing in the catching sector.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Infrastructure Scheme forms part of the wider UK Seafood Fund (UKSF). The UKSF, managed 
by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA), was created to support the 
long term future and sustainability of the UK fisheries and seafood sector.

The  Infrastructure  scheme  is  delivered  using  section  50(1)(a)  and  Section  50(1)(b)  of  the 
United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020 (UKIM). Using the UKIM power ensures there is a single 
power to deliver all pillars of the UKSF.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

To be eligible for funding an applicant must be either:

1.

2.

3.

4.
5.

The owner of a vessel and a commercial fisher - to be verified with the MMO Coastal 
team and checking the business is registered at companies' house.
An individual or business engaged within commercial fishing, that facilitate health and 
safety equipment or training.
A  public  body  or  local  authority  in  a  local  community  that  has  a  focus  on  fishing, 
including trust ports and local authority ports, and public bodies, using funds for health 
and safety improvements.
A university or research institute.
A new entrant to the industry that could benefit from knowledge or skills in fishing.

All applicants must secure a minimum of 20% match funding – depending on the type of business 
applying – to be successful.

1.

2.

3.
4.
5.

Small scale coastal fleet (SSCF) organisations (including ports predominantly used by 
SSCF) can have up to 80% of their total project costs funded.
Public bodies (including public ports), trust ports, producer organisations, fishermen's 
associations  or  organisations,  and  cooperatives  can  have  up  to  80%  of  their  project 
costs funded.
Micro-entities can have up to 80% of their project costs funded.
Small or medium-sized enterprises can have up to 50% of their project costs funded.
All other private applicants can have up to 30% of their project costs funded.

Projects need to be completed by 31 March 2025.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

 GBP 10 million was allocated to this round of the infrastructure pillar.


In 2024 and 2025, Round 3 committed GBP 7,948,524.22. Of this, GBP 6,706,668.23 was 
paid out before the 31st of March 2025.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

Applications for Round 3 closed on the 2nd of August 2024.

The majority of projects were paid out before the 31st of March 2025. GBP 4.5m of funding was 
transferred to the 25/26 financial year for late projects across Rounds 1, 2 and 3.

4.1.4  UK SEAFOOD FUND – SKILLS AND TRAINING

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

UK Seafood Fund (UKSF): Skills and Training scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The scheme supports the current and future training needs of the fisheries and seafood sector, by 
improving training accessibility and delivery. It also aims to upskill the current workforce and attract 
new entrants to ensure the future sustainability of the industry.

The scheme has been split into two rounds to provide:





funding  for  the  creation  of  courses,  qualifications  and  specialist  training  equipment  in 
round 1; and
funding for training facilities in round 2.

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  UK  seafood  industry  has  long-running  recruitment  and  retention  issues  with  insufficient  new 
joiners. This has resulted in a reliance on foreign labour that is no longer available, and an aging 
workforce. For existing workers, a lack of relevant training and career progression present enduring 
problems.

The scheme aims to alleviate these issues through a competitive grant process that makes available 
up to GBP 10,000,000 for the industry.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 147 -

The  Skills  and  Training  Scheme  forms  part  of  the  wider  UK  Seafood  Fund  (UKSF).  The  UKSF, 
managed  by  the  Department  for  Environment,  Food  and  Rural  Affairs  (DEFRA),  was  created  to 
support the long term future and sustainability of the UK fisheries and seafood sector.

The  Skills  and  Training  scheme  is  delivered  using  section  50(1)(a)  and  Section  50(1)(f)  of  the 
United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020 (UKIM). Using the UKIM power ensures there is a single 
power to deliver all pillars of the UKSF.

Rounds  1  and  2  of  the  scheme  commenced  in  2022.  Support  under  the  scheme  is  managed  by 
DEFRA.

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  UKSF  Skills  and  Training  scheme  is  grant-based  programme,  with  grants  used  to  co-finance 
projects alongside other private financing.

The following types of organisations can apply for this grant:










public bodies (including trust ports, local authority ports and public charities);
private small to medium-sized enterprises (SMEs);
private non-SMEs;
fishermen's associations;
private trusts, foundations or social enterprises;
trade associations;
officially recognised producer organisations set up by fishery or aquaculture producers;
trust ports.

Applicants must demonstrate involvement in the catching, processing, aquaculture or recreational 
angling sector.

The scheme is delivered via a competitive grant process. Applicants must past eligibility checks and 
a  robust  due  diligence  process.  Applicants  seeking  funding  for  projects  already  financed  and 
completed,  or  projects  funded  from  other  schemes  are  excluded  from  this  scheme,  as  well  as 
applicants previously convicted of serious infringements or fraud.

Applicants must provide answers and evidence towards the scheme's assessment criteria, with the 
criteria determining how the project can demonstrate value for money, deliverability, and its ability 
to  meet  the  scheme's  objectives.  The  assessment  team  comprises  of  industry  experts  with  a 
minimum of three evaluators assessing each bid.

Payments  are  made  in  arrears  following  a  submission  by  the  supplier  of  the  breakdown  of 
expenditure claimed. Successful projects require Project Progress reports as part of the deliverables.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Total budget for the Skills and Training scheme is up to GBP 10,000,000.

Of this GBP 2,400,000 has been paid out as of the 31st of March 2025.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2022 - 2025.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

4.2  ENGLAND

4.2.1  FISHERIES AND SEAFOOD SCHEME (FASS)

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Fisheries and Seafood Scheme (FaSS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The FaSS delivers investment to safeguard the long-term sustainability, resilience and prosperity of 
the seafood sector across England. Key funding priorities include:








Creating a more sustainable and resilient sector
Boosting the demand for English seafood and accessing new markets
Improving participation through co-design and co-management
Achieving  good  environmental  status  through  the  conservation  and  restoration  of  the 
marine environment
Supporting net zero through reducing emissions within the industry

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The  scheme  was  launched  in  2021  and  delivers  on  the  Government's  manifesto  commitment  to 
maintain  funding  for  the  seafood  sector  to  safeguard  long-term  sustainability,  resilience  and 
prosperity.  The  scheme  replaces  funding  previously  provided  through  the  European  Maritime  and 
Fisheries Fund (See separate return).

The  legal  authority  for  the  scheme  is  the  Fisheries  Act  2020  (Scheme  for  Financial  Assistance) 
(England) Regulations 2021. Granting authority is the Department for Environment, Food and Rural 
Affairs (DEFRA).

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grant.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

To be eligible for this scheme you must be one of the following:








An  individual  or  business  engaged  within  commercial  or  sea  fishing,  aquaculture  or 
processing or an organisation or business engaged within recreational sea fishing;
A  public  body  or  local  authority  in  a  local  community  that  has  a  focus  on  fishing, 
aquaculture  or  processing  activities  (including  trust  ports  and  local  authority  ports,  and 
public  bodies,  using  funds  for  environmental  improvements  or  the  management  of 
fisheries);
A university or research institute;
A new entrant to the industry or unemployed individual that could benefit from knowledge 
or skills in fishing, aquaculture or processing activities.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

Total across entire scheme so far: GBP 8,546,854.34.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

The FaSS began in 2021 and will continue until the end of the UK Financial Year 2025/2026.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 149 -

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

4.3  SCOTLAND

4.3.1  MARINE FUND SCOTLAND

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Marine Fund Scotland.

This encompasses grant funding provided under the Marine Fund Scotland (MFS) 2022-23, 2023-24, 
and 2024-25 schemes.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy



The Marine Fund Scotland is one of the ways that the Scottish Government will support 
Scotland's marine sectors to help Scotland achieve the Blue Economy Vision. Most of the 
outcomes  that  the  Marine  Fund  Scotland  2023-24  aims  to  support  are  based  on  the 
outcomes in the Blue Economy Vision document.





 Marine  Fund  Scotland  is  a  competitive  fund  where  eligible  projects  are  asked  to  show 
alignment with one or more of the following outcomes to be considered for grant funding:
Established and emerging marine sectors are innovative, entrepreneurial, productive 

and internationally competitive.
Scotland  is  a  global  leader  in  healthy,  quality,  sustainably-harvested  and  farmed 
Blue Foods, for our own population and beyond.
Scotland's Blue Economy is resilient to climate change, contributing to climate change 
mitigation  and  adaptation,  with  marine  sectors  decarbonised,  resource  efficient  and 
supporting Scotland's Net Zero commitments.
Thriving, resilient, regenerated, healthy communities have more equal access to the 
benefits that ocean resources provide.
Projects funded by the Marine Fund Scotland represent value for money, are delivered 
successfully, show clearly how they have met their outcomes, and share lessons so 
that projects can develop and improve in the future.





4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

 Marine  Fund  Scotland  grant  funding  is  being  given  in  exercise  of  Scottish  Ministers' 
statutory  powers  to  give  financial  assistance  under  the  Fisheries  Act  2020  (the 
Fisheries Act).  This  financial  assistance  is  given  in  accordance  with  the  scheme  (the 
Scheme)  established  by  The  Aquaculture  and  Fisheries  etc.  (Scheme  for  Financial 
Assistance)  (Scotland)  Regulations  2022[1]  (the  2022  Regulations).  The  Scheme  which 
the  2022  Regulations  establish  provides  the  framework  for  the  Marine  Fund 
Scotland 2022-23, 2023-24, and 2024-25.
Scottish  Ministers  may  award  financial  assistance  through  the  Marine  Fund  Scotland  in 
accordance  with  the  Scheme,  for  a  specific  set  of  permitted  purposes  (the  specific 
purposes). Only projects that deliver one or more of these specific purposes are eligible to 
apply to the Marine Fund Scotland.



5.

Form of the subsidy

The  subsidy  provided  through  MFS  is  a  grant.  This  grant  is  paid  to  successful  applicants  on 
completion of their project, and in most cases requires private co-financing.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 150 -

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

Applications  must  meet  a  number  of  eligibility  criteria  in  order  to  move  to  the  next  stage  of  the 
assessment  process.  Applications  from  new  entrants  to  the  fishing  sector  are  required  to  meet 
additional eligibility criteria on top of the general eligibility criteria.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

 MFS 2023-24 had a total budget of GBP 14m:

 GBP 151,491.50 of which was paid out as grant funding during 2023, and
 GBP 11,304,033.13 of which was paid out during 2024.

 MFS 2024-25 had a total budget of GBP 14m:

 GBP 1,605,296.54 of which was paid out as grant funding during 2024,
 GBP 7,829,764.60 of which has been paid out so far in 2025, and
 GBP 3,484,940 of which still needs to be paid out (following completion of projects).
In addition to MFS 2023-24 and MFS 2024-25, there were also funds from MFS 2022-23 
paid out during 2023. The total budget for this scheme was GBP 14m:
 GBP 9,431,215.53 of which was paid out in 2023 (and none in 2024).



8.

Duration of the subsidy

Each iteration of Marine Fund Scotland runs for one financial year (i.e. April 2023 to March 2024 for 
MFS  2023/24),  during  which  applications  are  accepted  and  assessed  in  one  or  more  rounds 
(depending on uptake).

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

4.3.2  HIE FISHERIES AND AQUACULTURE SUBSIDY SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Highlands and Islands Enterprise (HIE) Fishing and Aquaculture Subsidy Scheme.

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

The objective of the scheme is to assist businesses to increase their contribution to employment and 
Gross Value Added in the economy by investing in business growth.

This scheme is aimed at supporting specific sectors of the blue economy, namely aquaculture, fish 
processing  and  small-scale  inshore  fisheries  activity.  Support  is  provided  in  pursuit  of  strategic 
priorities of producing:





Successful, productive and resilient businesses
Conditions for growth, and
Strong, capable and resourceful communities

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

Highlands and Islands Enterprise (HIE), which administers the scheme, was established to promote 
the economic and social development of the HIE area, to enhance employment and business skills, 
improve industrial efficiency, international competitiveness and the environment to achieve these.

The legal basis for the scheme is the Enterprise and New Town (Scotland) Act 1990, as amended in 
1 April 2001 by the Scottish Statutory Instrument 2001 No 126.

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 151 -

5.

Form of the subsidy

Grants, interest subsidy, loans.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

The  scheme  is  open  to  all  types  of  commercial  entities  engaged  in  catching,  processing  and/or 
marketing  of  fish  and  fish  products,  whether  sourced  through  harvesting  of  natural  products  or 
aquaculture farms. To be eligible for the scheme, applicants must be undertaking new development 
in the qualifying area (the Highlands and Islands region of Scotland).

The amount of support is calculated to provide the minimum necessary of public funding to achieve 
the  aims  of  an  agreed  business  plan.  It  is  allocated  to  capital  investment,  product  development, 
skills development strategic planning and market awareness. Support is not allocated on the basis 
of product prices, product costs or the quantities produced or exported.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023: GBP 842,343,20.
2024: GBP 168,475.27.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

01 January 2021 - 31 December 2027.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.

4.4  BAILIWICK OF JERSEY

4.4.1  MARINE SUPPORT SCHEME

1.

Title of the subsidy programme

Marine Support Scheme (MSS).

2.

Period covered by the notification

2023 and 2024.

3.

Policy objective and/or purpose of the subsidy

Funding was allocated for the marine economy in 2023 with the aim of underpinning the economic, 
environmental, and social sustainability of the sector.

It  was  recognised  that  Jersey's  marine  businesses  needed  support  if  they  were  to  survive  to 
contribute to the economy, in terms of:

 maintaining employment and providing employment opportunities in the future





providing a baseline level of food security
continuing stewardship of Jersey's territorial waters
providing local produce for consumption locally and for export
contributing to the wider health of the economy

4.

Background and authority for the subsidy

The Scheme commenced in 2023 and is now in its third year. There has been a one-off financial 
injection of GBP 1.1 million into the rural and marine economy budget in 2025 to offset the increase 
in the living wage. To this end, the scheme has expanded in 2025 to capture the type of employee 
whether  a  full-time  employee  for  3-6  month,  6-9  months  or  12  months  of  the  year  and  whether 

G/SCM/N/430/GBR

- 152 -

employees  are  on  a  work  permit  of  either  6,  9  or  12  months.  The  responsible  authority  is  the 
Department for the Economy, Government of Jersey.

5.

Form of the subsidy

The Marine Support Scheme (MSS) aims to  provide direct financial support to the marine sector. 
A grant delivery method will reward local businesses using a points based 'marine credit' system, 
which will be used to calculate support payments.

6.

To whom and how the subsidy is provided

To qualify for a support payment applicants' need to meet the following conditions:

1.
2.

3.

4.

Have a valid (Jersey issue) fishing (vessel) licence;
Be  operating  as  a  going  concern  with  a  valid  business  licence  (Regulation  of 
Undertakings) at the time of application;
Provide business accounts or a tax return showing fishing income for the two prior years 
of the scheme application;
Business 'Gross Margin' must exceed GBP 5,000 per annum.

7.

Amount of the subsidy

2023: GBP 321,073.38.
2024: GBP 438,247.20.

8.

Duration of the subsidy

2023: 26 June – 30 September.
2024: 2 July - 30 September.

9.

Statistical data permitting an assessment of the trade effects of the subsidy

No statistical data is available.
